State approaches diverge into two opposing camps: New York affirmatively protects tenants (landlord cannot refuse to rent based on cannabis use and must permit registered medical cannabis use); Illinois takes the opposite default (landlord may prohibit smoking + vaporizing + cultivation including medical via lease).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Refuse to rent | Ban smoking | Medical patient protection | Federal-benefits exception |
|--------|---------|------------------|---------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| `NewYorkCannabisLaw134` | NY Cannabis Law § 134 | **Prohibited** | Allowed (general) | **Yes — must permit medical smoking** | Yes (Section 8/HUD risk excuses restriction) |
| `IllinoisCrta` | 410 ILCS 705/ (eff. 2020-01-01) | Permitted under CRTA (FHA may apply) | Permitted | No (CRTA does not protect; FHA may) | N/A |
| `Default` | — | Permitted (subject to FHA) | Permitted | No statewide; FHA may apply | N/A |

**Three regression-critical distinguishers**:
- **NY-only refuse-to-rent prohibition**: only NY makes refusing to rent based on cannabis use itself a § 134 violation. IL + Default rely on Fair Housing Act analysis (which doesn't apply to cannabis use directly).
- **NY-only medical-patient smoking-ban exception**: only NY's § 134 affirmatively bars landlord enforcement of smoking ban against a registered medical cannabis patient (other states leave medical patients to FHA reasonable-accommodation arguments).
- **NY-only federal-benefits exception**: only NY's § 134 has a built-in carve-out — when the landlord receives federal benefits (Section 8 / HUD subsidies), the medical-patient protection yields to preserve federal compliance.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-cannabis-use-protection`. Twenty tests pin: **NY refuse-to-rent violation** + § 134 citation; **NY ban-smoking-in-lease generally permitted** + "medical cannabis exception preserved" citation; **NY ban-cultivation permitted**; **NY enforce-against-medical-patient no-federal-benefits violation** (regression-critical); **NY enforce-against-medical-patient WITH federal benefits permitted** (regression — Section 8/HUD landlord exception fires); **NY enforce against NON-medical patient permitted**; **NY evict medical patient no-federal-benefits violation**; **NY evict non-medical-user permitted**; **IL landlord may ban smoking in lease** + 410 ILCS 705/ + "CRTA" citation; **IL landlord may ban cultivation**; **IL landlord may enforce against medical patient** + "FHA reasonable-accommodation" note (regression-critical — CRTA does not protect; FHA is the path); **IL landlord may evict for cannabis use**; **default landlord may prohibit cannabis use**; **default evict for cannabis use permitted**; **state routing NY/IL/Default**; **case-insensitive routing**; **only-NY-protects-refusal-to-rent** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **only-NY-protects-medical-patients-from-smoking-ban** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **only-NY-has-federal-benefits-exception** (single-test 2-regime invariant — IL ignores federal-benefits flag); **citations pin authorities**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_clothesline_drying_right` is the **California Civil Code § 1940.20 tenant clothesline / drying-rack right module** — under what conditions may a landlord lawfully prohibit a tenant from using a clothesline or drying rack in the tenant's private area? Trader-landlord critical for CA rental owners managing multifamily properties: an outright ban (lease clause or eviction notice) violates § 1940.20 effective January 1, 2016 per AB 1448. Distinct from siblings `tenant_solar_installation` (rooftop solar panels), `ev_charger_installation` (vehicle charging), `tenant_organizing` (tenant association formation), and `flag_display_right` (federal Freedom to Display the American Flag Act).

**Two regimes** — California § 1940.20 protection vs Default no-protection:

| Regime | Authority | Definitions | Private area | Conditions |
|--------|-----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| California | Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1940.20 + 4750.10 + AB 1448 Stats. 2015 ch. 415 | Clothesline = cord/rope/wire; drying rack = apparatus; balcony/railing/awning EXCLUDED | Outdoor area OR enclosed with door access; common areas EXCLUDED | Six conditions under § 1940.20(b) |
| Default | Lease provisions + common-law quiet enjoyment | None statutory | None statutory | None |

**§ 1940.20(a)(1)+(a)(2) device-definition exclusions** — "clothesline" requires cord / rope / wire; "drying rack" requires an apparatus. Balcony railings, awnings, and other parts of structures or buildings explicitly do NOT qualify. A tenant using a balcony railing as a drying surface gets no statutory protection. Pinned by `ca_balcony_railing_does_not_qualify`, `ca_awning_does_not_qualify`, `ca_balcony_railing_in_private_area_still_does_not_qualify` (location ok, device disqualifies), `device_truth_table` (4-cell sweep: Clothesline/DryingRack qualify; BalconyRailing/Awning do not), and `ca_note_pins_definitions`.

**§ 1940.20(a)(3) private-area gate** — outdoor area OR area in tenant's premises enclosed by wall or fence with access from a door of the premises. Common areas shared with other tenants do NOT qualify. Pinned by `ca_common_area_not_private`, `ca_enclosed_private_area_protected`, `ca_clothesline_in_common_area_violates_private_area_gate` (device ok, location disqualifies), `location_truth_table` (4-cell sweep: PrivateOutdoor/EnclosedPrivate/AffixedToBuilding are private; CommonArea is not).

**§ 1940.20(b) six conditions for tenant use** — (1) no interference with property maintenance; (2) no health or safety hazard; (3) no blocking doorways or interfering with walkways or utility service equipment; (4) tenant consent if affixed to building; (5) no violation of reasonable time or location restrictions imposed by landlord; (6) effective January 1, 2016 (AB 1448 Stats. 2015 ch. 415). Pinned by `ca_property_maintenance_interference_violates`, `ca_health_safety_hazard_violates`, `ca_blocks_doorway_violates`, `ca_reasonable_restriction_violation`, and `ca_five_violation_stack_when_all_5_conditions_failed` (exactly 5 simultaneous failures).

**§ 1940.20(b)(4) building-affix consent gate** — tenant must seek landlord's consent before affixing clothesline to building. Consent requirement engages ONLY when location is AffixedToBuilding; not required for free-standing private-area installations. Pinned by `ca_affixed_to_building_requires_consent`, `ca_affixed_to_building_with_consent_protected`, and `consent_only_required_when_affixed_to_building_invariant` (4-cell sweep: only AffixedToBuilding triggers consent requirement).

**§ 4750.10 HOA companion right-to-dry** — same right protected against HOAs under Davis-Stirling Common Interest Development Act. Pinned by `ca_note_pins_hoa_companion`.

**Default fallback** — no specific tenant clothesline / drying-rack right under state statute; lease provisions controlling; common-law quiet-enjoyment review may apply. Solar-access / HOA right-to-dry laws exist in 20+ states (FL, CO, MD, ME, NV, etc.) but specifically for HOMEOWNERS, not rental tenants. Pinned by `default_no_protection`, `default_no_consent_required`, `default_citation_pins_lease_and_common_law`, and `default_note_pins_homeowner_state_laws` (FL + CO + MD + ME + NV + HOMEOWNERS).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-clothesline-drying-right`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **CA clothesline private outdoor protected**; **CA drying rack protected**; **CA balcony railing does not qualify**; **CA awning does not qualify**; **CA common area not private**; **CA enclosed private area protected**; **CA affixed to building requires consent**; **CA affixed to building with consent protected**; **CA property maintenance interference violates**; **CA health safety hazard violates**; **CA blocks doorway violates**; **CA reasonable restriction violation**; **CA citation pins authorities** (§§ 1940.20 + 4750.10 + AB 1448 Stats. 2015 ch. 415); **CA note pins definitions** (cord + outdoor area); **CA note pins six conditions** (January 1, 2016 + AB 1448); **CA note pins HOA companion** (§ 4750.10); **default no protection**; **default citation pins lease and common law**; **default note pins homeowner state laws**; **two regimes routed correctly**; **CA uniquely protects clothesline invariant**; **CA five-violation stack when all 5 conditions failed**; **device truth table** (4-cell sweep); **location truth table** (4-cell sweep); **consent only required when affixed to building invariant** (4-cell sweep); **CA balcony railing in private area still does not qualify** (location ok device disqualifies); **CA clothesline in common area violates private area gate** (device ok location disqualifies); **default no consent required**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_data_privacy` is the **state tenant data privacy / records access compliance table** — emerging area as landlord-traders adopt digital tenant screening, smart-building tech (smart locks, doorbell cameras, facial recognition / biometric entry), and tenant records databases. Three regimes:

| Regime                                            | States                  | Source                                                                |
|---------------------------------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **BiometricStrictWrittenConsent**                 | IL                      | 740 ILCS 14/ Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA, 2008) — applies to ALL entities with NO revenue threshold; written informed consent required BEFORE biometric collection; **$1,000 per negligent / $5,000 per intentional violation**; private right of action |
| **ComprehensivePrivacyLawRevenueThreshold**       | CA, VA, CO, CT, OR, DE, MD, MN | Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.100 (CCPA/CPRA); Va. Code § 59.1-575 (VCDPA); Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6-1-1301 (CPA); Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42-515 (CTDPA); Or. Consumer Privacy Act (eff. 2024-07-01); Del. PDPA (eff. 2025-01-01); Md. ODPA (eff. 2025-10-01); Minn. CDPA (eff. 2025-07-31) — revenue / consumer thresholds + 45-day DSAR response window |
| **NoStatePrivacyLaw**                             | 42 other states + DC    | No comprehensive state privacy law; common-law and contract terms govern |

**Illinois BIPA is uniquely dangerous for landlord-traders using biometric building entry** — facial recognition cameras, fingerprint scanners, retina/iris scans all trigger BIPA regardless of building size or landlord revenue. Statutory damages stack PER TENANT PER VIOLATION: a 10-unit IL building using facial recognition without proper BIPA-compliant written consent faces **$10,000 negligent or $50,000 intentional** exposure ([740 ILCS 14/ statutory text](https://www.ilga.gov/legislation/ilcs/ilcs3.asp?ActID=3004) ). Pinned by `il_biometric_use_without_consent_violates` (10 tenants × $1k = $10k) + `il_intentional_violation_5x_damages` (10 × $5k = $50k) + `il_bipa_has_no_revenue_threshold` ($100k revenue landlord still subject).

**CCPA / CPRA thresholds in CA** (any ONE triggers the law):
- **$25M+** annual gross revenue
- **100K+** CA consumers/households processed annually
- **50%+** annual revenue from selling/sharing CA personal info

Pinned by `ca_under_25m_revenue_under_100k_consumers_not_subject` (small landlord exempt) + `ca_over_25m_revenue_triggers_law` (revenue test) + `ca_100k_consumers_triggers_law` (consumer test).

**VA / CO / CT / OR / MN follow the 100k threshold pattern**: 100k+ residents processed annually OR 25k residents + revenue from data sale. Pinned by `va_100k_residents_triggers_vcdpa` + `co_under_100k_residents_not_subject`.

**DE / MD lowered the threshold to 35k residents** (newer 2025 statutes) — exposing more mid-size landlords. Pinned by `de_35k_residents_triggers_law` + `md_lower_threshold_than_ca`.

**45-day DSAR (Data Subject Access Request) response window** applies in all 8 comprehensive-law states. A tenant who requests their personal data must receive a response within 45 days or the landlord violates the state privacy law. Pinned by `ca_dsar_response_within_45_days_complies` + `ca_dsar_response_46_days_violates` (boundary).

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- IL only on BiometricStrictWrittenConsent regime (`only_il_uses_bipa_regime`)
- Exactly **8 states** on ComprehensivePrivacyLawRevenueThreshold regime (`comprehensive_privacy_law_8_states`)
- IL only with BIPA per-violation damages > 0 (`only_il_has_bipa_damages`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-privacy-check`. Twenty-six tests pin: **IL biometric use without consent violates** ($10k exposure for 10 tenants); **IL biometric with consent complies**; **IL intentional violation 5× damages** ($50k); IL no biometric no violation; **IL BIPA has no revenue threshold** (even $100k revenue landlord subject); **CA under $25M revenue + under 100k consumers NOT subject**; **CA over $25M revenue triggers**; **CA 100k consumers triggers**; **CA DSAR within 45 days complies / 46 days violates** (boundary); CA DSAR not responded violates; **VA 100k residents triggers VCDPA**; **CO under 100k residents not subject**; **DE 35k residents triggers** (lower threshold); MD lower threshold than CA; **TX no state privacy law no compliance required** (even with biometric in use); NY no comprehensive state law; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 regime-uniqueness invariants** (IL only BIPA / 8 states comprehensive / IL only damages); unknown state falls back to no law; lowercase normalizes; IL violation note mentions BIPA + dollar amount; CA subject note describes DSAR response.

`traderview-expense::tenant_domestic_violence_lease_termination` is the **tenant domestic violence (DV) early lease termination compliance module** — when a tenant or household member is a victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, or human trafficking and seeks to terminate the rental agreement early without penalty. Trader-landlord operational concern: an improperly handled DV termination request creates anti-discrimination liability (federal VAWA), state-law statutory damages, and per-incident retaliation claims. Distinct from siblings `tenant_accessible_parking` (ADA), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (screening), `rental_bed_bug_disclosure` (lease disclosure), `tenant_data_privacy` (general data handling).

**Four regimes** — Federal VAWA 2022 vs California vs Illinois Safe Homes Act vs Washington:

**Federal — Violence Against Women Act Reauthorization Act of 2022 (34 USC § 12491 + 24 CFR § 5.2005)** — applies to all HUD-covered housing programs (Section 8, public housing, LIHTC § 42, HOME, Section 202/811). Landlord SHALL NOT terminate or refuse to renew a lease solely because tenant is DV victim. Form HUD-91066 self-certification accepted as documentation; landlord MAY request within 14 business days. Lease provisions purporting to terminate on **police calls in DV situations are VOID**. Lease provisions requiring **waiver of VAWA rights are VOID**. VAWA emergency transfer plan required.

**California — Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.7** — tenant may terminate lease **14 days after written notice**. Acceptable documentation (issued within prior **180 days**): (1) restraining/protective order; (2) police report; (3) qualified third-party statement from doctor, nurse, therapist, counselor, or caseworker. Tenant liable for rent only up to 14 calendar days after notice; if landlord re-rents sooner, rent prorated. Documentation confidential — landlord may disclose only with tenant written consent OR if required by law/court order. Landlord retaliation prohibited.

**Illinois — Safe Homes Act (765 ILCS 750)** — § 750/15(a)(1) survivor may terminate if DV occurred at the leased premises; written notice required. Documentation: protective order OR qualified third-party statement (police report alone insufficient — distinct from CA). § 750/30 eviction defense — landlord cannot evict based on tenant or household member being DV victim. § 750/25 confidentiality of documentation.

**Washington — RCW 59.18.575** — termination request must be made **within 90 days** of the reported DV act, event, or circumstance giving rise to a protective order or report to qualified third party. Tenant remains liable for rent for month in which termination occurs; discharged from rent for any period following last day of month of quitting date. Documentation: protective order OR qualified third-party report.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-domestic-violence-lease-termination`. Thirty-six tests pin: **CA clean termination compliant**; **CA no written notice violation**; **CA 14-day notice boundary compliant**; **CA 13-day notice violation**; **CA 180-day documentation boundary compliant**; **CA 181-day documentation violation**; **CA police report acceptable**; **CA qualified third-party acceptable**; **CA no documentation violation**; **CA landlord disclosure without consent violation**; **CA landlord retaliation violation**; **IL clean termination compliant**; **IL no documentation violation** (765 ILCS 750/15); **IL police report NOT acceptable** (distinct from CA — IL accepts only protective order or qualified third party); **IL landlord retaliation violation** (§ 750/30); **WA clean termination compliant**; **WA within 90-day compliant** (boundary); **WA 91-day violation**; **VAWA clean termination compliant**; **VAWA lease void clauses violation** (police-call termination + VAWA-rights-waiver VOID); **VAWA landlord retaliation violation** (34 USC § 12491); **VAWA HUD Form 91066 accepted**; **VAWA not HUD-covered no federal engagement**; **citation pins CA / IL / WA / VAWA authorities** (all four); **note pins CA 180-day lookback**; **note pins IL Safe Homes Act**; **note pins WA 90-day lookback**; **note pins VAWA void clauses**; **CA strictest 180-day vs WA 90-day invariant** (150 days OK CA but violates WA); **VAWA uniquely accepts HUD-91066 invariant** (CA rejects HUD-91066 form); **VAWA uniquely engages void-clause doctrine invariant** (state regimes don't); **documentation type truth table for CA** (5-cell sweep); **multiple CA violations stack** (4 violations).

`traderview-expense::tenant_ev_charging_installation_right` is the **multi-jurisdictional tenant electric vehicle (EV) charging installation right framework module** — trader-landlord critical because EV adoption in the U.S. residential market has shifted EVCS-installation requests from novelty to routine. State "right-to-charge" laws now exist in California + Colorado + Florida + Hawaii + Illinois + Maryland + New Jersey + New York + Oregon + Virginia. Failure to grant a qualifying request in a covered jurisdiction triggers breach-of-statute liability and tenant remedies. Distinct from siblings `tenant_solar_installation`, `tenant_clothesline_drying_right`, `rental_satellite_dish_installation_right`, `rental_broadband_mte_rules`.

**Five-jurisdiction statutory framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Effective Date | Architecture |
|--------------|--------|----------------|--------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 1947.6 (AB 2565 of 2014) + § 1952.7 (Commercial) | **July 1, 2015** | APPROVAL MANDATE with 4 exemptions + tenant payment obligations + $1M insurance |
| **Colorado** | Colorado HB 23-1233 | **August 7, 2023** | PROHIBITION OF FEE for placement/use + actual-electricity-cost pass-through |
| **Maryland** | Maryland HB 830 (Chapter 582 of 2023) | **2023** | NEW-CONSTRUCTION INFRASTRUCTURE MANDATE for separate garages/carports/driveways |
| **New York** | NY Gen. Bus. Law § 399-zzz + MDL amendments | — | REASONABLENESS REVIEW (may not unreasonably withhold) |
| **Default** | Common-law lease modification | — | No right-to-charge; ADA reasonable accommodation may apply |

Pinned by `ca_post_july_2015_full_compliance_engaged`, `colorado_post_august_2023_engages`, `maryland_new_construction_with_evse_compliant`, `ny_written_request_engages_obligation`, `default_no_obligation`, `jurisdiction_truth_table_five_cells`, `ca_uniquely_provides_four_exemptions_invariant`.

**California § 1947.6 four-exemption framework** (any one defeats right-to-charge):

| Exemption | Source | Trigger |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| 10%+ EVCS exist | § 1947.6(d)(1) | EVCS at ratio ≥ 10% of designated parking spaces |
| No parking in lease | § 1947.6(d)(2) | Parking not provided as part of lease agreement |
| < 5 parking spaces | § 1947.6(d)(3) | Property has fewer than 5 parking spaces |
| Rent-controlled property | § 1947.6(d)(4) | Dwelling subject to public-entity residential rent control ordinance |

Pinned by `ca_exemption_10_percent_evcs_exist` (20 spaces × 10% = 2 EVCS), `ca_exemption_no_parking_in_lease`, `ca_exemption_under_5_parking_spaces` (4 = exempt), `ca_exemption_5_parking_spaces_no_exemption` (5 = engaged), `ca_exemption_rent_controlled`.

**California § 1947.6(c) tenant obligations**:

| # | Obligation |
|---|-----------|
| 1 | Written agreement consenting to installation/use/maintenance/removal requirements |
| 2 | Payment of ALL electrical usage as part of rent |
| 3 | Payment of ALL damage + maintenance + repair + removal + replacement |
| 4 | Payment of modifications/improvements to property |
| 5 | **§ 1947.6(c)(8) — $1,000,000 LIABILITY INSURANCE naming landlord as ADDITIONAL INSURED** |

Pinned by `ca_missing_written_agreement_violation`, `ca_missing_payment_obligations_violation`, `ca_missing_1m_insurance_violation`, `ca_landlord_refusal_when_obligated_violation` (§ 1947.6(a) SHALL APPROVE).

**Colorado HB 23-1233 fee framework**: tenant may install Level 1 OR Level 2 EVCS at own expense; landlord SHALL NOT assess fee for placement or use; landlord MAY require reimbursement for ACTUAL ELECTRICITY COST OR REASONABLE ACCESS FEE (network fee pass-through permitted). State Electrical Board EVCS requirements effective March 1, 2024; cannot prohibit EVCS unless BONA FIDE SAFETY CONCERN. Pinned by `colorado_unreasonable_fee_violation`, `colorado_post_august_2023_engages` (boundary precision), `colorado_pre_august_2023_no_engagement`.

**Maryland HB 830 — new construction / renovation EV-ready mandate**: all newly constructed OR renovated housing units with SEPARATE GARAGES, CARPORTS, OR DRIVEWAYS for each unit must include EVSE-INSTALLED OR EV-READY parking space. Pinned by `maryland_new_construction_no_evse_installed_violation`, `maryland_new_construction_with_evse_compliant`, `maryland_existing_construction_no_obligation`.

**New York Gen. Bus. Law § 399-zzz**: tenant has right to install EVCS subject to landlord's reasonable procedural approval; landlord MAY NOT unreasonably withhold approval. Pinned by `ny_written_request_engages_obligation`, `ny_unreasonable_withholding_violation`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-ev-charging-installation-right`. Thirty-eight tests pin: **CA post-July 2015 full compliance engaged**; **CA pre-July 2015 no obligation** (boundary); **CA July 2015 boundary engages**; **CA exemption 10% EVCS exist** (2 of 20 spaces); **CA exemption no parking in lease**; **CA exemption under 5 parking spaces** (4 = exempt); **CA exemption 5 parking spaces no exemption** (boundary); **CA exemption rent controlled**; **CA no written request no engagement**; **CA missing written agreement violation**; **CA missing payment obligations violation**; **CA missing $1M insurance violation**; **CA landlord refusal when obligated violation**; **Colorado post-August 2023 engages**; **Colorado pre-August 2023 no engagement**; **Colorado unreasonable fee violation**; **Maryland new construction no EVSE installed violation**; **Maryland new construction with EVSE compliant**; **Maryland existing construction no obligation**; **NY written request engages obligation**; **NY unreasonable withholding violation**; **default no obligation**; **jurisdiction truth table five cells**; **CA uniquely provides four exemptions invariant**; **citation pins all jurisdictions** (§ 1947.6 + AB 2565 + § 1952.7 + Colorado HB 23-1233 + Maryland HB 830 + Chapter 582 + NY § 399-zzz); **note pins CA approval mandate**; **note pins CA four exemptions**; **note pins CA tenant payment obligations**; **note pins CA $1M insurance requirement**; **note pins CA commercial companion**; **note pins Colorado HB 23-1233 no fee actual cost**; **note pins Colorado electrical board 2024**; **note pins Maryland HB 830 new construction**; **note pins NY § 399-zzz reasonable review**; **note pins default common law + ADA**; **note pins ten-state right-to-charge list**; **note pins cross-jurisdictional architecture**; **multiple CA failures stack**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_fire_safety_plan_disclosure` is the **tenant fire safety plan + emergency preparedness notice disclosure compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord post and distribute fire safety plans + emergency preparedness notices to tenants of multi-unit residential properties? Trader-landlord critical for any multifamily building owner: failure to post + distribute required HPD-mandated signage exposes owner to HPD violations + civil penalties + tenant breach-of-habitability claims. Distinct from siblings `detector_requirements` (smoke + CO detector hardware standards), `fire_sprinkler_disclosure` (fire-suppression system disclosure), `water_heater_earthquake_strap` (CA § 19211 seismic), and `landlord_emergency_entry_notice` (post-emergency entry notice requirements).

**Three regimes** — NYC most explicit vs CA disclosure-only vs Default IFC:

| Regime | Authority | Unit threshold | Annual mailing |
|--------|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| NYC | HMC § 27-2046 (Article 11) + HPD Required Signs | **3+ apartments** | **YES** |
| California | Cal. Health & Safety Code §§ 13145, 17926 + 2010 CO Prevention Act + 1991 Smoke Detector Act | 3+ apartments | No statutory annual mailing |
| Default | International Fire Code (IFC) § 403.10 + state habitability | 4+ units | No annual mailing |

**NYC six-element disclosure framework** — HMC § 27-2046 + HPD Required Signs Checklist requires for 3+ apartment buildings:
1. HPD Fire Safety Plan posted at building entrance
2. Fire Safety Plan mailed to tenants ANNUALLY
3. Emergency Preparedness Notice posted on INSIDE of all apartment entrance doors
4. Emergency Preparedness Notice posted in lobby or common area
5. Smoke Detecting Devices Notice posted at or near mailboxes (Class A multiple dwellings)
6. Carbon Monoxide Detector requirement notice posted in common area

Pinned by `nyc_fully_compliant_passes`, `nyc_2_units_out_of_scope`, `nyc_3_unit_boundary_in_scope` (exactly 3 = in scope), `nyc_no_fire_safety_plan_at_entrance_violates`, `nyc_no_annual_mailing_violates`, `nyc_no_apartment_door_notice_violates`, `nyc_no_lobby_notice_violates`, `nyc_no_smoke_detector_mailbox_notice_violates` (§ 27-2046), `nyc_no_co_detector_notice_violates`, `nyc_all_6_violations_stack` (exactly 6 violations on full failure), `nyc_citation_pins_authorities` (§ 27-2046 + Article 11 + HPD Required Signs Checklist), `nyc_note_pins_3_plus_apartment_threshold`, `nyc_note_pins_annual_mailing`, `nyc_note_pins_co_detector_notice`, and `nyc_uniquely_requires_annual_mailing_invariant` (NYC alone requires annual mailing).

**California disclosure-only framework** — Cal. Health & Safety Code § 13145 requires fire alarm system disclosure for multi-unit buildings; § 17926 + 2010 Carbon Monoxide Poisoning Prevention Act + 1991 Smoke Detector Act govern detector requirements; emergency evacuation plan required for buildings with specific occupancy thresholds. Disclosure satisfied if posted at building entrance OR in lobby/common area. Pinned by `ca_compliant_passes`, `ca_no_disclosure_violates` (§ 13145), `ca_partial_disclosure_at_lobby_satisfies` (lobby satisfies disjunctive requirement), `ca_citation_pins_authorities`, and `ca_2_units_out_of_scope`.

**Default IFC § 403.10 framework** — International Fire Code § 403.10 requires Fire Safety Plans in covered occupancies (typically high-rise + larger multifamily; 4+ unit threshold); state-specific habitability standards may impose additional requirements. Pinned by `default_4_units_in_scope`, `default_3_units_out_of_scope`, `default_no_violations_regardless_of_inputs`, and `default_citation_pins_ifc`.

**NYC 3-unit threshold uniquely lower than Default 4-unit invariant** — pinned by `nyc_3_unit_threshold_lower_than_default_4_invariant` (3-unit building: in scope NYC; out of scope Default).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-fire-safety-plan-disclosure`. Twenty-six tests pin: **NYC fully compliant passes**; **NYC 2 units out of scope**; **NYC 3 unit boundary in scope**; **NYC no fire safety plan at entrance violates**; **NYC no annual mailing violates**; **NYC no apartment door notice violates**; **NYC no lobby notice violates**; **NYC no smoke detector mailbox notice violates** (§ 27-2046); **NYC no CO detector notice violates**; **NYC all 6 violations stack**; **NYC citation pins authorities** (§ 27-2046 + Article 11 + HPD Required Signs); **NYC note pins 3+ apartment threshold**; **NYC note pins annual mailing**; **NYC note pins CO detector notice**; **CA compliant passes**; **CA no disclosure violates** (§ 13145); **CA partial disclosure at lobby satisfies**; **CA citation pins authorities** (§§ 13145 + 17926 + 2010 CO Act); **CA 2 units out of scope**; **default 4 units in scope**; **default 3 units out of scope** (lower NYC threshold); **default no violations regardless of inputs**; **default citation pins IFC** (§ 403.10); **three regimes routed correctly**; **NYC uniquely requires annual mailing invariant** (NYC alone); **NYC 3-unit threshold lower than default 4 invariant**.

`traderview-expense::drug_eviction` is the **federal HUD One-Strike + state drug-related eviction compliance table** — drug-related eviction sits at the intersection of federal HUD authority (public housing + Section 8) and state just-cause eviction statutes (private market). Federal floor under **42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6)** is the famous "One-Strike" rule — authorizing eviction of any tenant whose household member, guest, or person under their control engages in drug-related criminal activity, regardless of the tenant's personal knowledge or culpability ([Federal Register — 1999 One-Strike Final Rule](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/1999/07/23/99-18801/one-strike-screening-and-eviction-for-drug-abuse-and-other-criminal-activity)).

**Federal floor (42 U.S.C. § 1437d(l)(6))**:
- Any criminal activity threatening health, safety, or peaceful enjoyment by other tenants, OR
- Any drug-related criminal activity ON OR OFF the premises (public housing) / ON OR NEAR (Section 8)
- Engaged in by "covered person": tenant, household member, guest, or person under tenant's control
- Shall be cause for termination of tenancy

Originally enacted in the Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1988. **Dept. of Housing & Urban Development v. Rucker, 535 U.S. 125 (2002)** confirmed strict liability — tenant need not be aware of the household member's or guest's activity. PHAs have **wide discretion** to evict; HUD policy encourages individualized circumstances review; the 2024 "Reducing Barriers to HUD-Assisted Housing" proposed rule would further require mitigation analysis ([Federal Register 2024 Reducing Barriers proposed rule](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2024/04/10/2024-06218/reducing-barriers-to-hud-assisted-housing)).

**Two regimes for state private-market overlay**:

| Regime                                       | States       | Source                                                                |
|----------------------------------------------|--------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **StateJustCauseListsCriminalActivity**      | CA, OR, WA, NJ | Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2 (TPA 2019/AB 1482); Or. ORS Ch. 90 (SB 608 of 2019); Wash. RCW 59.18.650 (SSB 5160 of 2021); N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.1(n) (Anti-Eviction Act) |
| **ContractGovernsPrivateMarket**             | 46 other states + DC | No statewide just-cause statute enumerating criminal activity; lease provisions govern |

**Federal One-Strike strict liability under Rucker** — when household member or guest commits drug activity, the tenant CAN be evicted even if they had no knowledge of the activity. Pinned by `pha_household_member_activity_strict_liability_applies` + `pha_guest_off_premises_still_triggers_strict_liability` (off-premises activity by guest still triggers). Tenant's OWN activity is not strict-liability (they're aware): `pha_tenant_self_activity_not_strict_liability`.

**On-or-off premises scope**: federal rule explicitly covers drug activity ON OR OFF the premises. Pinned by `pha_off_premises_drug_activity_still_triggers_one_strike` (TenantOffPremises path triggers).

**HUD discretion + 2024 mitigation rule**: when PHA hasn't considered mitigating factors, module surfaces `mitigation_analysis_recommended: true`. Pinned by `pha_mitigation_recommended_when_not_considered` + `pha_mitigation_considered_no_recommendation_flag`.

**State just-cause overlay for private market**: CA, OR, WA, NJ statutes explicitly enumerate criminal activity as just cause. Private-market landlords in other 46 states + DC operate under contract — lease can authorize eviction for drug activity, and most leases do. Pinned by all 4 just-cause states individually + `tx_private_market_contract_governs` + `ny_private_market_contract_governs` (regression — eviction still authorized via lease).

**Module invariants** (1 pinned):
- Exactly 4 states (CA + OR + WA + NJ) on StateJustCauseListsCriminalActivity regime (`state_just_cause_regime_only_ca_or_wa_nj`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/drug-eviction-check`. Twenty-two tests pin: **PHA tenant drug activity authorizes eviction**; **PHA off-premises drug activity still triggers One-Strike**; **PHA household member activity triggers strict liability** under Rucker; **PHA guest off-premises strict liability** (off-premises + non-tenant covered); PHA tenant self-activity not strict liability; **PHA mitigation recommended when not considered** + considered no recommendation; **Section 8 voucher drug activity authorizes eviction**; **CA private market authorizes drug eviction** + OR + WA + NJ (4-state sweep); **TX private market contract governs** + NY contract governs (regression); **no drug activity no eviction authorized**; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **state-just-cause-regime only 4 states** invariant; unknown state falls back to contract; lowercase normalizes; **Rucker strict liability mentioned in PHA note**; citation mentions both federal (§ 1437d(l)(6) + Rucker) and state (§ 1946.2 for CA).

`traderview-expense::quiet_enjoyment` is the **state quiet enjoyment / nuisance statutory compliance table** — the covenant of quiet enjoyment is implied into every residential lease at common law in every U.S. state. Most state remedies follow the common-law set: constructive eviction, rent abatement, actual damages. **Massachusetts is the unique outlier** with G.L. c. 186 § 14 codifying treble damages + criminal exposure for intentional landlord interference. Two regimes:

| Regime                                            | States | Source                                                                |
|---------------------------------------------------|--------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **MassachusettsTrebleDamagesAndCriminal**         | MA     | Mass. G.L. c. 186 § 14 — damages = GREATER of actual or **3× monthly rent**; criminal $25-$300 fine + up to **6 months jail** for intentional breach ([Mass. G.L. c. 186 § 14 official text](https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartII/TitleI/Chapter186/Section14)) |
| **CommonLawImpliedCovenant**                      | 49 other states + DC | Common-law implied covenant; CA codified at Civ. Code § 1927; NY follows RPL § 235-b implied warranty of habitability; IL 765 ILCS 705; remedies: constructive eviction + rent abatement + actual damages |

**Massachusetts's treble damages remedy** is the load-bearing distinction: a tenant whose landlord intentionally interferes with their quiet enjoyment (lockout, utility shutoff, harassment, refusal to address persistent nuisance) recovers the GREATER of actual damages or 3× monthly rent. A tenant with $1,000 of actual damages and $2,000 monthly rent recovers $6,000 — six times their actual damages. Pinned by `ma_substantial_breach_yields_3x_rent_floor` ($1k actual vs $6k floor → $6k recovered) + `ma_actual_higher_than_3x_recovers_actual` ($10k actual vs $6k floor → $10k recovered).

**Criminal exposure layer** — MA also imposes a $25-$300 fine + possible 6-month jail term for INTENTIONAL breaches. The criminal penalty requires intent; merely negligent interference (or failure to enforce against another tenant's nuisance) does not expose the landlord to criminal liability. Pinned by `ma_intentional_breach_triggers_criminal` + `ma_non_intentional_breach_no_criminal`.

**Substantial breach threshold** — every state requires SUBSTANTIAL interference with use and enjoyment; minor inconveniences (a one-time noisy party, a brief plumbing issue) do not rise to actionable breach. Pinned by `ma_minor_breach_not_actionable` (breach alleged but not substantial → not actionable). The common-law standard requires the interference to be "substantial enough to amount to actual or constructive eviction or make the premises unsuitable for the purposes for which they were leased."

**State-specific citations** for the common-law states track their statutory codifications:
- CA Civ. Code § 1927 — statutory covenant implied in every lease (pinned by `ca_citation_mentions_1927`)
- NY RPL § 235-b — implied warranty of habitability (pinned by `ny_citation_mentions_235_b`)
- IL 765 ILCS 705 — implied covenant (pinned by `il_citation_mentions_765_ilcs`)

**Module invariants** (3 separately pinned):
- MA only on MassachusettsTrebleDamagesAndCriminal regime (`only_ma_uses_treble_damages_regime`)
- MA only with criminal exposure (`only_ma_has_criminal_exposure`)
- MA only with rent multiplier > 0 (`only_ma_has_rent_multiplier`)

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/quiet-enjoyment-check`. Twenty-one tests pin: **MA substantial breach yields 3× rent floor** ($1k actual vs $6k floor → $6k recovered); **MA actual higher than 3× recovers actual** ($10k); **MA intentional breach triggers criminal** with "possible 6-month jail" note; MA non-intentional breach no criminal exposure; **MA minor breach NOT actionable**; **CA substantial breach actual damages only** (no statutory floor); **CA + NY + IL state-specific citations pinned**; TX common-law substantial breach actual damages; **CA no criminal exposure even with intent** (regression — common-law doesn't add criminal layer); no breach alleged not actionable; **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 MA-uniqueness invariants** (regime / criminal exposure / rent multiplier); unknown state falls back to common law; lowercase normalizes; **MA note describes "GREATER of actual or 3× monthly rent"**; common-law note describes constructive eviction.

`traderview-expense::flood_disclosure` is the **state flood-zone / flood-history disclosure compliance table** — as of 2025, nine U.S. states have enacted statutory pre-lease flood disclosure for residential rental property (CA, FL, GA, IN, NJ, NY, OK, OR, TX). Oklahoma (Okla. Stat. tit. 41 § 113a, eff. 1986) was first; Georgia (O.C.G.A. § 44-7-20, eff. 1995) second; the modern wave is 2018 onward. The 41 other states + DC have no statutory pre-lease flood disclosure rule, leaving common-law fraud / negligent misrepresentation as the only tenant remedy for concealed known floods.

**Five regimes** with effective-date gates so pre-statute leases are not retroactively non-compliant:

| Regime | States | Authority | Tenant termination remedy |
|--------|--------|-----------|---------------------------|
| `FloridaFloodHistoryClaimsFemaAid` | FL | Fla. Stat. § 83.512 (SB 948, eff. 2025-10-01) | 30 days from substantial loss (50%+ market value of personal property) + refund of prepaid rent |
| `NewJerseyFemaFloodZoneAndHistory` | NJ | N.J.S.A. 46:8-50 (P.L. 2023, c. 93, eff. 2024-03-20) | Immediate without penalty + refund within 30 days of surrender if undisclosed property is in FEMA SFHA / Moderate Risk zone or had prior flood |
| `CaliforniaNaturalHazardCombined` | CA | Cal. Gov. Code § 8589.45 (eff. 2018-07-01) | Damages / common-law fraud only — no statutory immediate termination |
| `PriorFloodKnowledgeDisclosure` | TX, NY, GA, IN, OK, OR (6 states) | TX § 92.0135 / NY RPL § 231-b / GA § 44-7-20 / IN § 32-31-1-21 / OK tit. 41 § 113a / ORS 90.228 | Damages only — no statutory termination |
| `NoStateFloodDisclosure` | 41 other states + DC | (none) | Common-law fraud only |

**FL-specific lease-term gate**: FL flood disclosure only applies to leases ≥ 12 months. Month-to-month leases in FL are not subject to the statute. This is unusual in tenant-rights landscape and is pinned explicitly.

**NJ flood-zone gate**: NJ termination + refund remedy only triggers when undisclosed property is in fact in FEMA SFHA / Moderate Risk Flood Hazard Area, OR there is a prior flood known to landlord. Bare non-disclosure of a non-flood-zone property carries no termination right.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/flood-disclosure-check`. Thirty-eight tests pin: **5 regime classifications** (FL/NJ/CA + TX/NY in PriorFloodKnowledge + 4-state sweep GA/IN/OK/OR also PriorFloodKnowledge + default AL/KS/DC/WY in NoState); **FL effective date gate** (2025-09-30 pre-eff. landlord-compliant; 2025-10-01 post-eff. required); **NJ effective date gate** (2024-03-19 pre-eff. landlord-compliant; 2024-03-20 post-eff. required); **FL 12-month minimum lease-term gate** (month-to-month NOT subject; exactly 12 months IS subject); **FL substantial-loss gate** for tenant termination (no loss → no termination even on noncompliance; substantial loss → 30-day termination + $6k refund); **NJ flood-zone gate** for tenant termination (not in zone + no prior flood → no termination; in zone → immediate termination + refund; prior flood + not in zone → immediate termination triggered); **CA non-statutory remedy** (damages only despite in-zone noncompliance); **TX non-statutory remedy** (damages only); **FL citation mentions § 83.512 + SB 948 + 2025-10-01**; **NJ citation mentions 46:8-50 + P.L. 2023, c. 93 + 2024-03-20**; **CA citation mentions Gov. Code § 8589.45**; **TX citation mentions § 92.0135**; **NY citation mentions RPL § 231-b**; **OK citation mentions 1986 + "earliest"** (historical-first claim regression target); **GA citation mentions 3 floods in 5 years** (statutory threshold); **3 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (FL only FloridaFloodHistoryClaimsFemaAid; NJ only NewJerseyFemaFloodZoneAndHistory; CA only CaliforniaNaturalHazardCombined); **PriorFloodKnowledgeDisclosure has exactly 6 states**; **51-state coverage**; **FL pre-effective note explains "not yet in effect"**; **NJ termination note says "immediately"**; no-state default note describes "no statutory pre-lease flood disclosure".

`traderview-expense::owner_identification` is the **state landlord-owner identification + agent-for-service disclosure compliance table** — every state has SOME rule (statutory, regulatory, or common-law) requiring a landlord to provide tenants with the owner's name, address, and any agent authorized to receive notices and legal process. Knowing who the landlord actually is and where to serve notice is foundational for every other tenant remedy in this module set — without it the tenant cannot serve a habitability notice, repair-and-deduct demand, security-deposit return demand, or lawsuit.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | States | Trigger | Window | Citation |
|--------|--------|---------|--------|----------|
| `AffirmativePreLeaseDisclosure` | CA, FL | At lease execution / commencement | CA 15 days post-execution; FL at-commencement | CA Civ. Code § 1962 / FL Stat. § 83.50 |
| `DisclosureUponWrittenDemand` | TX | Tenant's written demand OR continuous posting OR in-lease | 7 days from demand | TX Prop. Code § 92.201 / § 92.202 |
| `MultipleDwellingRegistration` | NY, NJ, MA | Building has ≥ 3 dwelling units | At registration period | NY MDL § 325 + NYC HMC § 27-2098 / NJ N.J.S.A. 55:13A / MA G.L. c. 111 § 197A + c. 186 § 15B(7) |
| `LocalLawOrCommonLawOnly` | 44 other states + DC | Local ordinance or common-law fraud | (varies) | (none statewide) |

**Tenant remedies on noncompliance**:
- **CA** (Civ. Code § 1962(c)): tenant may serve at rent-payment address as if it were the landlord's address.
- **TX** (Prop. Code § 92.202): one month's rent plus $100 plus attorney's fees; termination right after second 7-day failure following a written demand.
- **NY / NJ / MA**: registration noncompliance bars landlord from non-payment summary proceedings (NYC HMC § 27-2107) or rent collection until cured.
- **FL** (§ 83.50): no specific statutory damages — but disclosure is a precondition to other remedies.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/owner-identification-check`. Thirty-nine tests pin: **4 regime classifications** (CA/FL AffirmativePreLease + TX DisclosureOnDemand + NY/NJ/MA MultipleDwelling + default AL/KS/DC/WY/PA/IL LocalLawOrCommonLaw); **CA 15-day window boundary** (day 15 still within window → treated compliant; **day 16 post-execution without disclosure → non-compliant** + statutory damages available); CA post-window disclosure compliant; **FL at-commencement no-disclosure → immediately non-compliant** (zero-day window); FL with disclosure compliant; **FL has NO § 83.50 statutory damages path** (regression target — distinguishing FL from CA/TX); **TX no-demand + no-posting → compliant** (dormant duty); **TX 7-day demand window** (day 7 with disclosure compliant; day 8 without disclosure non-compliant); **TX continuous posting alternative satisfies** even without 7-day response; **TX in-lease alternative satisfies**; **TX second-failure triggers § 92.202(b) termination right** (regression target — first failure does NOT yet trigger); **NY 2-unit below 3-unit registration threshold → not required** (boundary); **NY 3-unit at threshold → required**; **NJ 4-unit registered → compliant**; **MA 3-unit unregistered → non-compliant**; **MA c. 186 § 15B(7) direct disclosure independent of c. 111 § 197A registration** (regression — separate compliance path); default-state no-statewide-rule → treated compliant; **6 citation regression targets** (CA mentions § 1962 + "15 days"; FL mentions § 83.50; TX mentions § 92.201 + § 92.202 + "7 days"; NY mentions MDL § 325 + 27-2098; NJ mentions 55:13A; MA mentions c. 111 § 197A + c. 186 § 15B(7)); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **3 single-regime-count invariants** (AffirmativePreLease exactly 2 states; DisclosureOnDemand exactly 1; MultipleDwelling exactly 3); CA noncompliance note mentions regime; TX compliance note mentions regime; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::tenant_death_termination` is the **state tenant-death lease-termination compliance table** — when a sole-occupant residential tenant dies before lease expiration, four distinct statutory regimes govern termination and property disposition; the default common-law rule (in states without a death-termination statute) is that the lease is a contract that survives death and the estate remains liable for remaining rent through the end of the term.

**Five regimes**:

| Regime | States | Trigger | Key window |
|--------|--------|---------|------------|
| `EstateRepresentativeTerminatesWith30DayNotice` | TX | Sole-occupant + written notice + property removal + signed inventory (if required) | 30 days from notice or all-conditions-met (whichever later) |
| `MonthToMonthAutoTerminationOnLastRent` | CA | Month-to-month tenancy only | 30 days after last rent payment by deceased; no notice from estate needed |
| `LeaseAutoTerminatesOnDateOfDeath` | VA | Sole-occupant residing in unit | Auto-terminated AS OF DATE OF DEATH; 10-day property-disposition notice to authorized contact |
| `MultiNoticeStorageRegime` | WA | Landlord mails first notice to representative / emergency contact / successor | 45 days after second notice (with storage) before disposal |
| `NoSpecificStatuteCommonLawContract` | 46 other states + DC | None (contract survives) | Estate liable through end of term subject to landlord's mitigation duty |

**TX-specific gate sequencing**: § 92.0162 requires THREE simultaneous conditions — written notice, property removal, AND signed inventory (only if landlord requires one). The 30-day clock starts at notice but termination only takes effect when ALL conditions are satisfied. Sole occupancy is a categorical prerequisite.

**CA-specific scope limit**: § 1934 applies only to month-to-month tenancies. Fixed-term leases in CA fall through to common-law contract continuation — the estate remains liable for remaining rent.

**VA timing asymmetry**: lease termination is instant on date of death, but landlord cannot dispose of personal property until 10 days after written notice to the authorized contact person.

**WA storage cascade**: lease termination by first-notice mailing alone, but property disposition requires both the second notice AND a 45-day storage hold.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-death-termination-check`. Thirty-seven tests pin: **5 regime classifications** (TX/CA/VA/WA each separately + default AL/FL/NY/PA/DC/WY common-law); **TX all-conditions met + 30 days → terminates**; **TX 29-day boundary not yet terminated** (strict ≥ 30); TX no-property-removal → not terminated + conditions incomplete; **TX inventory-required-but-unsigned → conditions incomplete** (regression target — the inventory step is independent of property-removal step); TX inventory-required-and-signed → terminates; **TX not-sole-occupant → never terminates** (§ 92.0162 sole-occupant prerequisite); **CA 30-day boundary for month-to-month** (29 days no; 30 days yes); **CA fixed-term lease falls through to common-law** (regression target — § 1934 month-to-month-only scope limit); **VA terminates immediately on date of death**; **VA dispose-only-after-10-day-notice** (day 9 lease terminated but no disposal; day 10 disposal allowed); VA not-sole-occupant → no auto-termination; **WA first notice alone terminates lease**; no first notice → no termination; **WA dispose requires second notice + 45-day storage hold** (day 44 not yet; day 45 allowed); WA dispose without second notice → never allowed even at day 100; default state lease continues; **4 citation regression targets** (TX § 92.0162 + "30th day"; CA § 1934 + "30 days"; VA § 55.1-1256 + "10-day"; WA 59.18.595 + "45 days"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (TX-only / CA-only / VA-only / WA-only regimes); terminated note describes regime; not-terminated note describes regime; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::late_payment_grace_period` is the **state tenant late-payment grace-period compliance table** — distinct from `late_fee_caps` (which caps the dollar amount of a late fee). This module captures the **window before any late fee can attach at all**. A landlord trying to charge a late fee one day after rent is due in Massachusetts is non-compliant regardless of whether the fee amount itself is "reasonable."

**Six regimes** with sharp day-count boundaries:

| Regime | States | Statutory grace | Authority |
|--------|--------|-----------------|-----------|
| `MassachusettsLongGracePeriod` | MA | **30 full days** (most generous in U.S.) | G.L. c. 186 § 15B(1)(c) |
| `ConnecticutNineDayGracePeriod` | CT | 9 days | Conn. Gen. Stat. § 47a-15a |
| `StandardFiveDayGracePeriod` | NY, NC, WA, VA | 5 days | NY RPL § 238-a (HSTPA 2019) / NC G.S. § 42-46 / WA RCW 59.18.170 / VA Code § 55.1-1204 |
| `OregonFourDayGracePeriod` | OR | 4 days | ORS 90.260 |
| `TexasShortGracePeriod` | TX | 2 days + written-lease disclosure required | Tex. Prop. Code § 92.019 |
| `NoStatutoryGracePeriodReasonablenessOnly` | 44 other states + DC | None (lease-contract reasonableness only) | (general contract / unconscionability) |

**Two regimes require written-lease disclosure** as a precondition to enforceability: NY (RPL § 238-a) and TX (Prop. Code § 92.019) and VA (§ 55.1-1204) and OR (ORS 90.260). Failure to include the late-fee terms in the written lease invalidates the fee even when the grace period has been satisfied.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/late-payment-grace-period-check`. Thirty-nine tests pin: **6 regime classifications** (MA / CT / NY+NC+WA+VA Standard / OR / TX / default AL/FL/CA/IL/GA/DC/NJ/WY no-statute); **MA 30-day boundary** (day 30 not yet; day 31 satisfies); MA short-lateness non-compliant; **NY 5-day boundary** (day 5 not yet; day 6 satisfies); **NY written-lease disclosure required** (compliant grace + no lease disclosure → non-compliant); **TX 2-day boundary** (day 2 not yet; day 3 satisfies); **TX written-lease disclosure required**; **OR 4-day boundary** (day 4 not yet; day 5 satisfies); **CT 9-day boundary** (day 9 not yet; day 10 satisfies); **default state any-day satisfies** (no statutory minimum); CA no-statute landlord compliant immediately; **no-fee-charged path → compliance dormant** (regression — module only flags when a fee is actually charged); **6 citation regression targets** (MA mentions c. 186 § 15B + "30 full days"; NY mentions § 238-a + "HSTPA 2019"; TX mentions § 92.019 + "2 full days"; NC mentions § 42-46 + "5-day grace"; OR mentions 90.260 + "fourth day"; CT mentions § 47a-15a + "nine-day"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **5 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (MA-only / CT-only / TX-only / OR-only / StandardFiveDay exactly 4 states); note non-compliant describes grace failure; note compliant describes compliance; **note distinguishes grace-period failure from lease-disclosure failure** (NY: grace met but no lease disclosure → "NOT disclosed in written lease" diagnostic); lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::owner_move_in_eviction` is the **state owner-move-in (OMI) / no-fault eviction restriction compliance table** — OMI is the most-litigated no-fault eviction ground nationally because of the obvious risk of pretextual abuse ("I'll move in" → tenant vacates → owner re-rents at market). Recent legislative waves significantly tightened the requirements: CA SB 567 (eff. 2024-04-01) and OR SB 608 (eff. 2019-02-28).

**Five regimes** with distinct compliance mechanisms:

| Regime | States | Move-in deadline | Residency requirement | Bad-faith remedy |
|--------|--------|------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| `CaliforniaSb567Strict` | CA | 90 days | 12 continuous months | § 1946.2(g) civil penalty + actual damages |
| `OregonSb608Combined` | OR | 90-day notice | (combined with relocation) | Actual damages |
| `NewJerseyTripleDamagesGoodFaith` | NJ | (none) | 6 months | **3× tenant damages + attorney fees** under § 2A:18-61.6 |
| `NewYorkRentStabilizedOnlyOneUnit` | NY (rent-stabilized only) | (none) | 3 years principal residence | 3-year rent-increase lockout on other building units |
| `NoStateOwnerMoveInRestriction` | 46 other states + DC | None | None | General landlord-tenant act applies |

**CA SB 567 four-prong test** — all required for compliance:
1. Occupant qualifies (owner OR 6 enumerated family relations: spouse, domestic partner, parent, child, grandchild, grandparent)
2. Written notice with statutory period provided
3. Owner moves in within 90 days of tenant vacating
4. Owner resides as primary residence ≥ 12 continuous months + relocation assistance paid

**NJ ≤ 3-unit gate**: § 2A:18-61.1(l)(3) restricts OMI to buildings of 3 residential units or fewer. A 4-unit building owner cannot use the OMI ground at all under New Jersey law.

**NJ 6-month / 3× damages mechanism**: if the owner gives OMI notice and "arbitrarily fails to personally occupy the premises for at least 6 months," § 2A:18-61.6 exposes the owner to **three times** the tenant's damages plus attorney fees and costs.

**NY rent-stabilized scope limit**: NYC RSC § 2524.4 applies only to rent-stabilized units. Non-rent-stabilized NY units fall to the no-state-restriction default.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/owner-move-in-eviction-check`. Thirty-six tests pin: **5 regime classifications** (CA / OR / NJ / NY + default AL/FL/TX/WA/DC/WY); **CA all 4 requirements met → compliant**; **CA 90-day boundary** (day 90 compliant; day 91 non-compliant); **CA 12-month residency boundary** (11 months non-compliant); **CA no-relocation-assistance non-compliant**; **CA non-qualifying-occupant OMI not permitted**; **OR notice + relocation compliant**; OR no-notice non-compliant; OR no-relocation non-compliant; **NJ 3-unit building OMI permitted**; **NJ 4-unit building OMI NOT permitted** (regression — strict ≤ 3-unit gate); **NJ 6-month residency satisfied compliant**; **NJ 5-month residency triggers 3× damages** ($10k actual → $30k award); **NJ $100M actual damages × 3 = $300M no precision loss**; **NY non-rent-stabilized falls through to no restriction**; **NY rent-stabilized 36-month residency compliant**; **NY rent-stabilized 35-month residency non-compliant**; default state allows OMI; **4 citation regression targets** (CA SB 567 + "90 days" + "12 continuous months"; OR SB 608 + "90-day notice"; NJ 2A:18-61.1(l)(3) + 2A:18-61.6 + "3× tenant damages"; NY § 2524.4 + "3-year"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (CA-only / OR-only / NJ-only / NY-only); CA non-compliant note mentions regime; NJ remedy note mentions damages award ($15000 for $5k actual × 3); lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::lease_copy_delivery` is the **state landlord duty to provide tenant a signed copy of the lease** — distinct from `owner_identification` (which is about disclosing the owner's identity and agent for service). Knowing what's in the lease is foundational; a tenant who has never received a copy cannot enforce the terms they signed up for and cannot easily defend against eviction proceedings based on lease provisions.

**Four regimes** with sharp day-count boundaries:

| Regime | States | Deadline | Penalty | Waiver |
|--------|--------|----------|---------|--------|
| `California15DayDelivery` | CA | 15 calendar days post-execution | (none) | Allowed |
| `Massachusetts30DayWith300DollarFine` | MA | 30 calendar days post-execution | **$300 fine** | **VOID and unenforceable** |
| `Texas3BusinessDayDelivery` | TX | **3 business days** post-all-parties-signing | (none) | Allowed |
| `NoStateLeaseCopyDeliveryDeadline` | 47 other states + DC | None | (none) | Allowed (common-law only) |

**MA is the strictest regime**: G.L. c. 186 § 15D imposes both a $300 statutory fine for missing the 30-day deadline AND voids any contractual attempt to waive the duty. CA gives a longer window (15 days) but no fixed-dollar fine. TX gives the shortest window (3 business days) but no fine.

**TX measures in business days**, not calendar days — the only regime in this table that does. A lease signed Friday evening must be delivered by Wednesday in normal weeks (Mon/Tue/Wed = 3 business days).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-copy-delivery-check`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **4 regime classifications** (CA / MA / TX + default AL/FL/NY/WA/DC/WY/NJ/IL); **CA 15-day boundary** (day 15 still within window; day 16 non-compliant); CA delivered within window compliant; CA delivered late still compliant per facts (statute imposes no per-day penalty); **MA 30-day boundary** (day 30 still within window; day 31 non-compliant + **$300 statutory fine available**); **MA contractual waiver is statutorily VOID** (regression target — only MA prohibits waiver); MA waiver does NOT excuse noncompliance; **TX 3-business-day boundary** (day 3 still within; day 4 non-compliant); **TX no statutory fine dollar amount** (regression — distinguishes TX from MA); **default state always compliant** (no deadline) even at 365 days non-delivery; default-state waiver not void; **3 citation regression targets** (CA mentions § 1962(a)(1) + "15 days"; MA mentions c. 186 § 15D + "$300" + "void and unenforceable"; TX mentions § 92.024 + "3 business days"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (CA-only 15-day / MA-only 30-day-fine / TX-only business-day / MA-only waiver-prohibited); MA non-compliant note mentions $300 fine; CA compliant note mentions regime; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::tenant_noise_nuisance_enforcement` is the **multi-jurisdictional tenant noise nuisance enforcement framework module** — trader-landlord critical because (1) noise complaints are among the most common multifamily tenant grievances; (2) failure to address noise nuisance breaches the warranty of QUIET ENJOYMENT and exposes landlord to rent abatement, constructive eviction claims, lease termination, and damages; (3) NYC + Chicago + Boston impose absolute decibel and quiet-hour standards regardless of tenant tolerance; (4) landlord has DUTY TO ABATE known noise nuisances created by other tenants. Companion to `landlord_self_help_eviction_prohibition`, `tenant_rent_escrow_withholding`, `landlord_emergency_entry_notice`, `rental_window_guard_installation`.

**Five-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Standard |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 1941.4 + Andrews v. Mobile Aire Estates, 125 Cal. App. 4th 578 (2005) | IMPLIED COVENANT of quiet enjoyment |
| **NYC** | NYC Admin. Code § 24-218 + NYC Noise Code | Nighttime 10pm-7am: 7 dB(A) above ambient; daytime: 10 dB(A); apartment-to-apartment amplified-audible = ANY HOUR violation |
| **Chicago** | Chicago Municipal Code § 8-32 | Quiet hours 10pm-8am weekdays / 10pm-10am weekends; 75-feet-audible presumptive violation; amplified music 9pm-8am prohibited |
| **Massachusetts** | Mass. G.L. c. 186 § 14 + Berman & Sons v. Jefferson, 379 Mass. 196 (1979) | Willful interference CRIMINAL + TREBLE DAMAGES + attorney fees |
| **Default** | Common-law nuisance + implied covenant | Local quiet-hour ordinances vary (LA / SF / Seattle / Portland / Denver 10pm-7am) |

Pinned by `nyc_10_db_above_ambient_nighttime_violation`, `nyc_5_db_above_ambient_nighttime_no_violation`, `nyc_amplified_adjacent_unit_any_hour_violation` (apartment-to-apartment any hour), `nyc_daytime_10_db_threshold`, `nyc_daytime_9_db_no_violation`, `nyc_quiet_hours_7am_boundary` (7am = not quiet), `nyc_quiet_hours_6am_engages`, `chicago_plainly_audible_75_feet_violation`, `chicago_weekday_quiet_hours_10pm_8am`, `chicago_weekend_quiet_hours_10pm_10am`, `chicago_amplified_music_9pm_8am_violation`, `california_implied_covenant_engagement`, `massachusetts_treble_damages_after_30_days`, `default_jurisdiction_common_law_nuisance`, `jurisdiction_truth_table_five_cells`, `chicago_uniquely_75_foot_audible_invariant`.

**NYC § 24-218 three-tier framework**:

| Tier | Source | Threshold |
|------|--------|-----------|
| Nighttime (10pm-7am) | § 24-218 | 7 dB(A) above ambient |
| Daytime (7am-10pm) | § 24-218 | 10 dB(A) above ambient |
| Apartment-to-apartment AMPLIFIED | § 24-218 | Audible inside adjacent dwelling with windows closed = violation ANY HOUR |

**Chicago § 8-32 dual quiet hours**: weekdays 10pm-8am, weekends 10pm-10am; plus 75-foot-audible presumptive violation + 9pm-8am amplified-music prohibition.

**Landlord duty to abate** — landlord has DUTY to investigate noise complaints + take reasonable abatement action against offending tenant (lease violation notice + cure-or-quit + eviction action); failure exposes landlord to (1) tenant rent abatement; (2) warranty of habitability claim; (3) lease termination; (4) constructive eviction; (5) damages action. Pinned by `landlord_failed_to_abate_complaint_liability_engaged`, `landlord_abated_no_liability`, `no_complaint_no_landlord_liability`, `tenant_remedy_available_after_14_days_without_action`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-noise-nuisance-enforcement`. Thirty-three tests pin: **NYC 10 dB above ambient nighttime violation**; **NYC 5 dB above ambient nighttime no violation**; **NYC amplified adjacent-unit any-hour violation**; **NYC daytime 10 dB threshold**; **NYC daytime 9 dB no violation**; **NYC quiet hours 7am boundary** (not quiet); **NYC quiet hours 6am engages**; **Chicago plainly audible 75 feet violation**; **Chicago weekday quiet hours 10pm-8am**; **Chicago weekend quiet hours 10pm-10am**; **Chicago amplified music 9pm-8am violation**; **California implied covenant engagement** (Andrews v. Mobile Aire Estates); **Massachusetts treble damages after 30 days** (Berman & Sons v. Jefferson); **default jurisdiction common-law nuisance**; **landlord failed to abate complaint liability engaged**; **landlord abated no liability**; **no complaint no landlord liability**; **tenant remedy available after 14 days without action**; **jurisdiction truth table five cells**; **Chicago uniquely 75-foot audible invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (§ 1941.4 + Andrews + § 24-218 + NYC Noise Code + § 235-b + Park West Management + § 8-32 + c. 186 § 14 + Berman & Sons); **note pins CA Andrews implied covenant**; **note pins NYC general dB ambient thresholds**; **note pins NYC apartment-to-apartment amplified**; **note pins NYC DEP/311 enforcement**; **note pins Chicago dual quiet hours schedule**; **note pins Chicago enforcement** (CPD + DOB); **note pins MA c. 186 § 14 criminal treble**; **note pins default common-law nuisance**; **note pins landlord duty to abate** (cure-or-quit + constructive eviction); **note pins trader-landlord most common grievances**; **note pins cross-jurisdictional architecture**; **multiple failures stack landlord failed to abate**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_organizing` is the **state tenant right-to-organize / tenant association protection compliance table** — distinct from `retaliation_windows` (generic post-protected-act timing) and from `senior_disabled_protection`. Tenant unions and associations are organized groups of building residents who collectively negotiate rent, habitability, services, eviction defense, and policy. Only 4 jurisdictions provide statewide statutory protection for tenant organizing activity, with sharply different remedies.

**Five regimes** with distinct compliance mechanisms:

| Regime | States | Mechanism | Maximum Penalty |
|--------|--------|-----------|------------------|
| `NewYorkAffirmativeRoomAccess` | NY | Affirmative duty to provide common-room meeting space at NO COST | Damages (no fixed civil penalty) |
| `DistrictColumbiaStrongCivilPenalty` | DC | Anti-interference + meetings in unit/common areas | **$10,000 per violation** + business license suspension/revocation + attorney fees |
| `NewJerseyOrganizerProtection` | NJ | Reprisal-against-organizer protection | Damages + injunctive (no fixed civil penalty) |
| `CaliforniaRetaliatoryEvictionDefense` | CA | 180-day protected window from organizing act | Affirmative defense to retaliatory eviction |
| `NoStatewideTenantOrganizingProtection` | 46 other states | None (general anti-retaliation may apply) | (none) |

**NY uniquely imposes an affirmative obligation**: RPL § 230 requires the landlord to permit tenant association meetings in any community or social room in the building **at no cost**, even if the room is normally fee-based. Refusing access or charging a fee is a standalone statutory violation independent of any retaliation.

**DC is the strongest penalty regime**: § 42-3505.06 carries a **$10,000 per-violation civil penalty** (inflation-adjusted annually) plus business-license suspension and attorney fees. No other jurisdiction in this table imposes a per-violation dollar penalty.

**CA's 180-day window**: Civ. Code § 1942.5(d) creates a 180-day protected period from the tenant's organizing act. Adverse landlord action (rent increase, decreased services, possession action) within this window is presumed retaliatory; outside it the tenant must prove retaliatory motive independently.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-organizing-check`. Thirty-two tests pin: **5 regime classifications** (NY / DC / NJ / CA + default AL/FL/TX/WA/WY/MA — note **MA is in the default regime** as it has no specific tenant-organizing statute despite strong general tenant protections); **NY refused common-room access → violation**; **NY charged fee for meeting room → violation** (RPL § 230 requires no-cost access); NY room access granted no violation; **DC adverse action triggers $10,000 civil penalty**; **DC business license remedy available on violation**; DC no violation → no license remedy; **CA day 90 within 180-day window → retaliation presumed**; **CA day 180 boundary still within window** (regression — strict ≤ 180); **CA day 181 outside window → no presumption** + landlord compliant; **NJ adverse action against organizer non-compliant**; **NJ no civil-penalty dollar amount** (regression — distinguishes NJ from DC); no organizing activity → statute dormant; default state TX no protection; **4 citation regression targets** (NY RPL § 230 + "NO COST"; DC § 42-3505.06 + "$10,000" + "business licenses"; NJ 2A:42-10.10 + "reprisal"; CA § 1942.5(d) + "180-day"); **51-state coverage**; non-empty citations; **4 single-state-uniqueness invariants** (NY-only / DC-only / NJ-only / CA-only); DC violation note mentions business license; NY violation note mentions RPL § 230; CA within-window note mentions 180-day; lowercase state code normalizes.

`traderview-expense::tenant_positive_rent_reporting` is the **multi-jurisdictional tenant positive rent reporting (credit-bureau) requirement framework module** — California AB 2747 of 2024 (codified at Cal. Civ. Code § 1954.07, **effective April 1, 2025**) mandates that landlords of 16+ unit residential buildings OFFER tenants the option to have their POSITIVE rental payments reported to at least one nationwide consumer reporting agency (Experian, Equifax, TransUnion). Companion to `landlord_annual_rent_statement`, `tenant_data_privacy`, `rental_application_denial_disclosure`, `fair_chance_housing`.

Trader-landlord critical because (1) AB 2747 imposes affirmative offer obligation with annual repetition; (2) $10/month fee cap is strictly enforced; (3) failure to offer creates per-violation civil exposure; (4) misreporting positive vs late payments creates FCRA exposure (15 USC § 1681 et seq.); (5) tenant non-payment of fee CANNOT be cause for termination of tenancy AND CANNOT be deducted from security deposit.

**Four-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Effective Date | Trigger |
|--------------|--------|----------------|---------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 1954.07 (AB 2747 of 2024) | **April 1, 2025** | 16+ unit buildings (or 15-or-fewer with corporate-multiple-building carveout) |
| **Colorado** | Colorado HB 23-1099 | 2023 | Section 8 voucher holders + tenant-elected |
| **Washington** | Washington SB 5495 of 2024 | 2024 | Buildings of 5+ units |
| **Default** | No statewide framework | — | FCRA (15 USC § 1681) furnisher duties only |

Pinned by `ca_16_plus_units_post_april_2025_obligation_engaged`, `ca_pre_april_2025_no_obligation`, `ca_april_2025_boundary_engages`, `colorado_engages_2023_and_later`, `washington_5_unit_threshold`, `jurisdiction_truth_table_four_cells`, `ca_carveout_only_engages_in_california_invariant`.

**California § 1954.07 building-size threshold + carveout**:

| Condition | Result |
|-----------|--------|
| ≥ 16 units | OBLIGATION TRIGGERED |
| 15 or fewer + individual landlord | EXEMPT |
| 15 or fewer + REIT/corp/LLC w/corp member + multiple buildings | **CARVEOUT ENGAGES — obligation triggered** |
| 15 or fewer + corp + single building | EXEMPT |

Pinned by `ca_15_or_fewer_units_individual_no_obligation`, `ca_15_units_corporate_multiple_buildings_carveout_engages`, `ca_15_units_reit_carveout_engages`, `ca_15_units_corporate_single_building_no_carveout`.

**California § 1954.07 offer timing — dual track**:

| Lease Type | Offer Required |
|------------|----------------|
| Leases entered into ON OR AFTER April 1, 2025 | At time of lease + at least once annually |
| Leases OUTSTANDING as of January 1, 2025 | No later than April 1, 2025 + at least once annually |

Pinned by `ca_16_units_no_offer_violation`, `ca_no_annual_offer_violation`.

**California § 1954.07 $10/month fee cap — LESSER of actual cost OR $10**: pinned by `fee_at_10_dollar_cap_compliant`, `fee_at_actual_cost_below_10_compliant`, `fee_above_10_dollar_cap_violation`, `fee_above_actual_cost_violation_even_below_10`.

**Positive-only definition** — "positive rental payment information" means COMPLETE, TIMELY payments of rent; specifically EXCLUDES incomplete or late payments. Pinned by `negative_reporting_violation`.

**California § 1954.07 tenant fee non-payment three-prong protection**:

1. Failure to pay fee SHALL NOT BE CAUSE FOR TERMINATION OF TENANCY
2. Landlord SHALL NOT DEDUCT unpaid fee from security deposit
3. 30+ days unpaid: landlord may stop reporting + tenant blocked from re-electing for 6 MONTHS

Pinned by `termination_for_fee_nonpayment_violation`, `deducting_fee_from_security_deposit_violation`, `fee_unpaid_30_days_stop_reporting_engages`, `fee_unpaid_29_days_no_stop_reporting` (1-day boundary precision).

**FCRA furnisher duties (15 USC § 1681s-2)** — landlord furnishing positive rental payment data is a "FURNISHER" subject to accuracy and dispute-investigation requirements; failure to investigate disputed inaccurate report creates private right of action under § 1681s-2(b).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-positive-rent-reporting`. Thirty-six tests pin: **CA 16+ units post-April 2025 obligation engaged**; **CA pre-April 2025 no obligation** (boundary precision); **CA April 2025 boundary engages**; **CA 15-or-fewer-units individual no obligation**; **CA 15 units corporate multiple buildings carveout engages**; **CA 15 units REIT carveout engages**; **CA 15 units corporate single building no carveout**; **CA 16 units no offer violation**; **CA no annual offer violation**; **fee above $10 cap violation**; **fee at $10 cap compliant**; **fee at actual cost below $10 compliant**; **fee above actual cost violation even below $10**; **negative reporting violation**; **termination for fee non-payment violation**; **deducting fee from security deposit violation**; **fee unpaid 30 days stop reporting engages**; **fee unpaid 29 days no stop reporting** (1-day boundary); **Colorado engages 2023 and later**; **Washington 5-unit threshold**; **default jurisdiction no obligation**; **jurisdiction truth table four cells**; **CA carveout only engages in California invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (§ 1954.07 + AB 2747 of 2024 + April 1, 2025 + Colorado HB 23-1099 + Washington SB 5495 of 2024 + HUD Tenant Credit Reporting Pilot + 15 USC § 1681 et seq. + § 1681s-2); **note pins CA 16-unit threshold**; **note pins CA 15-unit carveout**; **note pins CA offer timing dual track**; **note pins CA $10 fee cap**; **note pins CA positive-only definition**; **note pins CA tenant protections three-prong**; **note pins Colorado HB 23-1099**; **note pins Washington SB 5495**; **note pins HUD pilot 2023-2025**; **note pins FCRA furnisher duties**; **note pins optional election** (OFFER MANDATE not REPORT MANDATE); **multiple failures stack**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_rent_escrow_withholding` is the **multi-jurisdictional tenant rent escrow / rent withholding for habitability violations framework module** — trader-landlord critical because the implied warranty of habitability is among the most powerful tenant defenses — a tenant who establishes habitability violation can REDUCE OR WITHHOLD RENT ENTIRELY without losing possession, and many state waivers are VOID as contrary to public policy. Many states ALSO authorize TENANT REPAIR-AND-DEDUCT with statutory damage caps per repair and per year. Companion to `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector`, `rental_basement_water_intrusion_disclosure`, `rental_bedroom_egress_window`, `landlord_repair_response_timeframe`.

**Six-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Tenant Remedy |
|--------------|--------|----------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 1942 + § 1942.4 | Repair-and-deduct (1 month cap, 2x/year) + § 1942.4 $100-$5,000 statutory damages after 35-day governmental notice |
| **New York** | N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 235-b + Park West Management Corp. v. Mitchell, 47 N.Y.2d 316 (1979) | IMPLIED warranty (WAIVER VOID) + rent abatement + repair-and-deduct + rent withholding (RPAPL § 711 defense) + damages |
| **Massachusetts** | Mass. G.L. c. 239 § 8A + 105 CMR 410 | Rent withholding with LOCAL BOARD OF HEALTH report + INTO ESCROW payment |
| **Illinois Chicago** | Chicago RLTO § 5-12-110 | 14-day cure + half-month/$500 repair-and-deduct + lease termination option |
| **Pennsylvania** | Pugh v. Holmes, 486 Pa. 272 (1979) | COMMON-LAW implied warranty + rent withholding as ejectment defense |
| **Default** | Common-law habitability | Varies by state |

Pinned by `ca_repair_within_one_month_cap_compliant`, `ca_repair_exceeds_one_month_cap_violation`, `ca_twice_per_year_limit_violation`, `ca_under_30_day_notice_violation`, `ca_1942_4_35_day_governmental_notice_engages`, `ca_1942_4_violation_caused_by_tenant_no_protection`, `ny_warranty_engages_on_habitability_violation`, `ny_waiver_void_note`, `ma_rent_withholding_requires_code_enforcement_report`, `ma_rent_withholding_requires_escrow_payment`, `chicago_repair_within_half_month_cap_compliant`, `chicago_500_dollar_floor_overrides_low_rent`, `chicago_under_14_day_cure_violation`, `pa_pugh_v_holmes_engages_on_violation`, `jurisdiction_truth_table_six_cells`.

**California § 1942 + § 1942.4 framework**:

| Subsection | Rule |
|-------------|------|
| § 1942(a) repair-and-deduct cap | ONE MONTH'S RENT per repair |
| § 1942(a) annual limit | TWICE in any 12-MONTH PERIOD |
| § 1942(b) notice presumption | 30-DAY presumption of reasonable time |
| § 1942.4 governmental notice trigger | 35 DAYS since governmental notice + building substandard under H&S Code § 17920.3 + violation not caused by tenant |
| § 1942.4(b) statutory damages | ACTUAL + $100-$5,000 STATUTORY + reasonable attorney fees + costs |

**NY § 235-b warranty of habitability — WAIVER VOID** — pinned by `ny_uniquely_engages_waiver_void_invariant` (NY only — CA + MA + Chicago + PA + Default do not engage waiver-void invariant).

Holmes); **note pins CA § 1942 repair-and-deduct** (1-month cap + twice/year); **note pins CA § 1942(b) 30-day presumption**; **note pins CA § 1942.4 35-day statutory damages**; **note pins NY § 235-b(1) implied warranty**; **note pins NY § 235-b(2) waiver void**; **note pins NY Park West Management**; **note pins MA § 8A code enforcement + escrow**; **note pins Chicago § 5-12-110 14-day cure**; **note pins PA Pugh v. Holmes 1979**; **note pins default common-law framework**; **note pins trader-landlord powerful tenant defenses**; **note pins cross-jurisdictional architecture**; **multiple CA failures stack**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_rent_judgment_wage_garnishment` is the **post-judgment wage garnishment for tenant rent debt** module — when a trader-landlord obtains a money judgment for unpaid rent + damages after eviction, can it enforce the judgment by garnishing the former tenant's wages at the employer level?
