Distinct from `damage_deduction_itemization` (security deposit deductions at move-out), `rent_credit_reporting` (positive rent reporting to consumer reporting agencies), and `prevailing_party_attorney_fees` (lease attorney-fee shifting) — this module addresses ONLY the post-money-judgment wage garnishment enforcement pathway.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | States | Authority | Civil-debt outcome |
|--------|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Fully Prohibited** | TX, NC, PA, SC | Tex. Const. art. XVI § 28; N.C. Const. art. X § 1 + N.C. Gen. Stat. § 1-362; 42 Pa. Cons. Stat. § 8127; S.C. Code § 37-5-104 | **ABSOLUTELY BARRED** at employer level |
| **State More Protective** | CA, MA, VA, others | Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 706.050 (50× state min); Mass. G.L. c. 246 § 28 (50× state min); Va. Code § 34-29 (75% / 40× federal min) | lesser of federal CCPA + state-specific exemption |
| **Federal Floor** | most other states | 15 U.S.C. § 1673(a)(1) | lesser of 25% disposable OR excess over 30× federal minimum |

**Four states absolutely prohibit civil-debt wage garnishment at the employer level.** Texas, North Carolina, Pennsylvania, and South Carolina bar private creditors (including landlords) from garnishing wages on civil judgments regardless of judgment size. Trader-landlords in these states must pivot to bank-account levy (the prohibition operates at the employer, not the bank) or other enforcement tools. Pinned by `texas_civil_debt_garnishment_prohibited`, `fully_prohibited_note_lists_all_four_states`, `fully_prohibited_notes_bank_account_carveout`, `fully_prohibited_uniquely_zero_garnishment_for_civil_debt_invariant` (3-regime sweep confirms only FullyProhibited yields zero), and `fully_prohibited_disposable_earnings_irrelevant_to_outcome` (4-cell income sweep all return zero).

**Federal CCPA § 1673(a)(1) — lesser of 25% disposable or excess over 30× federal minimum.** At the current $7.25/hour federal minimum wage, the 30× threshold is $217.50/week. A tenant earning exactly $217.50/week or less is fully exempt from garnishment. A high earner is capped at 25% of weekly disposable. Pinned by `federal_floor_high_earner_25_percent_governs`, `federal_floor_at_30x_threshold_zero_garnishment` (exactly at $217.50), `federal_floor_below_30x_zero_garnishment`, and `federal_floor_low_earner_excess_governs` (small excess governs because it's smaller than 25%).

**State-more-protective exemptions stack with federal CCPA.** Caller supplies the state-specific minimum wage and exemption multiplier (e.g., CA 50× $16/hour = $800 weekly exemption). The garnishment maximum is the MINIMUM of (a) federal CCPA limit and (b) state exemption excess. Pinned by `state_more_protective_california_50x_state_min_governs`, `state_more_protective_high_state_min_blocks_garnishment`, `massachusetts_50x_state_min_at_15_dollar_state_wage_750_dollar_exemption`, and `high_earner_state_more_protective_capped_at_25_percent_under_federal_floor`.

**Three carve-outs bypass civil-debt prohibitions.** Child support / alimony (§ 1673(a)(2) 50%/55%/60%/65% tiers), federal/state tax debt, and federally backed student loans (15% Department of Education administrative ceiling) all remain enforceable in the four prohibited states. Pinned by `child_support_bypasses_civil_debt_prohibitions`, `tax_debt_bypasses_civil_debt_prohibitions`, `student_loan_bypasses_civil_debt_prohibitions`, and `only_civil_debt_blocked_in_fully_prohibited_invariant` (3-cell carve-out sweep confirms all non-civil judgment types remain garnishable).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-rent-judgment-wage-garnishment`. Twenty-three tests pin: **Texas civil-debt garnishment prohibited**; **fully prohibited note lists all four states** (TX, NC, PA, SC); **fully prohibited notes bank-account carveout** (employer-level prohibition only); **federal floor high earner 25% governs** ($1K weekly → ~$250 garnishment); **federal floor at 30× threshold zero garnishment** ($217.50 boundary); **federal floor below 30× zero garnishment**; **federal floor low earner excess governs**; **state more protective California 50× state min governs**; **state more protective high state min blocks garnishment** ($600 weekly + $16 state min × 50 = $800 exempt); **child support bypasses civil-debt prohibitions** (§ 1673(a)(2) tier); **tax debt bypasses civil-debt prohibitions**; **student loan bypasses civil-debt prohibitions** (15% DoE administrative); **citation pins federal statute + four-state authorities**; **federal floor note describes lesser-of calculation**; **state more protective note describes state multiplier**; **only civil debt blocked in fully-prohibited invariant** (3-cell carve-out sweep); **federal minimum 725 cents 30× threshold pinned**; **fully prohibited uniquely zero garnishment for civil debt invariant** (3-regime sweep); **high earner state more protective capped at 25% under federal floor**; **Massachusetts 50× state min at $15 state wage $750 exemption**; **note for state more protective includes min formula**; **empty disposable earnings zero garnishment all regimes** (defensive); **fully prohibited disposable earnings irrelevant to outcome** (4-cell income sweep).

`traderview-expense::tenant_rent_receipt_requirement` is the **multi-jurisdictional tenant rent receipt requirement framework module** — trader-landlord critical because (1) cash rent receipts are mandatory in many states regardless of tenant request; (2) receipt-issuance failures create per-violation civil exposure plus an evidentiary presumption against the landlord in rent-payment disputes; (3) record retention obligations (3-year NY, similar elsewhere) extend long beyond tenancy termination; (4) modern payment methods (Zelle, Venmo, ACH) require careful receipt practices to satisfy state mandates. Companion to `landlord_annual_rent_statement`, `tenant_late_fee_cap`, `tenant_positive_rent_reporting`, `rental_junk_fee_transparency`.

**Five-jurisdiction framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Trigger | Receipt Required |
|--------------|--------|---------|------------------|
| **New York** | N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 235-e | Cash / money order / cashier's check / non-personal-check OR personal-check + written request | Yes; immediate in-person OR 15 days non-in-person + 3-year retention for cash |
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 1499 | Tenant request | Yes; signed + dated at TIME OF PAYMENT |
| **Massachusetts** | Mass. G.L. c. 186 § 15B | LAST MONTH'S RENT at commencement of tenancy | LIMITED mandate; regular monthly rent NOT required |
| **Washington** | Wash. Rev. Code § 59.18.063 | Cash payment OR non-cash payment + tenant request | Yes |
| **Default** | Common-law + local ordinances (Chicago RLTO, San Francisco) | None statewide | Payment-of-rent dispute defense |

Pinned by `ny_cash_in_person_with_receipt_compliant`, `ca_request_triggers_obligation`, `ma_last_month_rent_receipt_required`, `wa_cash_mandatory_obligation`, `default_jurisdiction_no_obligation`, `jurisdiction_truth_table_five_cells`, `ny_uniquely_engages_3_year_retention_invariant`.

**NY § 235-e six required content elements**:

| # | Element |
|---|---------|
| 1 | PAYMENT DATE |
| 2 | AMOUNT received |
| 3 | PERIOD for which rent was paid |
| 4 | APARTMENT NUMBER |
| 5 | SIGNATURE of person receiving payment |
| 6 | TITLE of person receiving payment |

Pinned by `ny_missing_content_violation`.

**NY § 235-e timing — immediate vs 15-day split**:

| Payment Mode | Required Timing |
|--------------|------------------|
| In-person cash or money order | **IMMEDIATE** at time of payment |
| Non-in-person (mail, ACH, electronic) | Within **15 DAYS** |

Pinned by `ny_cash_in_person_no_receipt_violation`, `ny_15_day_boundary_non_in_person_compliant` (day 15 = compliant), `ny_16_day_non_in_person_violation` (day 16 = violation).

**NY § 235-e personal-check special rule** — tenant may REQUEST in writing a rent receipt for personal-check payment; after first request, landlord must provide receipt EVERY MONTH thereafter. Pinned by `ny_personal_check_no_request_no_obligation`, `ny_personal_check_with_request_obligation_triggered`.

**NY § 235-e 3-year record retention** — landlord must keep proof of CASH rent receipts for **3 YEARS**. Pinned by `ny_no_3_year_retention_violation`, `ny_uniquely_engages_3_year_retention_invariant` (only NY mandates retention).

**CA § 1499 time-of-payment requirement** — receipt must be provided AT TIME OF PAYMENT (not year-end / not in lump sum at tax time); applies to ALL payment methods (cash, check, money order, electronic). Pinned by `ca_year_end_lump_sum_violation`.

**MA § 15B limited last-month-only mandate** — regular monthly rent does NOT require receipt (even cash); last month's rent at commencement of tenancy requires signed receipt with amount + date + description + landlord name + tenant name + recipient name. Pinned by `ma_regular_cash_no_obligation`, `ma_last_month_no_receipt_violation`.

**WA § 59.18.063 cash + request dual mandate** — cash payments MANDATORY receipt; non-cash payments require receipt on tenant request. Pinned by `wa_cash_no_receipt_violation`, `wa_non_cash_request_triggers_obligation`, `wa_non_cash_no_request_no_obligation`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-rent-receipt-requirement`. Thirty-six tests pin: **NY cash in person with receipt compliant**; **NY cash in person no receipt violation**; **NY money order in person no receipt violation**; **NY cashier's check no receipt violation** (15-day non-in-person); **NY personal check no request no obligation**; **NY personal check with request obligation triggered**; **NY 15-day boundary non-in-person compliant**; **NY 16-day non-in-person violation**; **NY missing content violation** (6 elements); **NY no 3-year retention violation**; **CA request triggers obligation**; **CA no request no obligation**; **CA request no receipt violation**; **CA year-end lump sum violation**; **MA last month rent receipt required**; **MA regular cash no obligation**; **MA last month no receipt violation**; **WA cash mandatory obligation**; **WA cash no receipt violation**; **WA non-cash request triggers obligation**; **WA non-cash no request no obligation**; **default jurisdiction no obligation**; **jurisdiction truth table five cells**; **NY uniquely engages 3-year retention invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (§ 235-e + § 1499 + c. 186 § 15B + § 59.18.063 + Chicago RLTO § 5-12-080(g)); **note pins NY immediate vs 15-day split**; **note pins NY six content elements**; **note pins NY personal check recurring**; **note pins NY 3-year retention**; **note pins CA request signed dated time of payment**; **note pins MA limited last month only**; **note pins WA cash mandatory + request non-cash**; **note pins default local ordinances**; **note pins trader-landlord modern payment methods**; **note pins cross-jurisdictional architecture** (METHOD-TRIGGERED + REQUEST-TRIGGERED + LIMITED LAST-MONTH-ONLY + CASH-TRIGGERED); **multiple NY failures stack**.

`traderview-expense::condominium_conversion_protection` is the **state landlord condominium-conversion tenant protection compliance check** — operational concern for any landlord planning to convert a rental building to condominium / cooperative ownership. Companion to `tenant_relocation_assistance` (general no-fault displacement) and `foreclosure_tenant_rights` (owner default), focused specifically on the OWNER-INITIATED conversion path. Five states + DC have layered protections; other jurisdictions rely on lease enforcement plus common law.

**Six regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Tenant majority vote | Senior/disabled bar | Notice (standard) | First refusal | Relocation | Unique feature |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|--------------------|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| `DistrictOfColumbia` | DC Code § 42-3401.01 et seq. (Rental Housing Conversion and Sale Act) + § 42-1904.08 | **YES** — majority vote in certified election + Mayor certification | — | 120d | 60d | — | Tenant **majority-vote prerequisite** to conversion |
| `Massachusetts` | G.L. c. 527 of Acts of 1983 | — | — | 365d (1 yr) | **90d** | **$750/$1,000** | Applies only to **4+ unit buildings**; elderly/disabled get 4-year notice + $1,000 relocation |
| `NewJersey` | N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.22 (Senior Citizens and Disabled Protected Tenancy Act) | — | **YES** — up to 40-year protected tenancy | 365d | 60d | — | Senior + disabled tenants effectively **BARRED** from conversion eviction |
| `NewYork` | N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 352-e + § 352-eee + § 352-eeee | — | Senior/disabled get **99-year non-eviction tenure** | 365d / 1095d | 90d | — | **EVICTION PLAN 51%** vs **NON-EVICTION PLAN 15%** purchase commitment thresholds |
| `MarylandMontgomery` | Mont. County Code Ch. 11A + Md. Code Real Property § 11-102.1 | — | — | 180d | 60d | — | 180-day state + county pre-recording notice |
| `Default` | None | — | — | — | — | — | No statewide protection; lease + common law control |

**DC majority-vote prerequisite is the unique state expansion.** DC Code § 42-3401.01 et seq. requires the owner to obtain (a) Mayor certification of compliance AND (b) a MAJORITY of tenants voting for conversion in a certified election. Without the majority vote, conversion is BARRED at the threshold — no notice or first-refusal procedure cures the gap. Pinned by `dc_majority_vote_obtained_compliant`, `dc_no_majority_vote_conversion_barred`, and the 6-regime invariant `only_dc_requires_tenant_majority_vote_invariant` (5 other regimes confirmed do NOT bar conversion on absence of tenant majority vote).

**Massachusetts is the only state with a building-size threshold.** G.L. c. 527 of the Acts of 1983 reaches only buildings of 4 or more rental units. A 3-unit building is outside the statute entirely and the landlord has no statutory notice / first-refusal / relocation obligation. Pinned by `massachusetts_3_units_below_threshold_no_statute`, `massachusetts_4_units_at_threshold_statute_applies`, and the 6-regime invariant `only_massachusetts_has_size_threshold_invariant`.

**Massachusetts tenant-class tiering** ties notice AND relocation to tenant status:
- Standard tenant: 365-day notice + $750 relocation
- Low/moderate income: 730-day notice + $1,000 relocation
- Elderly OR disabled: 1,460-day (4-year) notice + $1,000 relocation

Pinned by `massachusetts_senior_4_year_notice_required`, `massachusetts_disabled_4_year_notice_and_1000_relocation`, `massachusetts_low_moderate_income_2_year_notice_and_1000_relocation`, and the regression tests `massachusetts_under_notice_violation` and `massachusetts_relocation_below_threshold_violation`.

**New Jersey's senior/disabled protected tenancy BARS conversion eviction.** N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.22 establishes up to a 40-year protected tenancy period for senior (62+) and disabled tenants — effectively prohibiting the landlord from bringing an action for possession during that window. Standard tenants are not barred. Pinned by `new_jersey_senior_protected_tenancy_bars_conversion`, `new_jersey_disabled_protected_tenancy_bars_conversion`, `new_jersey_standard_tenant_no_bar`, and the 6-regime invariant `only_nj_bars_conversion_for_protected_senior_disabled_invariant` (5 other regimes confirmed do NOT statutorily bar conversion for senior tenants).

**New York's plan-type purchase-commitment thresholds.** § 352-eeee requires either (a) EVICTION PLAN — at least 51% of occupied apartments purchased by tenants in occupancy or eligible non-purchaser purchasers (allows eviction of non-purchasing tenants after a transition period); or (b) NON-EVICTION PLAN — at least 15% of all units purchased by tenants OR by non-tenant purchasers committed to non-eviction (non-purchasing tenants remain as rent-regulated). Pinned by `new_york_eviction_plan_at_51_percent_compliant`, `new_york_eviction_plan_below_51_percent_violation`, `new_york_non_eviction_plan_at_15_percent_compliant`, `new_york_non_eviction_plan_below_15_percent_violation`, and the invariant `only_ny_uses_plan_type_threshold_branching_invariant` (5 other regimes ignore plan type entirely).

**NY senior + disabled 99-year non-eviction tenure** is a unique senior/disabled protection layered ON TOP of either plan type. Pinned by `new_york_senior_99_year_non_eviction_note` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/condominium-conversion-protection`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **DC majority vote compliant + missing majority vote BARRED**; **MA 4+ units standard tenant compliant + 3 units below threshold no statute + 4 units at threshold**; **MA senior 4-year notice + disabled 4-year notice + $1000 relocation + low-moderate 2-year + $1000**; **MA under notice violation + relocation below threshold violation**; **NJ senior protected tenancy BARS conversion + disabled BARS + standard no bar**; **NY eviction plan at 51% + below 51% violation + non-eviction at 15% + below 15% violation + senior 99-year non-eviction note**; **MD-Montgomery 180-day notice compliant + under 180 violation**; **Default no statutory protection**; **missing first-refusal offer violation + first-refusal acceptance window too short violation**; **only-DC-requires-tenant-majority-vote 6-regime invariant**; **only-NJ-bars-conversion-for-protected-senior-disabled 6-regime invariant**; **only-MA-has-size-threshold 6-regime invariant**; **only-NY-uses-plan-type-threshold-branching 6-regime invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (§ 42-3401.01 for DC + c. 527 for MA + 2A:18-61.22 for NJ + § 352-e for NY + Mont. County Code Ch. 11A for MD + "No statewide" for Default); **sibling-module note across all 6 regimes** (UX-text regression for tenant_relocation_assistance + foreclosure_tenant_rights + OWNER-INITIATED scope).

`traderview-expense::tenant_relocation_assistance` is the **landlord-paid tenant relocation assistance compute module** — three U.S. jurisdictions ship concrete dollar formulas for relocation assistance owed when a no-fault eviction or qualifying displacement event occurs. Most other states leave the question to local ordinance or the lease itself. Modeling the three that have legislated amounts gives the calculator real cash-flow numbers while keeping the default branch honest (no obligation).

**Five regimes** with distinct trigger sets and dollar formulas:

| Regime | Trigger set | Amount | Notes |
|--------|-------------|--------|-------|
| `CaliforniaAb1482` (Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2(d)(3)) | owner move-in / withdrawal / demo / gov order | ONE MONTH rent | Paid within 15 days of notice OR waived from last month; strict-compliance voids non-compliant notice; post-2020-07-01 leases require written tenant agreement OR lease clause for owner move-in |
| `PortlandOr` (PCC 30.01.085) | no-cause / qualifying landlord-cause / rent ≥10% / owner move-in / demo | $2,900 studio · $3,300 1BR · $4,200 2BR · $4,500 3BR+ | 90-day notice required; non-compliance up to 3× rent + actual damages + attorney fees |
| `SeattleTrao` (SMC Ch. 22.210) | demo / substantial-rehab / change-of-use | $2,776 landlord + $2,776 City = $5,552 total | Only households ≤50% Seattle AMI qualify |
| `SeattleEdra` (SMC Ch. 22.212, eff. 2022-07-01) | rent increase ≥10% in rolling 12-month window | 3× monthly housing cost | Households ≤80% AMI only; City advances payment; landlord reimburses |
| `Default` | — | $0 | No statewide statute identified; local ordinance or lease may apply |

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-relocation-assistance`. Twenty-five tests pin: **CA owner-move-in owes one month rent** + note describes 15-day deadline; **CA written consent omits July-1-2020 caveat** (regression — caveat only fires when tenant_agreed_in_writing=false); **CA withdrawal-from-market qualifies**; **CA rent-increase does NOT qualify** (regression — § 1946.2 lists the 4 no-fault grounds only); **CA demolition + government-order both qualify**; **Portland studio = $2,900 / 1BR = $3,300 / 2BR = $4,200 / 3BR+ = $4,500** (4-tier bedroom regression); **Portland 10% rent-increase triggers**; **Portland government-order NOT qualifying** (regression — government order is a CA-only trigger, not Portland); **Seattle TRAO at 50% AMI boundary qualifies** + over-50% ineligible + 65%-message regression; **Seattle TRAO owner-move-in not qualifying** (regression — TRAO only fires on building changes); **Seattle TRAO city-contribution = $2,776** (split-pay regression target); **Seattle EDRA at 80% AMI boundary qualifies** + above-80% ineligible + 81%-message regression; **Seattle EDRA no-cause-termination NOT qualifying** (regression — EDRA only fires on rent-increase); **Default regime returns $0 + not-eligible**; **jurisdiction routing** (OR/portland → PortlandOr, WA/seattle → SeattleTrao, CA → CaliforniaAb1482, TX → Default); **case-insensitive jurisdiction lookup**; **citations pin authorities** (§ 1946.2(d)(3) + 30.01.085 + 22.210 + 22.212 + "July 1, 2022"); **city_contribution only for TRAO** (regression — every other regime's city contribution is 0).

`traderview-expense::tenant_rights_statement_disclosure` is the **Tenant Rights Statement disclosure compliance module** — when must a residential landlord distribute the official state-prepared statement of tenants' rights and responsibilities? Distinct from siblings `lease_disclosures` (mandated lease content), `plain_language_lease` (lease readability), and `landlord_identification_disclosure` (party-identification). New Jersey Truth in Renting Act is uniquely aggressive — annual distribution + new-tenant delivery + prominent posting + no tenant waiver permitted + Consumer Fraud Act treble damages exposure.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Annual distribution | Posting requirement | Treble damages |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| New Jersey § 46:8-43 to -50 | YES (30 days post-DCA publication) | YES (prominent location) | YES (CFA § 56:8-1) |
| Maryland § 8-208 | Limited (county supplements) | Limited | Common-law / county |
| New York HSTPA 2019 | NO statewide mandate | Voluntary | Per HSTPA discrete-disclosure framework |
| Default | NO statewide mandate | Voluntary | Municipal ordinance dependent |

**New Jersey — Truth in Renting Act, N.J.S.A. 46:8-43 to -50**. Most aggressive framework in the country. **§ 46:8-44** — NJ Department of Community Affairs (DCA) publishes Statement of Legal Rights and Responsibilities of Tenants and Landlords in BOTH English and Spanish, posted on DCA website, UPDATED ANNUALLY. **§ 46:8-45** — landlord MUST distribute one copy to each tenant within **30 DAYS** after DCA publication AND provide copy to each new tenant at or prior to occupancy. **§ 46:8-46** — landlord MUST post current Statement in one or more prominent accessible locations. **§ 46:8-47** — applies to buildings with > 2 units (or non-owner-occupied > 3 units). **§ 46:8-48** — no tenant waiver alters landlord obligations. **§ 46:8-49** — penalties + private right of action via N.J.S.A. 56:8-1 Consumer Fraud Act **treble damages**.

**Scoping rule**: NJ Truth in Renting Act applies to (a) any building with > 2 units OR (b) non-owner-occupied buildings with > 3 units. Owner-occupied 2-3 unit buildings are OUT of scope. Pinned by `scoping_truth_table_owner_occupied_vs_unit_count` (6-cell truth table across unit_count × owner_occupied).

**Maryland — Md. Code, Real Property § 8-208**. Limited tenant-rights notice; landlord must provide certain notices but not the comprehensive Statement-of-Rights document NJ requires. County / municipal supplements (Montgomery County, Prince George's County) extend coverage.

**New York — HSTPA of 2019**. Imposes discrete-disclosure mandates (rent stabilization status, security deposit handling) but NO statutory annual-distribution mandate equivalent to NJ. NY DHCR Residential Tenants' Rights Guide + NY AG Residential Tenants' Rights Guide — voluntary distribution recommended but not statutorily mandated.

**Default**. Most states have NO statewide Tenant-Rights-Statement distribution mandate. Municipal ordinances may impose distribution requirements (Chicago Tenants in Foreclosure Notification Ordinance, San Francisco Rent Ordinance § 37).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-rights-statement-disclosure`. Twenty-six tests pin: **NJ full compliance passes**; **NJ missing 30-day distribution violates** (§ 46:8-45); **NJ missing new-tenant delivery violates**; **NJ missing posting violates** (§ 46:8-46); **NJ relying on tenant waiver violates** (§ 46:8-48); **NJ 2-unit non-owner-occupied out of scope** (§ 46:8-47); **NJ 3-unit non-owner-occupied in scope**; **NJ 3-unit owner-occupied out of scope**; **NJ 4-unit owner-occupied in scope**; **NJ DCA annual publication note** (§ 46:8-44 ANNUALLY); **NJ bilingual English + Spanish note**; **NJ penalties note with Consumer Fraud Act** (§ 56:8-1 treble damages); **NJ all four violations simultaneous**; **NJ citation pins 46:8-43 to 46:8-50**; **MD no violations even when no distribution**; **MD county / municipal supplement note** (Montgomery County + Prince George's County); **MD citation pins § 8-208**; **NY no violations + no mandate note** (HSTPA NO statutory annual-distribution mandate); **NY DHCR + AG guide note**; **default no obligation compliant**; **default municipal examples note** (Chicago + San Francisco § 37); **NJ unique distribution mandate invariant** (3-other-regime sweep); **four regimes routed correctly**; **NJ 2-unit owner-occupied out of scope**; **NJ out-of-scope compliant even with no distribution**; **scoping truth table owner-occupied vs unit count** (6-cell sweep across 1/2/3/4 units × owner-occupied / non-owner-occupied).

`traderview-expense::tenant_utility_account_designation` is the **tenant right to designate utility service account in tenant's own name module** — when may the residential tenant become the direct customer of record with the utility company (vs being forced onto the landlord's master account)? Distinct from siblings `submetering_rules` (sub-metering setup framework), `utility_shutoff` (shutoff procedural protections), and `non_refundable_cleaning_fees` (move-out fees). Trader-landlord operational concern when landlord defaults on utility bills — CA § 777 lets tenant assume the account WITHOUT paying landlord's arrears.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Scoping | Right engaged when | Arrears protection |
|--------|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| California Cal. Pub. Util. Code § 777 | Individually metered only (MASTER CARVED OUT) | Landlord delinquent + tenant requests + verification | YES (tenant not liable for landlord arrears) |
| New York N.Y. Pub. Serv. Law §§ 32, 33, 33-a (HEFPA) | Individually OR sub-metered (master carved out) | Tenant requests + landlord is customer | Via PSC tariff |
| Default | Individually OR sub-metered | No statewide right | None |

**California — Cal. Pub. Util. Code § 777**. Most explicit tenant-protection framework. Applies to landlord-tenant arrangements where landlord is customer of record for INDIVIDUALLY METERED residential service (electric, gas, heat, water). Tenant has right to become customer WITHOUT being required to pay any delinquent amount due on landlord's account. Utility corporation MUST inform occupants of right. Verification methods: lease, rent receipts, or government document confirming tenancy. **Master-metered apartment buildings are CARVED OUT** — RUBS / master-meter pass-through scenarios fall outside § 777 protection. § 777.1 supplements with related utility-corporation disclosure obligations.

**Three conjunctive elements for CA right** — landlord is customer of record AND tenant requests to become customer AND tenant can verify landlord's status. All three required. Pinned by `ca_three_conjunctive_elements_required_for_right` (8-cell truth table sweep).

**New York — N.Y. Pub. Serv. Law §§ 32, 33, 33-a (Home Energy Fair Practices Act / HEFPA)**. Residential utility customer protections under PSC tariff framework. Tenant in shared-meter arrangement may petition NY PSC for separate account designation. Sub-metered units within scope (unlike CA § 777 which only covers individually metered).

**Default — lease + utility tariff**. No statewide tenant utility-designation right. Rules set by state-PUC-regulated utility tariff and lease provisions. Master-metered buildings typically lack tenant-side designation right.

**CA master-meter carve-out is the most distinctive feature**. Across CA, NY, and Default, master-metered buildings (RUBS pass-through scenarios) all fall outside protection — but CA's § 777 has the strongest individually-metered protection of any state. Pinned by `ca_master_meter_carveout_unique_invariant` (3-regime sweep all reject master-metered).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-utility-account-designation`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **CA individually metered full request right engaged**; **CA master metered not applies no right** (§ 777 master-meter carve-out); **CA landlord not customer of record no right**; **CA tenant cannot verify landlord status no right**; **CA tenant does not request no right**; **CA utility failed to notify violation** (§ 777 utility MUST inform); **CA no delinquency no violation for missing notice**; **CA right engaged note describes delinquent-amount protection** (tenant NOT required to pay landlord arrears); **CA verification methods note present** (lease + rent receipts + government document); **CA citation pins §§ 777 + 777.1**; **CA sub-metered does not apply**; **NY HEFPA individually metered right engaged**; **NY master metered does not engage right**; **NY PSC petition note for shared meter**; **NY citation pins HEFPA §§ 32 + 33 + 33-a**; **default no statewide right no engagement**; **default individually metered within scope note** (statute-set tariff + lease); **default master metered outside scope**; **default citation references PUC tariff and lease**; **CA unique explicit § 777 protection invariant** (default lacks protection); **three regimes routed correctly**; **metering type truth table California** (3-type sweep: IndividuallyMetered / MasterMetered / SubMetered); **NY sub-metered outside master meter engages right** (NY broader than CA on sub-metering); **CA master meter carveout unique invariant** (3-regime sweep all reject master); **CA three conjunctive elements required for right** (8-cell truth table); **CA § 777.1 disclosure note present**; **CA master meter no violation even when landlord delinquent** (carve-out absolute).

`traderview-expense::tenant_smart_lock_biometric_consent` is the **tenant right to refuse landlord-installed BIOMETRIC smart lock module** — biometric privacy framework when landlord installs fingerprint / facial recognition / retinal scanner smart locks. Distinct from siblings `security_camera_disclosure` (landlord-installed surveillance cameras), `lock_change_between_tenancies` (between-tenancy lock changes), `landlord_tenant_recording_consent` (audio recording consent), and `tenant_data_privacy` (general tenant data handling). Emerging trader-landlord privacy issue in IL, WA, TX (biometric privacy states) + CA/NY frameworks.

**Five regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Statutory damages | Private right of action |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Illinois 740 ILCS 14 BIPA | § 15(b) consent + § 15(c) no-profit + § 20 private action | $1,000 negligent / $5,000 reckless per violation | YES — § 20 |
| Washington RCW 19.375 | Biometric Identifiers Act | AG-only enforcement | NO |
| Texas Bus. & Comm. § 503.001 | Capture or Use of Biometric Identifier | $25,000 per violation AG civil penalty | NO |
| California CCPA/CPRA + Civ. Code § 1940.2 | Sensitive personal information classification | Data-breach private action only | Limited |
| Default | FTC Act § 5 + state UDAP + common-law privacy | Varies by state | Varies |

**Illinois — 740 ILCS 14 BIPA (most aggressive framework in the country)**. § 15(b) requires private entity to (1) inform individual in writing biometric information is being collected, (2) identify in writing specific PURPOSE and LENGTH OF TERM for collection/storage/use, (3) receive WRITTEN RELEASE from individual. § 15(c) prohibits sale, lease, trade, or other profit from biometric information. § 20 PRIVATE RIGHT OF ACTION with $1,000 negligent / $5,000 reckless statutory damages (or actual damages, whichever greater) + reasonable attorney fees + costs + injunctive relief. **Cothron v. White Castle System, Inc., 2023 IL 128004** — each biometric scan is a SEPARATE BIPA violation (per-scan accrual). Pinned by `il_cothron_per_scan_stacking` (50 scans × $1,000 = $50,000 exposure), `il_reckless_and_50_scans_per_scan_5000` (50 × $5,000 = $250,000), `il_negligent_vs_reckless_damages_5x_ratio`.

**Washington — RCW 19.375 Biometric Identifiers Act**. Enrollment of biometric identifier in database for COMMERCIAL PURPOSE requires NOTICE and CONSENT. Enforcement limited to Washington Attorney General — **NO statutory private right of action** (unlike IL BIPA). Pinned by `wa_no_private_right_of_action`.

**Texas — Tex. Bus. & Comm. Code § 503.001 Capture or Use of Biometric Identifier**. Capture of biometric identifier for COMMERCIAL PURPOSE requires NOTICE and CONSENT. Civil penalty **$25,000 PER VIOLATION** enforced by Texas Attorney General. NO statutory private right of action. Pinned by `tx_civil_penalty_25000_per_violation`.

**California — Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1798.80 + 1798.100 et seq. (CCPA/CPRA)**. Biometric identifiers classified as SENSITIVE PERSONAL INFORMATION subject to disclosure + deletion rights. No California-specific biometric statute. Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.2 prohibited harassment includes coercive conduct (relevant when landlord conditions access on biometric enrollment).

**Default — no biometric statute**. Federal FTC § 5 (15 U.S.C. § 45) UDAP doctrine applies. State UDAP statutes (47 states + DC) and common-law invasion-of-privacy torts available.

**Anti-tying principle across ALL regimes** — even where landlord may install a biometric smart lock with proper consent, tenant retains right to TRADITIONAL KEY ACCESS as alternative when tenant refuses biometric enrollment. Landlord conditioning entry solely on biometric enrollment violates anti-tying + quiet enjoyment. Pinned by `anti_tying_violation_across_all_regimes_when_no_key_alternative` (5-regime sweep).

**Illinois unique private right of action invariant** — only IL provides per-violation statutory damages with private right of action. WA, TX use AG-only enforcement. Pinned by `il_unique_private_right_of_action_invariant` (4-regime sweep).

**Illinois unique per-scan stacking invariant** — only IL stacks damages per scan under Cothron. Pinned by `il_unique_per_scan_stacking_invariant` (IL 100 scans × $1,000 = $100,000 vs TX fixed $25,000).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-smart-lock-biometric-consent`. Thirty-six tests pin: **IL full compliance no violation**; **IL missing consent triggers violation** (14/15(b)(3) WRITTEN RELEASE); **IL missing purpose disclosure triggers violation** (14/15(b)(2) PURPOSE and LENGTH OF TERM); **IL profit from biometric triggers § 15(c) violation**; **IL negligent damages $1,000 per violation**; **IL reckless damages $5,000 per violation**; **IL Cothron per-scan stacking** (50 × $1,000); **IL reckless and 50 scans per-scan $5,000** (50 × $5,000); **IL no violations no exposure**; **IL private right of action always available**; **IL attorney fees recoverable when violation**; **IL anti-tying violation when no key alternative**; **IL citation pins BIPA subsections and Cothron**; **WA missing consent violation** (RCW 19.375); **WA no private right of action**; **WA no statutory damages per violation**; **TX missing consent violation** (§ 503.001 $25,000 per violation); **TX civil penalty $25,000 per violation**; **TX no private right of action**; **CA CCPA classifies biometric as sensitive PI**; **CA missing consent violation** (§ 1798.100 CCPA/CPRA); **CA no state-specific biometric statute note**; **default no state-specific statute note** (FTC Act § 5); **default anti-tying violation when no key alternative**; **traditional key access required when biometric lock installed** (5-regime sweep); **no lock installed no key access required**; **five regimes routed correctly**; **IL unique private right of action invariant** (4-regime sweep); **IL unique per-scan stacking invariant** (vs TX fixed); **IL negligent vs reckless damages 5x ratio**; **IL saturating mul no overflow on extreme scans** (u32::MAX); **IL zero scans floors at one scan**; **anti-tying violation across all regimes when no key alternative** (5-regime sweep); **full IL compliance no violations**; **tenant refusal engaged when refused**; **tenant refusal not engaged when consented**.

`traderview-expense::source_of_income_discrimination` is the **source-of-income (SOI) discrimination ban module** — when may a landlord lawfully refuse to accept Housing Choice Voucher (Section 8), HUD-VASH, SSI/SSDI, child support/alimony, TANF, or other lawful non-wage income as the basis for rejecting an applicant? Trader-landlord critical: refusing a Section 8 voucher in a SOI-protected jurisdiction triggers five-figure penalties + actual damages + attorney fees + punitive damages; in non-protected jurisdictions the federal Fair Housing Act does NOT make voucher refusal a per se violation. Distinct from `fair_chance_housing` (criminal background screening) and `tenant_in_foreclosure_protection` (post-foreclosure protections).

**Four regimes** — SOI-protected (CA + NJ + NY) versus federal FHA baseline:

| Regime | Authority | Max civil penalty | First-offense penalty | Subsequent offense |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| California | Cal. Gov. Code §§ 12955 + 12987 + 12989.2 + SB 329 (eff. 2020) | $50,000 | Same | Same |
| New Jersey | N.J.S.A. 10:5-12.5 (NJ LAD) + DCR | **Tiered**: $10K first / $25K subsequent | $10,000 | $25,000 |
| New York | N.Y. Exec. Law § 296(5)(a)(1) (eff. April 2019) + NYC Admin. Code § 8-107(5)(a)(5) (eff. 2008) | $250,000 | Same | Same |
| Default | 42 USC § 3604 (federal FHA baseline) | $0 — no per se SOI protection | N/A | N/A |

**California SB 329 (Housing Opportunities Act of 2019)** — Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers explicitly added to FEHA source-of-income definition under Cal. Gov. Code § 12955. Remedies under § 12987 + § 12989.2: actual damages + emotional distress + civil penalty + attorney fees + injunctive relief. Narrow owner-occupied single-family-dwelling carve-out (≤4 units) available but rarely satisfied for investor traders. Pinned by `ca_voucher_refusal_violates`, `ca_disparate_income_multiple_violates` (disparate income-multiple application), `ca_note_pins_sb329_eff_2020`, `ca_max_penalty_50k_cents`, and `ca_small_landlord_carveout_pins_warning_note`.

**New Jersey LAD tiered penalty structure** — uniquely tiered among the three protected regimes: DCR administrative $1,000-$5,000 initial enforcement; LAD up to $10,000 first offense / $25,000 subsequent offense. Penalty escalation triggered by `prior_findings_count > 0`. Pinned by `nj_first_offense_penalty_10k`, `nj_subsequent_offense_penalty_25k`, `nj_uniquely_tiered_penalty_invariant` (tiered escalation vs CA + NY fixed-cap), and `nj_note_pins_dcr_penalty_range`.

**New York dual-layer state + NYC enforcement** — N.Y. Exec. Law § 296(5)(a)(1) state-level SOI ban (eff. April 2019) layered on top of NYC Admin. Code § 8-107(5)(a)(5) city-level ban (eff. 2008). NYC Commission on Human Rights has obtained over $780K in damages + penalties since 2014 enforcement. Pinned by `ny_voucher_refusal_violates`, `ny_citation_pins_state_and_nyc`, `ny_nyc_enforcement_note_pinned` ($780K + 2014), and `ny_note_pins_eff_april_2019`.

**Default federal FHA baseline (42 USC § 3604) — NO per se SOI protection**. Section 8 voucher refusal is NOT a federal FHA violation absent disparate-treatment or disparate-impact showing. Local protections may apply (Cook County IL, Seattle WA, DC, etc.) but not universal. Pinned by `default_no_protection_no_violation`, `default_uniquely_no_protection_invariant` (3-regime sweep: CA + NJ + NY engage, Default does NOT), and `default_note_pins_local_protections_warning` (Cook County + Seattle hints).

**Protected source truth table** — Section 8 + HUD-VASH + SSI/SSDI + child support/alimony + TANF all engage SOI protection; employment wages do NOT (income-multiple screening lawful). Pinned by `protected_source_truth_table` (6-source sweep), `ssi_ssdi_protected_in_ca`, `child_support_protected_in_ny`, `tanf_protected_in_nj`, `hud_vash_protected_in_ca`, and `ca_employment_wages_no_protection`.

**Three remedies engage together in protected regimes** — actual damages + attorney fees + punitive damages are cumulative when a SOI violation is established. Pinned by `three_remedies_engage_together_in_protected_regimes` (3-regime sweep) and `protected_regimes_all_punish_voucher_refusal`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/source-of-income-discrimination`. Thirty-two tests pin: **CA voucher refusal violates** (FEHA § 12955); **CA employment wages no protection**; **CA disparate income multiple violates** (income-multiple applied to voucher portion); **CA unrelated legitimate reason no violation**; **CA citation pins SB 329 and subsections**; **CA max penalty 50k cents**; **NJ voucher refusal violates**; **NJ first offense penalty 10k**; **NJ subsequent offense penalty 25k**; **NJ citation pins LAD**; **NY voucher refusal violates**; **NY citation pins state and NYC**; **NY NYC enforcement note pinned** ($780K + 2014); **default no protection no violation**; **default citation pins FHA baseline**; **protected source truth table** (6-source sweep); **four regimes routed correctly**; **protected regimes all punish voucher refusal** (3-regime sweep); **default uniquely no protection invariant** (Default vs CA + NJ + NY); **refusal reason truth table California** (4-cell); **CA small landlord carveout pins warning note** (≤4 units); **NJ uniquely tiered penalty invariant** (NJ tiered vs CA + NY fixed); **defensive prior findings overflow clamped** (u32::MAX → tier 2); **SSI SSDI protected in CA**; **child support protected in NY**; **TANF protected in NJ**; **HUD VASH protected in CA**; **CA note pins SB 329 eff 2020**; **NJ note pins DCR penalty range** ($1K-$5K + $25K); **NY note pins eff April 2019**; **default note pins local protections warning** (Cook County + Seattle); **three remedies engage together in protected regimes** (3-regime sweep).

`traderview-expense::fair_chance_housing` is the **landlord criminal-background-check restriction compliance check** — three U.S. jurisdictions have enacted statutes layering on top of the federal FCRA (15 U.S.C. § 1681c) to restrict how residential landlords may inquire about and consider applicants' criminal history. Distinct from `tenant_data_privacy` (data-handling) and `application_fees` (cost of the background check itself); this module models the substantive limits on what convictions a landlord may consider and when.

**Four regimes** with distinct pre-/post-conditional-offer rules, barred categories, and lookback windows:

| Regime | Statute | Pre-offer inquiry | Lookback window | Barred categories | Other reqs |
|--------|---------|--------------------|-----------------|--------------------|------------|
| `NewJerseyFcha` | N.J.S.A. 46:8-52 et seq. (S250 / A1919, eff. 2022-01-01) | Prohibited | FCHA formula (e.g. 1st-deg indictable 6yr; 4th-deg 1yr) | arrests, pending cases, sealed/expunged, juvenile adj. | Individualized assessment + 30-day appeal window |
| `NewYorkCityFchha` | NYC Admin Code § 8-107.1 (Local Law 24 of 2024, eff. 2025-01-01) | Prohibited | Felony 5yr / Misdemeanor 3yr (from release or sentencing) | arrests, pending cases, ACDs, juvenile adj., sealed/expunged, non-criminal-violation convictions | Sex-offender-registry convictions always considerable |
| `CaliforniaFeha` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1786.18 + 2 Cal. Code Regs. § 12266 | Allowed | None statutory; CRA reporting limit applies | arrests, sealed/expunged | No blanket bans; individualized assessment (nature/severity + time + rehabilitation) |
| `Default` | 15 U.S.C. § 1681c (FCRA) | Allowed | 7-year ceiling on CRA arrest reports; conviction reports have no federal time limit | arrests > 7 years old (FCRA cap) | None state-specific |

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/fair-chance-housing`. Twenty-five tests pin: **NJ pre-offer inquiry violation** (inquiry_before_offer=true + conditional_offer_made=false); **NJ post-offer inquiry OK** with individualized assessment; **NJ arrest barred category**; **NJ juvenile-adjudication barred** (regression — NJ FCHA bars 4 categories); **NJ no-individualized-assessment violation** + "30-day appeal" citation regression; **NYC pre-offer inquiry violation** + "Local Law 24" citation; **NYC felony at 5-year boundary OK** (regression — ≤ 5 strict); **NYC felony 6 years outside lookback** (regression — > 5 strict); **NYC misdemeanor at 3-year boundary OK**; **NYC misdemeanor 4 years outside lookback** (regression — > 3 strict); **NYC sex-offender-registry always considerable** regardless of age (30 years old still OK); **NYC non-criminal-violation barred** + "non-criminal" citation regression (NYC-only barred category not in NJ); **NYC ACD-disposition barred** + "ACDs" citation; **CA arrest barred**; **CA no-individualized-assessment violation** + "rehabilitation" note regression + "12266" citation; **CA with individualized assessment OK**; **default FCRA 7-year arrest window expired** + "1681c" + "FCRA" citation; **default no-violation recent conviction**; **jurisdiction routing** (NJ → NJ FCHA, NY/New York + NY/NYC → NYC FCHHA, NY/Buffalo → Default, CA → CA FEHA, TX → Default); **case-insensitive jurisdiction lookup**; **NJ-only single-state-uniqueness invariant**; **NYC lookback off-by-one boundary exhaustive** (felony 5 OK / 6 expired + misdemeanor 3 OK / 4 expired in single test); **citations pin authorities** (46:8-52 + FCHA + 8-107.1 + Local Law 24 + 1786.18 + 12266 + FCRA + 1681c).

`traderview-expense::family_childcare_home_right` is the **tenant family child-care home (FCCH) operation right module** — when may a landlord lawfully prohibit a tenant from operating a licensed family day care home for children in a residential rental unit? Trader-landlord critical: in California, a lease provision prohibiting family child care home operation is VOID, and a landlord cannot reject a tenant for intending to operate one. Distinct from siblings `tenant_organizing` (tenant-association-formation right), `tenant_data_privacy` (data handling), and `fair_chance_housing` (criminal background screening).

**Three regimes** — California's "void as a matter of law" framework vs New York's reasonableness review vs Default federal FHA familial status only:

| Regime | Authority | Lease prohibition | Notice required | Deposit cap | License authority |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|-------------------|
| California | Cal. Health & Safety Code §§ 1597.40-1597.46 + Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5 | **VOID** under § 1597.40(a) | **30 days advance written** | **1 month rent** (AB 12) | Title 22 CDSS |
| New York | N.Y. Social Services Law § 390 + N.Y. RPL § 235-b | Subject to reasonableness review | 0 days statutory | None statutory | OCFS |
| Default | 42 USC § 3604 (FHA familial status) | Allowed (covers families with children but NOT childcare-business) | 0 days | None | N/A federal |

**California § 1597.40(a) void-as-a-matter-of-law lease prohibition** — every restriction or prohibition on use of property for a family day care home for children is VOID, whether by covenant, condition upon use or occupancy, or upon transfer of title. State preemption under § 1597.40(e) occupies the field to exclusion of municipal zoning + building + fire codes. Pinned by `ca_lease_prohibition_void`, `ca_uniquely_voids_lease_prohibition_invariant` (3-regime sweep: only CA voids prohibition), `ca_note_pins_state_preemption`, and `ca_citation_pins_six_health_safety_sections` (§§ 1597.40 + 1597.41 + 1597.42 + 1597.43 + 1597.44 + 1597.46 + Civ § 1950.5).

**California § 1597.40(c) 30-day advance written notice** — prospective FCCH provider tenant must provide 30 days' written notice to landlord prior to commencement of operation. Pinned by `ca_30_day_notice_boundary_compliant` (boundary), `ca_29_day_notice_violates`, `ca_zero_days_notice_violates`, `ca_60_day_notice_compliant`, `ca_uniquely_requires_30_day_notice_invariant` (CA = 30 days; NY + Default = 0 days), and `ca_note_pins_30_day_notice`.

**California § 1597.40(d) increased deposit subject to AB 12 cap** — landlord may require increased security deposit specifically for FCCH operation (notwithstanding lower amount required of non-FCCH tenants), but TOTAL security deposit may NOT exceed Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5 ceiling (AB 12 = 1 month's rent for most landlords post-July 2024). Pinned by `ca_deposit_at_1_month_boundary_compliant`, `ca_deposit_over_1_month_violates`, `ca_max_total_deposit_equals_one_month_rent`, `ca_increased_deposit_within_cap_does_not_violate`, `ca_deposit_cap_uniquely_engaged_invariant` (CA engages cap; NY + Default do not), and `ca_note_pins_ab_12_deposit_cap`.

**California § 1597.42 Title 22 licensing required** — FCCH operation requires license from California Department of Social Services Community Care Licensing under Title 22 California Code of Regulations. Pinned by `ca_unlicensed_fcch_violates`.

**California 4-violation stack on full failure** — lease prohibition + < 30-day notice + over-AB 12 deposit + unlicensed = 4 simultaneous violations. Pinned by `ca_stacks_4_violations_when_all_4_failures`.

**New York reasonableness-review framework** — N.Y. Social Services Law § 390 protects licensed family day care and group family day care home operation; landlord may not unreasonably withhold consent. Lacks CA's explicit void-as-matter-of-law provision. OCFS licensing required. Pinned by `ny_compliant_passes`, `ny_lease_prohibition_violates_reasonableness`, `ny_unlicensed_fcch_violates` (OCFS), and `ny_citation_pins_state_law`.

**Default federal FHA familial-status baseline** — 42 USC § 3604 covers families with children from refusal to rent, but does NOT extend to childcare-business operation. Lease prohibition is enforceable at federal default level. Local protections may apply (Cook County IL, Seattle WA, Minneapolis MN). Pinned by `default_no_prohibition_permitted`, `default_lease_prohibition_blocks_operation`, `default_citation_pins_fha_baseline`, and `default_note_pins_local_protections` (Cook County + Seattle).

**Defensive arithmetic** — negative rent / negative deposits clamped to 0 via `.max(0)` and `.saturating_add()`. Pinned by `defensive_negative_rent_clamped` and `defensive_negative_deposit_clamped`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/family-childcare-home-right`. Thirty-two tests pin: **CA compliant passes**; **CA lease prohibition void** (§ 1597.40(a)); **CA 29-day notice violates**; **CA 30-day notice boundary compliant**; **CA deposit at 1-month boundary compliant**; **CA deposit over 1-month violates** (AB 12); **CA unlicensed FCCH violates** (§ 1597.42 Title 22); **CA citation pins six Health & Safety sections**; **CA note pins state preemption**; **CA note pins 30-day notice**; **CA note pins AB 12 deposit cap**; **NY compliant passes**; **NY lease prohibition violates reasonableness**; **NY unlicensed FCCH violates** (OCFS); **NY citation pins state law**; **default no prohibition permitted**; **default lease prohibition blocks operation**; **default citation pins FHA baseline**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **CA uniquely voids lease prohibition invariant**; **CA uniquely requires 30-day notice invariant**; **CA stacks 4 violations when all 4 failures**; **defensive negative rent clamped**; **defensive negative deposit clamped**; **CA 60-day notice compliant**; **CA zero-day notice violates**; **CA increased deposit within cap does not violate**; **NY no deposit tracking**; **default no deposit tracking**; **CA deposit cap uniquely engaged invariant**; **default note pins local protections**; **CA max total deposit equals one month rent**.

`traderview-expense::fha_design_construction` is the **Fair Housing Act design and construction requirements for covered multifamily dwellings** module — 24 CFR § 100.205. Federal accessibility floor for new-construction multifamily buildings first occupied AFTER March 13, 1991. Trader-landlord operational concern when acquiring, developing, or substantially renovating a covered multifamily dwelling. Distinct from `reasonable_accommodation_modification` (individual tenant accommodation requests under FHA § 3604(f)(3)), `service_animal` (ADA/FHA service-animal framework), and `emotional_support_animal_documentation` (ESA reliability).

**Seven § 100.205(c) requirements** all must be satisfied:

| # | Requirement | Subsection |
|---|-------------|------------|
| 1 | Accessible building entrance on accessible route (terrain-impracticality defense available, burden on builder) | § 100.205(c)(1) |
| 2 | Accessible and usable public/common-use areas (lobbies, mailboxes, parking, recreation) | § 100.205(c)(2) |
| 3 | Usable doors (32" clear opening for wheelchair passage) | § 100.205(c)(3) |
| 4 | Accessible route INTO AND THROUGH covered dwelling unit | § 100.205(c)(4) |
| 5 | Light switches, outlets, thermostats, environmental controls in accessible locations | § 100.205(c)(5) |
| 6 | Reinforced walls in bathrooms for later grab-bar installation | § 100.205(c)(6) |
| 7 | Usable kitchens and bathrooms (wheelchair maneuverability) | § 100.205(c)(7) |

**Two applicability gates** kill the rule at the door:

| Gate | Effect |
|------|--------|
| First occupancy on or before March 13, 1991 | Grandfathered — rule does NOT apply |
| Not a covered multifamily dwelling (< 4 units / not elevator-served / not ground-floor) | Rule does NOT apply |

**Federal enforcement carries dual private + administrative paths.** 42 U.S.C. § 3613(c) private suit — actual damages + punitive + attorney fees. 42 U.S.C. § 3612 HUD administrative civil penalty — $25,597 first violation / $63,991 prior violation within 5 years (2025 inflation-adjusted). Pinned by `first_violation_penalty_25597`, `prior_violation_within_5_years_penalty_63991`, `private_suit_remedies_in_violation_note`, `no_civil_penalty_when_compliant`.

**Terrain-impracticality defense is § 100.205(a)-specific to Requirement 1.** Burden is on designer/builder to prove that terrain or unusual site characteristics make the accessible building entrance impractical. The defense extends ONLY to Requirement 1 — it does NOT excuse failures on Requirements 2-7. Pinned by `missing_requirement_1_with_terrain_impracticality_no_violation`, `impracticality_defense_does_not_extend_to_other_requirements`, `impracticality_carveout_only_applies_to_requirement_1`.

**All seven requirements must be satisfied — single gap defeats compliance.** Pinned by `all_seven_requirements_must_be_satisfied_invariant` (7-iteration sweep flipping each requirement to false confirms each individually breaks compliance), `all_seven_violations_accumulate_when_nothing_satisfied` (all 7 violations stack), `multiple_violations_accumulate` (3 simultaneous failures = 3 violations listed).

**Federal floor — state codes may add but cannot reduce.** CA Title 24, MA Architectural Access Board (AAB), and NY Human Rights Law all add stricter accessibility requirements; the FHA design and construction baseline cannot be reduced by state law. Pinned by `federal_floor_note_always_present`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/fha-design-construction`. Twenty-six tests pin: **full compliance passes**; **pre-March 13, 1991 grandfathered** (rule does NOT apply); **non-covered multifamily dwelling rule does not apply**; **missing Requirement 1 without impracticality violation**; **missing Requirement 1 with terrain impracticality no violation** (defense documented); **missing Requirement 2 public/common areas violation**; **missing Requirement 3 usable doors violation** (wheelchair passage); **missing Requirement 4 accessible route violation**; **missing Requirement 5 environmental controls violation** (thermostats); **missing Requirement 6 reinforced walls violation** (grab-bar); **missing Requirement 7 kitchens/bathrooms violation** (wheelchair maneuverability); **first violation penalty $25,597**; **prior violation within 5 years penalty $63,991**; **private suit remedies in violation note** (§ 3613(c) actual + punitive + attorney fees); **all seven requirements must be satisfied invariant** (7-iteration sweep); **multiple violations accumulate**; **federal floor note always present**; **citation pins subsections and authorities** (§ 3604(f)(3)(C) + § 100.205(a)/(c)(1)-(7) + Subpart D + § 3613(c) + § 3612 + HUD Design Manual); **rule applies only when post-March 13 1991 AND covered**; **note describes civil penalty for first violation** ($25,597); **note describes civil penalty for prior violation** ($63,991); **no civil penalty when compliant**; **impracticality defense does not extend to other requirements** (Req 3 still violates with Req 1 defense); **all seven violations accumulate when nothing satisfied**; **impracticality carveout only applies to Requirement 1**; **pre-March 13, 1991 with full violations remains compliant** (grandfathered building immune).

`traderview-expense::demolition_tenant_notice` is the **state demolition-tenant-notice landlord compliance check** — addresses the NOTICE PERIOD a landlord must provide BEFORE a tenant must vacate for demolition. Distinct from `owner_move_in_eviction` (landlord MOVES INTO the unit) and `tenant_relocation_assistance` (DOLLAR amount owed); this module models the notice-time floor.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Standard notice | Senior/disabled extension | First-year prohibition |
|--------|---------|------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| `CaliforniaEllisAct` | Cal. Govt Code § 7060.4(a)/(b) | **120 days** | **365 days** for tenants ≥ 62 OR disabled AND ≥ 1 year residency | No |
| `Oregon` | ORS 90.427 + ORS 90.323(3) | **90 days** | None | **Yes** — no termination in first 12 months |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.650 | **120 days** | None | No |
| `Default` | — | 0 | — | — |

**California Ellis Act § 7060.4(b) 365-day extension stacked prongs**: tenant must be (≥ 62 OR disabled) AND have ≥ 1 year residency — both prongs required. Under-62 + non-disabled OR < 1 year residency falls back to the 120-day default.

**Oregon's first-year prohibition is regression-critical**: ORS 90.323(3) blocks ANY landlord-cause termination during the first 12 months of a non-week-to-week tenancy, regardless of notice — demolition is no exception. WA and CA have no equivalent floor.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/demolition-tenant-notice`. Twenty-six tests pin: **CA standard tenant 120-day compliant** + § 7060.4(a) citation; **CA 119-day insufficient** + note regression; **CA senior 62+ with 1-yr residency 365-day extension** + § 7060.4(b) citation; **CA disabled tenant with 1-yr residency 365-day extension**; **CA senior without 1-yr residency only 120 days** (regression — both prongs required); **CA under-62 not-disabled only 120 days**; **CA at 62 boundary qualifies** (≥ strict); **CA at 12-month residency boundary qualifies**; **CA senior with 365-day notice compliant**; **CA senior with only 120 days insufficient** (regression target — must give 365); **OR first-year prohibition blocks demolition regardless of notice** + ORS 90.323(3) citation; **OR at 12-month boundary demolition allowed**; **OR 90-day compliant**; **OR 89-day insufficient**; **OR first-year takes precedence over insufficient notice** (regression — same input both violations would fire, first-year wins); **WA 120-day compliant** + RCW 59.18.650 citation; **WA 119-day insufficient** + "demo / substantial-rehab" note; **WA no age extension** (regression — only CA has the extension); **WA no first-year prohibition** (regression — only OR has it); **default no obligation**; **state routing CA/OR/WA/Default**; **case-insensitive lookup**; **only CA has age-disability extension** (single-test 3-regime invariant — same senior + disabled + long-tenancy input only triggers CA extension); **only OR has first-year prohibition** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **notice ordering OR < CA = WA for standard** (3-regime ordering invariant); **citations pin authorities** (§ 7060.4(a) + § 7060.4(b) + ORS 90.427 + RCW 59.18.650).

`traderview-expense::death_in_unit_disclosure` is the **state death-in-unit landlord disclosure compliance check** — addresses the narrow but litigated question of whether a landlord must affirmatively tell prospective tenants that someone died on the premises. The default U.S. position is caveat emptor (no statutory duty), but three jurisdictions carve out distinct affirmative-disclosure regimes with sharply different scope: California has the broadest (3 years, all causes including natural) while South Dakota and Alaska cover only homicides/suicides over 12 months.

**Four regimes** with distinct lookback windows and cause coverage:

| Regime | Statute | Lookback | Covered causes | Direct-inquiry override? |
|--------|---------|----------|----------------|---------------------------|
| `California1710_2` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1710.2(a) | **36 months** | ALL deaths (natural + homicide + suicide + other) | **YES** under § 1710.2(b) — even 50-year-old death must be truthfully disclosed if directly asked |
| `SouthDakota` | S.D. Codified Laws § 43-4-44 | 12 months | Homicide / suicide / felony only — natural deaths NOT covered | No |
| `Alaska` | AS 08.88.615 | 12 months | Homicide / suicide only — agent disclosure not owner disclosure | No |
| `Default` | — | 0 | None statutorily | Common-law fraud may apply |

**California's two unique features**:
1. **§ 1710.2(a)(1) HIV/AIDS carve-out** — landlord may NOT be required to disclose that an occupant was living with HIV or died from AIDS-related complications. Written into law to prevent discrimination.
2. **§ 1710.2(b) intentional-misrepresentation override** — the 3-year window does NOT immunize a landlord who LIES in response to a direct inquiry. The HIV/AIDS carve-out also yields to § 1710.2(b) — affirmative-disclosure-not-required is one thing; lying in response to a direct question is still actionable fraud.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/death-in-unit-disclosure`. Twenty-three tests pin: **CA natural-death within 3 years must disclose** (CA-only — natural causes are covered); **CA at 36-month boundary still within window** (regression — ≤ strict); **CA at 37 months outside window** + "37 months ago" note regression; **CA HIV/AIDS carve-out not disclosable**; **CA direct-inquiry misrep overrides 3-year window** (50-year-old death + lie = actionable under § 1710.2(b)); **CA direct-inquiry truthful response compliant**; **CA HIV/AIDS direct-inquiry lie STILL actionable** (regression — § 1710.2(b) override on top of § 1710.2(a)(1) carve-out); **SD homicide within 12 months must disclose**; **SD natural-death not disclosable** (regression — SD's narrower cause coverage); **SD homicide outside 12 months not disclosable**; **SD at 12-month boundary within window**; **SD no direct-inquiry override** (regression — § 1710.2(b) is CA-only); **AK homicide within 12 months must disclose** + AS 08.88.615 citation; **AK natural-cause not disclosable**; **default regime no obligation** + "caveat emptor" citation; **no-death no-obligation across all 4 regimes** (1-test 4-regime invariant); **CA longest lookback window** (CA 36 > SD 12 = AK 12); **CA broadest cause coverage** (natural causes only covered by CA); **state routing CA/SD/AK/Default** (NY → Default, TX → Default); **state routing case-insensitive**; **CA-only direct-inquiry-misrep override** (single-test 4-regime invariant: only CA returns `direct_inquiry_misrep_exposure: true`); **citations pin authorities** (§ 1710.2(a) + HIV/AIDS + § 43-4-44 + 12-month + 08.88.615 + agent disclosure); **CA required-disclosure compliant when no direct inquiry**.

`traderview-expense::dog_breed_restriction_ban` is the **dog breed-specific restriction (BSL) ban in residential rental housing module** — when may a landlord lawfully refuse to rent to a tenant purely because of the breed of the tenant's dog? Trader-landlord critical for any multifamily rental property considering breed restrictions: Nevada SB 166 (effective October 1, 2025) prohibits insurance underwriting using breed as a factor for landlord liability policies for multifamily residential properties; combined with Nevada's 2013 statewide BSL preemption, landlords generally cannot refuse to rent based on breed alone. Distinct from siblings `pet_fees` (pet deposit caps), `emotional_support_animal_documentation` (ESA framework), `service_animal` (ADA / FHA service-animal protection), `tenant_organizing`, and `fair_chance_housing` (criminal-background screening).

**Two regimes** — Nevada (3-layer protection) vs Default (no protection):

| Regime | Authority | Insurance ban | BSL preemption | Breed-alone denial |
|--------|-----------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Nevada | NV SB 166 (eff. Oct 1, 2025) + NV SB 103 (2021) + NV Rev. Stat. § 202.500 (2013) | **YES** (multifamily) + general (single-family) | **YES** statewide | **UNLAWFUL** |
| Default | No statewide statute; ~22 states have BSL preemption laws varying | NO | NO statewide | Lawful (subject to local) |

**Nevada three-layer protection**:
1. **SB 166 (October 1, 2025)** — insurers MAY NOT use dog breed as a factor when underwriting landlord liability policies for **multi-family residential properties**.
2. **SB 103 (2021)** — insurers MAY NOT deny homeowners or renters coverage based SOLELY on dog breed (broader scope).
3. **NV Rev. Stat. § 202.500 (2013)** — statewide BSL preemption; local governments CANNOT pass laws banning specific breeds (e.g., pit bulls). Nevada was the **14th state** to prohibit BSL by local governments.

Pinned by `nv_breed_alone_denial_unlawful`, `nv_insurance_breed_underwriting_multifamily_violates_sb166`, `nv_insurance_breed_underwriting_single_family_violates_sb103` (single-family falls under SB 103 instead of SB 166), `nv_local_government_bsl_preempted`, `nv_citation_pins_three_authorities`, `nv_note_pins_sb166`, `nv_note_pins_sb103`, `nv_note_pins_2013_bsl_preemption` (14th state + pit bulls), `nv_sb166_multifamily_specific_vs_sb103_general_distinguished` (multifamily → SB 166; single-family → SB 103), and `nv_uniquely_engages_statewide_protection_invariant`.

**Three lawful pathways under Nevada (landlords may still require)**:
1. Pet deposits within security-deposit cap
2. Liability coverage
3. Behavior screenings (history of bites, training, dog temperament evaluation)

But landlords MAY NOT refuse to rent purely because of breed. Pinned by `nv_behavior_screening_lawful`, `nv_declined_liability_coverage_lawful`, `nv_declined_pet_deposit_lawful`, `nv_three_lawful_pathways_unaffected_by_multifamily_status` (3-basis × 2-property-type sweep = all 6 lawful), and `nv_note_pins_lawful_requirements`.

**Default fallback** — no statewide breed discrimination protection; ~22 states have BSL preemption laws at local-government level (varies by state); many states still allow breed-specific bans at city/county level; insurance may still consider breed in most states. Maryland counties and Massachusetts municipalities have enacted breed discrimination bans. FHA does NOT cover breed unless tied to disability service-animal accommodation. Pinned by `default_no_protection_breed_alone_lawful`, `denial_basis_truth_table_default` (all 6 bases lawful at default), `default_citation_pins_no_statewide_statute`, `default_note_pins_22_state_landscape`, and `default_note_pins_fha_carveout`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/dog-breed-restriction-ban`. Twenty-two tests pin: **NV breed alone denial unlawful** (SB 166); **NV behavior screening lawful**; **NV declined liability coverage lawful**; **NV declined pet deposit lawful**; **NV insurance breed underwriting multifamily violates SB 166**; **NV insurance breed underwriting single-family violates SB 103** (broader SB 103); **NV local government BSL preempted** (§ 202.500); **NV citation pins three authorities** (SB 166 + October 1, 2025 + SB 103 + § 202.500 + 2013 BSL preemption); **NV note pins SB 166** (multi-family residential); **NV note pins SB 103** (SOLELY); **NV note pins 2013 BSL preemption** (14th state + pit bulls); **NV note pins lawful requirements** (pet deposits + liability coverage + behavior screenings); **default no protection breed alone lawful**; **default citation pins no statewide statute**; **default note pins 22-state landscape**; **default note pins FHA carve-out** (disability service-animal accommodation); **two regimes routed correctly**; **denial basis truth table NV** (6-cell sweep: 3 unlawful + 3 lawful); **denial basis truth table default** (6-cell sweep: all 6 lawful); **NV uniquely engages statewide protection invariant**; **NV SB 166 multifamily-specific vs SB 103 general distinguished**; **NV three lawful pathways unaffected by multifamily status** (3-basis × 2-property-type sweep).

`traderview-expense::sex_offender_database_notice` is the **state landlord sex-offender-database notice disclosure compliance check (Megan's Law database notice in residential leases)** — California is the ONLY state with a statutory mandate that every residential lease include specific verbatim notice language pointing tenants to the state's Megan's Law database. Other states rely on community notification by law enforcement (the registry itself + AG/prosecutor-driven community alerts) rather than a lease-side disclosure. Rounds out the hazard-disclosure / community-information cluster alongside `military_ordnance_disclosure`, `asbestos_disclosure`, `lead_disclosure`, `mold_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, `flood_disclosure`, `meth_contamination_disclosure`, `bedbug_disclosure`, `fire_sprinkler_disclosure`, and `death_in_unit_disclosure`.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Lease disclosure mandate | Verbatim language required |
|--------|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 2079.10a + Pen. Code § 290.46 | **YES** | **YES** (no paraphrasing) |
| `NewJersey` | N.J.S.A. 2C:7-1 et seq. (RCNL) | No (community notification only) | n/a |
| `Default` | 34 U.S.C. § 20911 et seq. (federal Megan's Law) | No | n/a |

**California § 2079.10a — three independent compliance gates**:

| Gate | Statute | Rule |
|------|---------|------|
| § 2079.10a(a) | Cal. Civ. Code § 2079.10a(a) | Lease must contain Megan's Law database notice — VERBATIM statutory language, no paraphrasing |
| § 2079.10a(b) | Cal. Civ. Code § 2079.10a(b) | Notice must be provided at lease execution / when rental period begins |
| § 2079.10a(c) | Cal. Civ. Code § 2079.10a(c) | Landlord NOT required to provide specific offender names or addresses; tenant searches database directly at www.meganslaw.ca.gov |

The verbatim notice reads: "Notice: Pursuant to Section 290.46 of the Penal Code, information about specified registered sex offenders is made available to the public via an Internet Web site maintained by the Department of Justice at www.meganslaw.ca.gov. Depending on an offender's criminal history, this information will include either the address at which the offender resides or the community of residence and ZIP code in which he or she resides."

**Three independent California violations** — each is a standalone failure:
- Missing notice (§ 2079.10a(a))
- Paraphrased instead of verbatim language (§ 2079.10a(a))
- Notice not at lease execution (§ 2079.10a(b))

Pinned by `california_missing_notice_violation`, `california_paraphrased_notice_violation`, `california_notice_after_execution_violation`, and the 3-cell truth-table invariant `ca_three_independent_violations_invariant`.

**§ 2079.10a(c) — landlord has NO statutory duty to investigate the registry or affirmatively warn tenants about specific registered offenders.** Affirmative denial or cancellation of tenancy based on registry status alone may trigger FHA + state anti-discrimination scrutiny. Pinned by `california_landlord_no_specific_offender_info_required_note` and `california_no_duty_to_investigate_note` (UX-text regressions).

**Common-law fraudulent-concealment liability applies universally across regimes.** Where the landlord ACTIVELY misrepresents the absence of registered offenders (e.g., affirmatively states "there are no offenders in this neighborhood" when the landlord knows otherwise), common-law fraud liability attaches regardless of state lease-disclosure regime. Pinned by `active_misrepresentation_triggers_common_law_fraud_default`, `active_misrepresentation_triggers_fraud_even_in_california`, `active_misrepresentation_triggers_fraud_even_in_new_jersey`, and the universal invariant `active_misrepresentation_triggers_fraud_universally_invariant`.

**Community notification framework exists across ALL regimes.** Every regime sits within at least the federal Megan's Law framework (34 U.S.C. § 20911 et seq., formerly 42 U.S.C. § 14071). The federal framework requires states to maintain public registries; what differs is whether the state ALSO imposes a lease-side disclosure mandate. Pinned by `community_notification_framework_exists_across_all_regimes_invariant`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/sex-offender-database-notice`. Twenty-one tests pin: **CA verbatim notice at execution compliant + missing notice violation + paraphrased notice violation + notice after execution violation + no specific offender info required note + no duty to investigate note**; **NJ no lease disclosure mandate + compliant without notice**; **Default no state-specific mandate + compliant without lease notice**; **active misrepresentation triggers common-law fraud (Default + CA + NJ)**; **no misrepresentation no common-law fraud**; **only-California-imposes-lease-disclosure-mandate 3-regime invariant**; **only-California-requires-verbatim-language 3-regime invariant**; **community notification framework exists across all 3 regimes invariant**; **active misrepresentation triggers fraud universally 3-regime invariant**; **CA three independent violations 3-cell invariant** (missing/paraphrased/timing); **citation pins authority per regime** (§ 2079.10a + § 290.46 for CA; N.J.S.A. 2C:7-1 for NJ; 34 U.S.C. § 20911 for Default); **sibling-module note across all 3 regimes**.

`traderview-expense::short_term_rental_conversion` is the **short-term rental (STR) conversion restriction compliance module** — when may a trader-landlord lawfully convert a long-term residential rental unit to a short-term rental (Airbnb / VRBO / Booking.com listing under 30 nights) without violating local registration ordinances? Trader-landlord critical for any rental owner in NYC + SF + LA considering STR conversion: unregistered hosting exposes owner to per-violation penalties + booking-platform verification refusal + ordinance enforcement. Distinct from siblings `rental_property_registration` (general landlord registration), `condominium_conversion_protection` (condo conversion), `tenant_relocation_assistance` (relocation), and `just_cause_eviction` (eviction).

**Four regimes** — three city-specific frameworks vs Default:

| Regime | Authority | Registration | Primary residence | Unhosted cap | Max penalty |
|--------|-----------|--------------|-------------------|--------------|--------------|
| NYC | Local Law 18 of 2022 (eff. Sep 5, 2023) | OSE | Permanent occupant + **host present during stay** | **0** (no unhosted allowed) | **$5,000** per violation |
| SF | Admin. Code Chapter 41A (Ord. 218-14) | Business Registration Certificate | **270 nights/year occupancy** | **90 nights/year** | **$1,000** per day |
| LA | LAMC § 12.22 A.32 (Home-Sharing Ordinance eff. Nov 1, 2019) | LADBS Home-Sharing Program | Yes | **120/year** default; **240/year** with Extended permit | LAMC enforcement + recordation |
| Default | Locality-controlled | None statewide | None | N/A | N/A |

**NYC Local Law 18 host-present model** — NYC uniquely requires the host to be the permanent occupant AND PRESENT during the stay (host-and-guest model only); unhosted stays under 30 nights are unlawful. Booking platforms (Airbnb, VRBO, Booking.com) must verify valid OSE registration before processing reservations. Penalties up to $5,000 per violation for both hosts AND platforms. Pinned by `nyc_registered_host_present_lawful`, `nyc_unregistered_unlawful`, `nyc_host_not_present_unlawful`, `nyc_stay_over_30_nights_no_registration_required` (≥30 nights = long-term rental, not subject to Local Law 18), `nyc_max_penalty_5000_per_violation`, `nyc_uniquely_requires_host_present_invariant` (NYC host-presence requirement absent in SF/LA), and `nyc_citation_pins_local_law_18`.

**SF Chapter 41A primary-residence 270-night occupancy + 90-night unhosted cap** — host must register and obtain Business Registration Certificate; primary residence requirement is 270 nights/year occupancy; cap of 90 unhosted nights per year; penalties up to $1,000 per day for unregistered hosting. Pinned by `sf_registered_within_caps_lawful`, `sf_unregistered_unlawful`, `sf_below_270_night_primary_residence_violates` (269 fails), `sf_at_270_night_boundary_compliant` (exactly 270 compliant), `sf_over_90_unhosted_nights_violates` (91 violates), `sf_at_90_unhosted_nights_boundary_compliant` (exactly 90 compliant), `sf_max_penalty_1000_per_day`, `sf_uniquely_requires_270_night_occupancy_invariant`, and `sf_citation_pins_chapter_41a`.

**LA § 12.22 A.32 120-day default vs 240-day Extended Home-Sharing permit** — host must register with LADBS Home-Sharing Program; cap of 120 days/year unhosted by default; cap extended to 240 days/year with Extended Home-Sharing permit. Pinned by `la_registered_within_default_cap_lawful`, `la_unregistered_unlawful`, `la_at_120_default_boundary_compliant` (exactly 120 compliant), `la_121_default_violates`, `la_extended_permit_240_day_cap` (240 days OK with permit), `la_extended_permit_241_violates`, `la_not_primary_residence_violates`, `la_extended_permit_doubles_unhosted_cap_invariant` (default 120; extended 240), and `la_citation_pins_lamc_12_22_a_32`.

**Default locality-controlled fallback** — most states do not regulate STR conversion at state level; some states have preempted local STR regulation (FL + AZ + partial TX); some have permitted it (NY + CA + CO). Pinned by `default_no_regulation_lawful`, `default_unregistered_still_lawful`, and `default_citation_pins_locality_preemption`.

**Regime unhosted cap truth table** — NYC: 0 (none allowed unhosted); SF: 90/year; LA: 120/year default + 240/year extended; Default: 0 (no cap). Pinned by `regime_unhosted_cap_truth_table` (4-regime sweep).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/short-term-rental-conversion`.
