Thirty tests pin: **NYC registered host present lawful**; **NYC unregistered unlawful** (Local Law 18); **NYC host not present unlawful**; **NYC stay over 30 nights no registration required**; **NYC max penalty $5,000 per violation**; **SF registered within caps lawful**; **SF unregistered unlawful** (Chapter 41A); **SF below 270-night primary residence violates** (269); **SF at 270-night boundary compliant** (270); **SF over 90 unhosted nights violates** (91); **SF at 90 unhosted nights boundary compliant** (90); **SF max penalty $1,000 per day**; **LA registered within default cap lawful**; **LA unregistered unlawful** (§ 12.22 A.32); **LA at 120 default boundary compliant**; **LA 121 default violates**; **LA Extended Permit 240-day cap**; **LA Extended Permit 241 violates**; **LA not primary residence violates**; **default no regulation lawful**; **default unregistered still lawful**; **NYC citation pins Local Law 18**; **SF citation pins Chapter 41A**; **LA citation pins LAMC § 12.22 A.32**; **default citation pins locality preemption** (FL/AZ preempt; NY/CA/CO permit); **four regimes routed correctly**; **NYC uniquely requires host present invariant**; **SF uniquely requires 270-night occupancy invariant**; **LA Extended Permit doubles unhosted cap invariant** (120 → 240); **regime unhosted cap truth table** (4-regime sweep: NYC=0 / SF=90 / LA=120 / Default=0).

`traderview-expense::military_ordnance_disclosure` is the **state landlord former-federal-or-state-ordnance-location disclosure compliance check** — specialized hazard-disclosure topic prompted by the December 10, 1983 Tierra Santa tragedy in San Diego where a live munition exploded in a residential area that was formerly a U.S. military ordnance location, killing two residents. California responded with Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7, the only statute of its kind. Rounds out the hazard-disclosure cluster alongside `asbestos_disclosure`, `lead_disclosure`, `mold_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, `flood_disclosure`, `meth_contamination_disclosure`, `bedbug_disclosure`, `fire_sprinkler_disclosure`, and `death_in_unit_disclosure`.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Disclosure mandate | Trigger |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|---------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7 | **YES** | Actual knowledge + within 1 mile |
| `FederalMMRP` | 10 U.S.C. § 2710 + § 2701 (Defense Environmental Restoration Program / FUDS database) | No (public information only) | n/a |
| `Default` | None | No (common-law latent-defect may apply) | n/a |

**Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7 — actual-knowledge AND within-1-mile**:

| Subsection | Statute | Rule |
|------------|---------|------|
| § 1940.7(a) | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7(a) | **Legislative findings** — Tierra Santa tragedy of December 10, 1983 |
| § 1940.7(b) | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7(b) | **Prospective tenant** — written notice prior to execution of rental agreement |
| § 1940.7(c) | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7(c) | **Grandfathered tenancies** in existence on January 1, 1990 — notice as soon as practicable |
| § 1940.7(d)(1) | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7(d)(1) | **"Former federal or state ordnance location"** — area identified by federal or state government as once used for military training and may contain potentially explosive munitions |
| § 1940.7(d)(2) | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.7(d)(2) | **"Neighborhood area"** — within ONE MILE of the residential dwelling |

**The disclosure trigger requires BOTH actual knowledge AND within-1-mile.** A 4-cell truth table:

| Actual knowledge | Within 1 mile | Trigger fired |
|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| No | No | No |
| No | Yes | No |
| Yes | No | No |
| **Yes** | **Yes** | **YES** |

Pinned by the invariant `disclosure_trigger_requires_actual_knowledge_AND_within_1_mile_invariant` (4 cells).

**Constructive knowledge is NOT enough.** § 1940.7 imposes the disclosure obligation only where the landlord has ACTUAL KNOWLEDGE of the former ordnance location. Constructive knowledge, duty to investigate, or imputed knowledge does not trigger the statute. Pinned by `california_no_actual_knowledge_trigger_not_fired` and the UX-text note `california_constructive_knowledge_not_triggered_note`.

**Locations beyond 1 mile fall outside the neighborhood area.** § 1940.7(d)(2) precisely defines "neighborhood area" as within ONE MILE of the residential dwelling. A former ordnance site 1.1 miles away — even with landlord's actual knowledge — does not trigger the duty. Pinned by `california_actual_knowledge_but_location_beyond_1_mile_no_trigger`.

**Federal FUDS database is public but creates no landlord disclosure duty.** The DoD/Army Corps of Engineers maintains a Formerly Used Defense Sites inventory under 10 U.S.C. § 2710. It's public information that informs the California actual-knowledge analysis but does not impose an independent federal mandate on landlords. Pinned by `federal_mmrp_no_landlord_disclosure_mandate`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/military-ordnance-disclosure`. Eighteen tests pin: **CA actual knowledge within 1 mile with notice compliant**; **CA actual knowledge no notice violation** (§ 1940.7(b)); **CA no actual knowledge trigger not fired** (compliant with no duty); **CA actual knowledge but beyond 1 mile no trigger**; **CA grandfathered tenant practicable notice compliant** (§ 1940.7(c)); **CA grandfathered tenant no practicable notice violation**; **federal MMRP no landlord disclosure mandate**; **Default no statutory mandate**; **Default even with actual knowledge no violation**; **CA definitional notes present** (§ 1940.7(d)(1) + (d)(2)); **CA constructive knowledge not triggered UX-text note**; **only-California-mandates-disclosure 3-regime invariant**; **disclosure trigger requires actual knowledge AND within 1 mile 4-cell invariant**; **non-California regimes never trigger invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime**; **sibling-module note across all 3 regimes** (UX-text regression for hazard-disclosure cluster); **neighborhood radius constant 1 mile invariant**; **legislative findings cite Tierra Santa in California path** (UX-text regression).

`traderview-expense::meth_contamination_disclosure` is the **state methamphetamine-contamination landlord disclosure compliance check** — sits adjacent to `lead_disclosure`, `mold_disclosure`, `bedbug_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, and `flood_disclosure` in the suite of substance-/hazard-specific disclosure tables. Three states ship statutory regimes with explicit ug/100cm² remediation standards; the rest rely on the federal FHA material-defect doctrine. The defining axis between the regulated regimes is **whether remediation extinguishes the disclosure obligation** (yes in CO + AZ; **no** in MT, the regression-critical distinguisher).

**Four regimes** with distinct contamination thresholds and post-remediation obligation extinguishment:

| Regime | Statute | Remediation standard | Post-remediation extinguishes disclosure? | Unique feature |
|--------|---------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------|----------------|
| `Arizona` | A.R.S. § 32-1166.04 | **0.1 ug/100cm²** (STRICTEST) | Yes | Entry by non-owners barred until state-standard cleaning |
| `Colorado` | Colo. Rev. Stat. § 38-35.7-103 + 6 CCR 1014-3 | 0.5 ug/100cm² (4.0 attics/crawl + 1.5 encapsulated) | Yes | CDPHE-certified remediation extinguishes disclosure |
| `Montana` | Mont. Code Ann. § 75-10-1301 et seq. | 1.5 ug/100cm² | **NO** | Landlord MUST disclose knowledge + remediation status even after cleanup |
| `Default` | 42 U.S.C. § 3604 (FHA) | — | NO (general fraud + FHA material-defect doctrine) | No statewide statute identified |

Strict ordering of standards: AZ (0.1) < CO (0.5) < MT (1.5). The integer field `remediation_standard_ug_per_100cm2_x10` is stored ×10 to avoid float math (1, 5, 15).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/meth-contamination-disclosure`. Twenty tests pin: **CO no-knowledge no-obligation**; **CO knowledge unremediated undisclosed → violation** + 0.5 ug/100cm² citation regression; **CO knowledge unremediated disclosed → compliant**; **CO knowledge remediated → disclosure extinguished + "extinguished" note regression**; **AZ strictest standard 0.1 ug/100cm²** + "STRICTEST" + "entry by non-owners barred" citation regression; **AZ post-remediation extinguishes**; **MT remediation does NOT extinguish disclosure** (regression-critical distinguisher — Montana's distinct rule); **MT post-remediation with disclosure compliant**; **MT standard 1.5 ug/100cm²**; **MT no-knowledge no-obligation**; **default no-statewide-statute falls back to FHA** + "3604" + "FHA" citation regression; **default no-knowledge no-disclosure**; **state routing CO/AZ/MT/Default** (TX → Default, CA → Default); **state routing case-insensitive**; **standards strict ordering AZ < CO < MT** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **extinguishment invariants** (CO + AZ yes; MT + Default no); **citations pin authorities** (§ 38-35.7-103 + 6 CCR 1014-3 + § 32-1166.04 + § 75-10-1301 + "does NOT extinguish"); **CO remediation note mentions extinguished**; **MT unremediated violation note mentions material defect**; **AZ-only entry-restriction in citation** (regression — entry-restriction is AZ-only, not in CO or MT).

`traderview-expense::prevailing_party_attorney_fees` is the **state prevailing-party attorney-fees lease-clause reciprocity compliance check** — addresses a common landlord drafting trap: lease clauses that award attorney fees only to the LANDLORD on default are often unenforceable as written in states with a reciprocity statute. The clause is transformed by operation of law into a bilateral right, so a prevailing TENANT collects the landlord's attorney fees even though the clause never said so. Distinct from `late_fee_caps` (penalty on tenant late payments) and `lockout_penalties` (penalty for landlord self-help).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Reciprocity? | Waiver | Effective date |
|--------|---------|---------------|--------|-----------------|
| `CaliforniaCivCode1717` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1717(a) | **Yes** — one-way fee clauses construed as mutual | § 1717 is fundamental CA policy; choice-of-law clauses overridden | All leases |
| `WashingtonRcw484330` | RCW 4.84.330 | **Yes** — same transformation | **PROHIBITED** — any waiver clause is VOID | Leases entered AFTER 1977-09-21 |
| `Default` | — | **No** — American Rule | — | One-way clauses enforced as written |

**The reciprocity rule** (CA + WA): a lease that says "Landlord may recover attorney fees if Landlord prevails" is automatically read as "the PREVAILING PARTY may recover attorney fees." Tenant beats landlord at trial → tenant collects fees from landlord.

**Washington's 1977 effective date is the regression-critical pin**: a one-way-landlord clause in a 1976 lease that goes to trial today is enforced as written under common law (tenant gets nothing); the same clause in a 1978 lease is reformed by RCW 4.84.330 (tenant gets fees). The post-1977 reciprocity / pre-1977 grandfathered split is a real boundary.

**Default-regime trap**: in states without a reciprocity statute, a landlord who drafts a one-way-landlord fee clause and LOSES at trial pays his own fees but does not get the tenant's. Landlord-favorable drafting backfires only via the lost-case path; if landlord wins the clause works as intended.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/prevailing-party-attorney-fees`. Twenty tests pin: **no clause American Rule** + "American Rule" citation; **CA one-way landlord clause + tenant prevails → fees** + § 1717(a) + "MUTUAL" citation regression (classic § 1717 case); **CA one-way landlord + landlord prevails → fees** (clause works as intended); **CA bilateral clause prevailing party fees**; **WA post-1977 one-way landlord + tenant prevails → fees** + RCW 4.84.330 + "waiver PROHIBITED" citation; **WA pre-1977 lease no reciprocity** (regression — 1977 boundary excludes); **WA pre-1977 one-way + landlord prevails still gets fees** (clause as-written enforces under common law); **WA post-1977 one-way-tenant + landlord prevails → reciprocity flips to give landlord fees** (symmetric regression); **WA bilateral post-1977 compliant**; **default one-way-landlord + tenant prevails → NO fees** + "reciprocity does NOT apply" note (American Rule outcome); **default one-way-landlord + landlord prevails → fees** (clause as written); **default bilateral works**; **state routing CA/WA/Default**; **case-insensitive routing**; **CA no pre-1977 carve-out** (regression — only WA has it, CA applies regardless); **WA pre-1977 vs post-1977 paradox split** (same input → opposite results regression-critical); **only CA + WA have reciprocity** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **citations pin authorities**; **negative fees clamped**; **CA one-way-tenant + landlord prevails → fees** (symmetric reciprocity test — flips in both directions).

`traderview-expense::damage_deduction_itemization` is the **state security-deposit damage-deduction itemization, receipt-attachment, photographic-documentation, and depreciation-method compliance check** — sibling to `security_deposit_caps` (how much), `deposit_return_windows` (when), `deposit_interest` (accrual), `pre_move_out_inspection` (cure-period inspection), and `carpet_replacement_useful_life` (specific finite-life category). This module handles the **ITEMIZATION half** of the move-out accounting: the form, content, and method of the damage-deduction notice that accompanies the security-deposit return.

**Five regimes diverge along five axes**:

| Regime | Statute | Deadline | Receipt threshold | Photographic mandate | Depreciation required | Bad-faith penalty |
|--------|---------|----------|-------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5(g) + AB 2801 (Stats. 2024 ch. 444) | **21 days** | **$125** | **Yes** (eff. 2025-04-01 repair/cleaning; 2025-07-01 pre-tenancy) | Yes | § 1950.5(l) up to **2× withheld** |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.280 | 30 days | None | No | Yes (case law) | Up to **2× deposit** intentional refusal (discretionary) |
| `Oregon` | ORS 90.300(13) | 31 days | None | No | **Yes** (statutory) | Up to **2× wrongfully withheld** |
| `Florida` | Fla. Stat. § 83.49(3) | 30 days | **$200** | No | Yes | Court costs + attorney fees (no statutory multiplier) |
| `Texas` | Tex. Prop. Code § 92.103 + § 92.104(c) + § 92.109 | 30 days | None | No | **No** (statutory) | **$100 + 3× retained** + attorney fees — **steepest** |

**California AB 2801 is the regime-defining 2025 expansion.** Stats. 2024 ch. 444, signed Sept 2024, amended § 1950.5 to require landlords to take photographs of the unit (a) within a reasonable time after possession is returned and before any repair/cleaning, (b) within a reasonable time after any repair/cleaning, and (c) immediately before or at the beginning of any tenancy commencing on or after 2025-07-01. Photographs must be retained for at least four years and provided to the tenant on request. Failure to take the required photographs is itself a violation independent of any other defect. Pinned by `california_ab2801_no_photographs_violation`, the `only_california_imposes_ab2801_photographic_mandate_5_regime_invariant`, and `california_ab2801_pretenancy_photograph_note_present` (UX-text regression).

**Texas has the steepest bad-faith penalty.** § 92.109(a) imposes $100 + 3× the amount of the deposit wrongfully retained + reasonable attorney fees on a landlord who acts in bad faith — versus California / Washington / Oregon's 2× multiplier and Florida's no-multiplier fee-shifting. At $1000 withheld in bad faith, Texas exposure is $3100; CA / WA / OR exposure is $2000; Florida exposure is $0 (statutory) plus court costs and attorney fees. Pinned by `texas_bad_faith_steepest_penalty_100_plus_3x` and the 5-regime invariant `texas_has_steepest_bad_faith_penalty_5_regime_invariant`.

**Texas is the only state in this matrix without a statutory depreciation mandate for finite-useful-life items.** § 92.104 prohibits charges for normal wear and tear and requires an itemized list, but does not statutorily compel the landlord to depreciate the replacement cost of a finite-life item like paint or carpet. California, Washington, Oregon, and Florida all require depreciation either by statute or by binding case law. Pinned by `texas_no_depreciation_not_a_statutory_violation` and the 5-regime invariant `only_texas_lacks_statutory_depreciation_requirement_5_regime_invariant`.

**California's 21-day deadline is the shortest in the matrix.** WA + FL + TX are 30 days; OR is 31 days; CA is 21. The compressed timeline means California landlords have nine fewer days than the federal-median state to complete the itemized accounting plus AB 2801 photographic deliverable plus receipt-attachment review for any line item above $125. Pinned by the 5-regime invariant `only_california_has_21_day_shortest_deadline_5_regime_invariant`.

**Ordinary-wear-and-tear exclusion is universal.** All five regimes statutorily prohibit deductions for wear resulting from ordinary use of the premises — paint fade, minor scuffs, carpet matting in traffic paths, etc. Pinned by `wear_and_tear_excluded_across_all_regimes_invariant` (single failure point covers all 5 regimes).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/damage-deduction-itemization`. Thirty tests pin: **CA full compliance**; **CA past 21-day deadline violation**; **CA at 21-day boundary compliant** (≤); **CA $125 threshold no receipts violation**; **CA at $125 threshold no receipts compliant** (NOT above); **CA AB 2801 no photographs violation**; **CA bad-faith 2× withheld amount**; **WA full compliance**; **WA past 30-day deadline violation**; **WA at 30-day boundary compliant**; **WA no receipt threshold no violation on large deduction** (regression — WA has no statutory threshold); **WA bad-faith 2× deposit exposure**; **OR full compliance**; **OR no depreciation violation** (regression — ORS 90.300(13) statutory mandate); **FL full compliance**; **FL $200 threshold no receipts violation**; **FL bad-faith no statutory multiplier only fees** (regression — § 83.49(3)(c) doesn't multiply); **TX full compliance**; **TX no depreciation not a statutory violation** (regression — TX is the ONLY regime in this matrix without statutory depreciation mandate); **TX bad-faith steepest penalty $100 + 3×**; **wear-and-tear excluded across all 5 regimes invariant**; **only-CA-imposes-AB2801-photographic-mandate 5-regime invariant**; **only-TX-lacks-statutory-depreciation-requirement 5-regime invariant**; **only-CA-has-21-day-shortest-deadline 5-regime invariant**; **only-CA-and-FL-have-receipt-thresholds 5-regime invariant**; **TX-has-steepest-bad-faith-penalty 5-regime invariant** (at $1000 withheld: TX = $3100 vs CA/WA/OR = $2000 vs FL = $0); **no-bad-faith-zero-penalty across all 5 regimes invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (§ 1950.5(g)(2) for CA, RCW 59.18.280 for WA, ORS 90.300(13) for OR, § 83.49(3) for FL, § 92.109 for TX); **note pairs module with sibling modules** (deposit_return_windows + security_deposit_caps + deposit_interest + pre_move_out_inspection — UX-text regression); **CA AB 2801 pre-tenancy photograph note present** (UX-text regression with 2025-07-01 effective date).

`traderview-expense::pre_move_out_inspection` is the **state pre-move-out inspection landlord compliance check** — California is currently the only state with a statutory pre-move-out inspection regime under Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5(f), but it imposes substantial procedural obligations that materially affect security-deposit disputes via a unique waiver mechanic. Distinct from `move_in_inspection` (START-of-tenancy condition documentation) and from `abandoned_property_handling` (belongings left behind after vacating).

**Two regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Notice required | Earliest inspection date | Itemized statement | § 1950.5(f)(4) waiver of undisclosed damages |
|--------|---------|------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `CaliforniaCivCode19505F` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5(f) | **Yes** — written notice of right to request inspection + be present | **No earlier than 14 days (2 weeks)** before termination | **Required** | **YES** — if premises were clear at inspection, landlord SHALL NOT deduct for items not on itemized statement |
| `Default` | — | No | — | — | No |

**The § 1950.5(f)(4) waiver mechanic is regression-critical**: if (a) landlord conducted the pre-move-out inspection AND (b) tenant possessions did NOT prevent landlord from identifying repairs/cleanings during the inspection, the landlord CANNOT use the security deposit to deduct for items NOT identified in the itemized statement at move-out. This effectively converts the optional pre-move-out inspection into a waiver-of-undisclosed-damages event for the landlord. Result: if premises were clear, landlord must catch all the damages during the pre-move-out walk-through or lose the right to deduct.

**Notice obligation independent of tenant request**: § 1950.5(f)(1) requires landlord to NOTIFY tenant of the right to request inspection regardless of whether tenant requests one. Skipping the notice itself is a violation.

**Cure period**: tenant has from inspection date until termination to remedy identified deficiencies. This is the practical purpose of the regime — tenant gets a chance to fix issues to avoid deposit deductions.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/pre-move-out-inspection`. Twenty tests pin: **CA missing notice of right violation** + § 1950.5(f)(1) citation; **CA notice provided no request compliant**; **CA inspection 15 days before termination too early** + § 1950.5(f)(2) + "15 days" note; **CA at 14-day boundary compliant** (≤ strict); **CA inspection 5 days compliant**; **CA missing itemized statement violation** + § 1950.5(f)(3); **CA premises clear with undisclosed deduction waiver violation** + § 1950.5(f)(4) (regression-critical waiver mechanic); **CA premises blocked no waiver — undisclosed deduction allowed** (regression — blocking possessions defeat the waiver); **CA full compliance**; **default no obligation** + "No statewide pre-move-out inspection statute" citation; **default landlord can deduct undisclosed no waiver** (regression — default regime has no waiver); **state routing CA/Default + case-insensitive**; **CA notice requirement does NOT depend on tenant request** (regression — landlord must notify regardless); **CA one day above 14 too early** off-by-one; **CA zero days same-day compliant** (statute only caps EARLIEST not LATEST); **CA waiver only when premises clear + itemized provided** (3-condition gate regression); **only-CA-has-pre-move-out-inspection-obligation** 2-regime invariant; **citations pin authorities** (§ 1950.5(f)(1) + (f)(2) + (f)(3) + (f)(4)); **CA no inspection no waiver even premises clear** (regression-critical — if tenant never requested + no inspection conducted, the § 1950.5(f)(4) waiver does not apply and landlord retains full back-end deduction rights).

`traderview-expense::written_lease_requirement` is the **state written-vs-oral lease requirement compliance check** — universal U.S. floor under the Statute of Frauds: leases of real property for a term EXCEEDING ONE YEAR must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged; leases of ONE YEAR OR LESS may be oral and remain enforceable. State variations add consequences for oral leases that exceed the threshold (Illinois year-to-year conversion) and provide part-performance escape valves. Sibling to `plain_language_lease` (content requirement for written leases) and `lease_translation` (non-English-language translation requirement).

**Five regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Oral lease > 1 year consequence |
|--------|-----------|-----------------------------------|
| `NewYork` | N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 5-703 | Unenforceable beyond 1 year; part-performance exception |
| `Illinois` | 740 ILCS 80/2 + case-law conversion | **UNIQUE** — converts to year-to-year tenancy terminable on 60-day written notice |
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1624(a)(3) + § 1971 | Unenforceable beyond 1 year |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.230 + RCW 64.04.010 | Unenforceable beyond 1 year |
| `Default` | UCC § 2A-201 + state common law | Unenforceable beyond 1 year; part-performance exception varies |

**Universal one-year threshold (365 days) is the boundary.** Statute reads "exceeding 1 year" — exactly 365 days = oral enforceable; 366 days = writing required. Pinned by `lease_365_days_at_boundary_oral_enforceable`, `lease_366_days_above_boundary_requires_writing`, and the 4-cell boundary invariant `one_year_threshold_strict_boundary_invariant` (1/365/366/730 days).

**Illinois is the only regime that converts oral > 1-year leases into enforceable year-to-year tenancies.** Other regimes leave such leases entirely unenforceable beyond the first year (subject to part-performance exception). The IL conversion preserves the tenancy as a year-to-year arrangement terminable on 60-day written notice — protecting the tenant from sudden ejectment while preserving the original landlord-tenant common-law framework. Pinned by `illinois_oral_2_year_lease_converts_to_year_to_year`, `illinois_oral_6_month_lease_no_conversion`, `illinois_written_2_year_lease_no_conversion`, and the 5-regime invariant `only_illinois_converts_oral_to_year_to_year_invariant`.

**Part-performance exception engages only when (a) lease oral AND (b) exceeds 1 year.** Part performance + ≤ 1-year lease = no-op (the lease is already enforceable orally). Part performance + written lease = no-op (the lease is enforceable by writing alone). Only the (oral + > 1 year) intersection invokes the part-performance escape valve. Pinned by `part_performance_engages_for_oral_lease_exceeding_1_year`, `no_part_performance_oral_lease_exceeding_1_year_unenforceable`, `part_performance_irrelevant_when_lease_within_one_year`, and the 4-cell truth-table invariant `part_performance_only_engages_when_oral_and_exceeding_one_year_invariant`.

**Universal invariants pinned across all 5 regimes**:
- `written_lease_always_enforceable_regardless_of_term_invariant` (4 term levels × 5 regimes = 20 cells)
- `oral_lease_within_one_year_always_enforceable_invariant` (4 term levels × 5 regimes = 20 cells)
- `oral_lease_above_one_year_unenforceable_unless_il_or_part_performance_invariant` (3 term levels × 4 non-IL regimes + IL always-enforceable)

**NY GBL § 5-702 Plain Language Law is a separate content requirement.** The written-vs-oral threshold (§ 5-703) is distinct from the plain-language content requirement (§ 5-702 — covered by `plain_language_lease` module). A lease can be in writing yet fail plain-language scrutiny. Pinned by `new_york_plain_language_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/written-lease-requirement`. Twenty-four tests pin: **lease 30 days oral enforceable under all regimes**; **lease 365 days at boundary oral enforceable**; **lease 366 days above boundary requires writing**; **lease 2 year written enforceable**; **Illinois oral 2-year lease converts to year-to-year** + 60-day termination notice; **Illinois oral 6-month no conversion**; **Illinois written 2-year no conversion**; **part-performance engages for oral lease exceeding 1 year**; **no part-performance oral > 1 year unenforceable**; **part-performance irrelevant when lease within 1 year**; **NY plain-language note present**; **California 2-year oral unenforceable**; **California oral within year enforceable**; **Washington oral lease within year authorized** (RCW 59.18.230); **Washington oral 2-year unenforceable**; **Default one-year rule**; **one-year threshold strict boundary 4-cell invariant** (1/365/366/730 days); **only-Illinois-converts-oral-to-year-to-year 5-regime invariant**; **written lease always enforceable regardless of term 20-cell invariant** (5 regimes × 4 term levels); **oral lease within 1 year always enforceable 20-cell invariant**; **oral lease above 1 year unenforceable unless IL or part performance 12-cell + 3-IL invariant**; **part-performance only engages when oral and exceeding 1 year 4-cell truth-table invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (§ 5-703 for NY; 740 ILCS 80/2 for IL; § 1624(a)(3) for CA; RCW 59.18.230 for WA; UCC § 2A-201 for Default); **sibling-module note across all 5 regimes** (UX-text regression for plain_language_lease + lease_translation pairing).

`traderview-expense::holdover_tenant_damages` is the **holdover tenant landlord-damages calculation module** — what a landlord may recover when a tenant remains in possession after lease expiration. Two structurally distinct regimes operate across U.S. landlord-tenant law: **(1) STATUTORY DOUBLE RENT** recovery imposes a multiplier on the holdover damages (Florida — Fla. Stat. § 83.58); **(2) RENT-ACCEPTANCE month-to-month conversion** automatically renews the tenancy on the same terms when the landlord accepts post-expiration rent (California — Cal. Civ. Code § 1945; New York — N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 232-c). Default common-law (Restatement (Second) of Property § 14.5) gives the landlord an election between treating the holdover as a new tenancy or suing for use-and-occupancy damages.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Damages multiplier | Rent-acceptance effect |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Florida | Fla. Stat. § 83.58 | **2×** rent for holdover period | Split authority on waiver — document carefully |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1945 | 1× rent (actual damages) | Rebuttable presumption of month-to-month renewal on same terms |
| New York | N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 232-c | 1× rent (use-and-occupancy) | Mandatory month-to-month conversion from day after expiration |
| Default | Restatement (2d) of Property § 14.5 | 1× rent (actual damages) | Landlord election between new-tenancy and trespass-damages theories |

**Florida § 83.58 partial-month rule.** "The landlord may recover DOUBLE the amount of rent due ... for the period during which the tenant refuses to surrender possession" — the "any part thereof" clause means a partial month counts as a full period. 1 day of holdover triggers a full month's double rent. Pinned by `florida_1_day_partial_month_full_charge_per_any_part_thereof`, `florida_29_days_one_month_boundary`, `florida_30_day_holdover_full_month_double_rent`, `florida_31_day_holdover_rounds_up_to_2_months_double_rent`, `florida_60_day_holdover_2_months_double_rent`, and `florida_365_day_extreme_holdover_double_rent` (13 months × 2×).

**California § 1945 rebuttable presumption.** Rent acceptance creates a rebuttable presumption of month-to-month renewal on the same terms — landlord must then serve fresh 30/60/90-day notice under Cal. Civ. Code § 1946 / § 1946.1 to terminate. Attempting to maintain eviction on the original notice after accepting rent is a violation; the acceptance waives the original demand. Pinned by `california_rent_accepted_creates_month_to_month`, `california_no_rent_accepted_no_month_to_month`, and `california_rent_accepted_plus_eviction_attempt_violation`.

**New York § 232-c mandatory conversion.** Statute states: "If the landlord shall accept rent for any period subsequent to the expiration of such term, then ... the tenancy created by the acceptance of such rent shall be a tenancy from month to month commencing on the first day after the expiration of such term." Stricter than CA — conversion is mandatory, not rebuttable. Pinned by `new_york_rent_accepted_creates_month_to_month`, `new_york_no_rent_accepted_no_month_to_month`, and `new_york_rent_accepted_plus_holdover_proceeding_violation`.

**Default common-law landlord election.** Restatement (Second) of Property § 14.5 — landlord may (a) treat the holdover as a new tenancy at the same rent OR (b) sue the tenant for use-and-occupancy / trespass damages equal to the actual rental value. No statutory multiplier and no automatic conversion regardless of rent acceptance. Pinned by `default_regime_single_rent_recovery` and `default_regime_no_month_to_month_auto_creation`.

**Multi-regime invariants** — `florida_is_the_only_2x_multiplier_regime_invariant` confirms FL is the unique double-rent state (CA/NY/Default all 1×); `rent_acceptance_creates_month_to_month_only_for_ca_and_ny_invariant` confirms automatic conversion engages only in CA + NY (4-regime sweep, 2 hits); `same_input_florida_double_vs_others_single_damages_invariant` pins FL at exactly 2× the other three for identical input; `zero_days_zero_damages_all_regimes_invariant` confirms no recovery without holdover across all 4 regimes; `single_state_uniqueness_invariant` produces a 4-cell truth table of `(multiplier, mtm_created)` showing each regime is structurally distinct.

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references the structural split between **statutory-double-rent** (FL) and **rent-acceptance** (CA + NY) regimes, with **default common-law election** (Restatement § 14.5) as the residual. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/holdover-tenant-damages`. Twenty-five tests pin: **Florida 30-day holdover full month double rent**; **Florida 31-day rounds up to 2 months double rent**; **Florida 1-day partial month full charge** ("any part thereof"); **Florida 60-day 2 months double rent**; **Florida zero days zero damages**; **Florida 365-day extreme holdover** (13 months × 2×); **Florida rent acceptance does not create month-to-month**; **California rent accepted creates month-to-month**; **California no rent accepted no month-to-month**; **California rent accepted + eviction attempt violation**; **New York rent accepted creates month-to-month**; **New York no rent accepted no month-to-month**; **New York rent accepted + holdover proceeding violation**; **Default regime single rent recovery**; **Default regime no month-to-month auto-creation**; **Florida is the only 2× multiplier regime invariant** (4-regime sweep); **rent acceptance creates month-to-month only for CA and NY invariant**; **same input Florida double vs others single damages invariant** (4-regime comparison); **zero days zero damages all regimes invariant**; **Florida 29 days one month boundary**; **citation pins all regime authorities** (§ 83.58 + § 1945 + § 1946 + § 232-c + RPAPL § 711(1) + § 1161 + Restatement § 14.5); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for statutory-double-rent vs rent-acceptance vs common-law-election); **defensive negative days clamped to zero**; **defensive negative rent clamped to zero**; **single state uniqueness invariant** (4-cell truth table).

`traderview-expense::lease_assignment_consent` is the **lease assignment consent module** — landlord consent rules for tenant assignment of a residential lease. Direct sibling to `sublet_consent` but structurally distinct: ASSIGNMENT is a complete transfer of the leasehold interest (tenant discharged); SUBLEASING leaves the tenant on the hook with a subtenant occupying under the tenant's lease. Only New York draws a sharp structural distinction between the two in statute (§ 226-b); other regimes treat them as variants of the same consent question.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Standard | Distinctive feature |
|--------|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| New York | N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 226-b | Sublease: reasonable; Assignment: unconditional | **30-day release valve** — landlord must release tenant on 30 days notice for unreasonable assignment refusal |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1995.260 + § 1995.270 | Reasonable if silent on standard | NOT retroactive — pre-September 23, 1983 silent-standard leases keep unconditional rule |
| Restatement | Restatement (2d) of Property § 15.2 | Free assignability default | No clause → tenant may freely assign; clause exists → reasonableness implied (strict construction) |
| LeaseControls | Modern majority | Clause as written | Texas + Illinois + Massachusetts commercial follow this rule |

**New York § 226-b — the only sharp structural distinction.** Sublease branch (4+ unit buildings): tenant has STATUTORY right with landlord's reasonable consent; landlord's 30-day silent treatment = DEEMED CONSENT under § 226-b(2)(b); unreasonable refusal allows tenant to proceed AND recover costs + attorney fees on bad-faith showing. Assignment branch: landlord may UNCONDITIONALLY withhold consent BUT if the refusal is unreasonable, landlord MUST RELEASE THE TENANT from the lease on 30 days written notice — the **strongest tenant-side exit valve in U.S. landlord-tenant law**. Pinned by `ny_sublease_4plus_units_unreasonable_refusal_tenant_may_proceed`, `ny_sublease_30_day_failure_to_respond_deemed_consent`, `ny_sublease_29_days_pending_no_deemed_consent`, `ny_sublease_less_than_4_units_no_statutory_right` (statute only applies to 4+ unit buildings), `ny_assignment_unreasonable_refusal_triggers_30_day_release`, `ny_assignment_reasonable_refusal_no_release`, `only_ny_distinguishes_sublease_from_assignment_structurally`, `deemed_consent_only_applies_to_ny_sublease` (8-cell sweep over 4 regimes × 2 transfer types — only NY-sublease branch engages), and `ny_release_only_for_unreasonable_assignment_refusal` (4-cell truth table).

**California § 1995.260 — silent-standard implied reasonableness.** Codifies Kendall v. Ernest Pestana, Inc., 40 Cal. 3d 488 (1985) — if the lease has a consent clause but specifies no standard, an implied reasonableness standard applies. Tenant has burden of proof; satisfied by showing landlord failed to state a written reasonable objection within reasonable time of the tenant's written request. NOT retroactive — leases executed before September 23, 1983 with silent-standard clauses remain governed by pre-statute common-law rule allowing unreasonable refusal. Express-standard leases (whether reasonable or unconditional) governed by § 1995.270. Pinned by `ca_silent_standard_post_1983_implies_reasonableness`, `ca_silent_standard_pre_1983_no_implied_reasonableness` (grandfather clause), `ca_express_standard_governs`, `ca_reasonable_refusal_under_implied_standard_compliant`, and `ca_pre_1983_grandfather_only_when_silent_standard` (express-standard pre-1983 still respects express).

**Restatement § 15.2 — default free assignability.** Common-law default: tenant may freely assign the leasehold absent a lease restriction. When a restriction exists, it is strictly construed against the landlord; many Restatement-following states imply reasonableness on the restriction. Pinned by `restatement_no_consent_clause_free_assignment` and `restatement_with_consent_clause_strict_construction` and `restatement_free_assignability_only_when_no_clause` (free assignability engages ONLY when there is no clause).

**LeaseControls modern majority.** Lease enforced as written. No clause → tenant may freely transfer (no restriction to enforce). Clause with no specified standard → landlord may withhold for any reason absent state statute imposing reasonableness (Texas, Illinois, Massachusetts commercial context, and others follow this rule). Pinned by `lease_controls_no_clause_free_transfer`, `lease_controls_clause_no_standard_unconditional`, and `lease_controls_clause_with_reasonable_standard`.

**Sibling-distinction note** — every result references the structural ASSIGNMENT vs. SUBLEASING distinction and points to the sibling `sublet_consent` module. Only New York § 226-b draws a sharp structural difference between the two in statute. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-assignment-consent`. Twenty-six tests pin: **NY sublease 4+ units unreasonable refusal → tenant may proceed**; **NY sublease 4+ units reasonable refusal → may not proceed**; **NY sublease 30-day failure to respond → deemed consent**; **NY sublease 29 days pending → no deemed consent**; **NY sublease less than 4 units → no statutory right**; **NY assignment unreasonable refusal → 30-day release**; **NY assignment reasonable refusal → no release**; **CA silent standard post-1983 → implied reasonableness**; **CA silent standard pre-1983 → no implied reasonableness** (grandfather); **CA express standard governs**; **CA reasonable refusal under implied standard → compliant**; **Restatement no consent clause → free assignment**; **Restatement with consent clause → strict construction**; **LeaseControls no clause → free transfer**; **LeaseControls clause no standard → unconditional**; **LeaseControls clause with reasonable standard**; **only NY distinguishes sublease from assignment structurally invariant**; **CA pre-1983 grandfather only when silent standard invariant**; **deemed consent only applies to NY sublease invariant** (8-cell sweep — 4 regimes × 2 transfer types); **NY release only for unreasonable assignment refusal invariant** (4-cell truth table); **citation pins all regime authorities** (§ 226-b + § 1995.260 + § 1995.270 + Restatement § 15.2 + Kendall + September 23, 1983 effective date); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for ASSIGNMENT vs. SUBLEASING + § 226-b); **defensive negative pending days no deemed consent**; **Restatement free assignability only when no clause**; **NY 30-day response window constant invariant** (both 30-day windows); **single regime uniqueness invariant** (8 combinations).

`traderview-expense::lease_cure_period` is the **tenant cure period for non-rent lease breach module** — the statutory window during which a tenant may correct a non-rent lease violation (unauthorized pets, parking, occupancy limits, cleanliness, lease-clause violations) and avoid forfeiture. Distinct from rent-payment cure (covered by `eviction_notices`) and grace periods before late fees (covered by `late_payment_grace_period`). Four regimes with sharply different cure-period lengths and weekend-exclusion rules.

**Four regimes** (cure period strictly ordered CA < FL < NY < Default):

| Regime | Authority | Cure period | Weekends excluded | Recurrence bypass |
|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| California | Cal. Code Civ. Proc. § 1161(3) | 3 days | **YES — excludes Saturdays, Sundays, judicial holidays** | No |
| Florida | Fla. Stat. § 83.56(2)(b) | 7 days curable; 7-day vacate non-curable | No | **YES — 12-month recurrence rule** |
| New York | N.Y. RPAPL § 753(4) | 10 days | No | No |
| Default | Restatement (2d) of Property § 13.1 + § 16.1 | 14 days (safe-harbor baseline) | No | No |

**California § 1161(3) — only regime excluding weekends + holidays.** 3-day cure period explicitly excludes Saturdays, Sundays, and judicial holidays — effectively 3 business days. NOT applicable to non-payment-of-rent cases (use § 1161(2) 3-day pay-or-quit) or nuisance (use § 1161(4) unlawful detainer for waste). Notice must specifically identify the violation, what must be corrected, and by when. Pinned by `california_3_business_days_cure_curable`, `california_2_business_days_still_within_window`, `california_uses_business_days_not_calendar` (5 calendar days but only 2 business days → not expired — confirms regime uses business-day count), and `california_non_curable_no_cure_right`.

**Florida § 83.56(2) — only regime with 12-month recurrence bypass.** Two statutory tracks: § 83.56(2)(b) CURABLE noncompliance gets 7-day cure period (unauthorized pets, guests, vehicles; parking; cleanliness; sanitation); § 83.56(2)(a) NON-CURABLE noncompliance (intentional destruction, damage, or continued unreasonable disturbance) gets 7-day notice to VACATE with NO cure right. 12-MONTH RECURRENCE RULE: if the same curable noncompliance recurs within 12 months of initial notice, landlord may commence eviction WITHOUT delivering a subsequent notice (tenant's cure right does not reset on recurrence). Pinned by `florida_7_day_cure_curable`, `florida_at_7_day_boundary_expired`, `florida_non_curable_no_cure_right_7_day_vacate`, `florida_recurrence_within_12_months_no_second_notice`, and `florida_no_recurrence_requires_second_notice`.

**New York RPAPL § 753(4) — longest baseline cure.** 10-day cure period for breach of lease covenant in residential leases. Distinct from HSTPA 2019 30-day cure for chronic late-rent / nuisance defenses (those are evidentiary defenses in housing court, not § 753(4) lease-covenant breaches). Pinned by `new_york_10_day_cure_curable`, `new_york_at_10_day_boundary_expired`, and `new_york_9_days_within_window`.

**Default common-law reasonable cure.** Restatement (Second) of Property § 13.1 + § 16.1 — reasonable cure period determined case-by-case based on nature of breach + good-faith effort to remedy. Safe-harbor baseline: 14 days. Pinned by `default_14_day_safe_harbor` and `default_at_14_day_boundary_expired`.

**Multi-regime invariants** — `cure_period_strict_ordering_ca_lt_fl_lt_ny_lt_default_invariant` (3 < 7 < 10 < 14 day ordering); `only_california_excludes_weekends_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms unique CA weekend-exclusion); `only_florida_has_recurrence_bypass_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms unique FL recurrence bypass); `non_curable_no_cure_right_all_regimes_invariant` (non-curable violations forfeit cure right uniformly); `cure_window_expired_truth_table_curable` (8-cell sweep — 4 regimes × 2 day-counts each at boundary and one-day-before).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references the rent-payment-cure regime distinction (`eviction_notices` covers CA § 1161(2) 3-day pay-or-quit, FL § 83.56(3) 3-day, NY HSTPA 14-day rent demand) and the late-fee grace-period regime distinction (`late_payment_grace_period`). Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lease-cure-period`. Twenty-two tests pin: **California 3 business days cure curable**; **California 2 business days still within window**; **California uses business days not calendar** (5 calendar / 2 business → not expired); **California non-curable no cure right**; **Florida 7-day cure curable**; **Florida at 7-day boundary expired**; **Florida non-curable no cure right 7-day vacate**; **Florida recurrence within 12 months no second notice**; **Florida no recurrence requires second notice**; **New York 10-day cure curable**; **New York at 10-day boundary expired**; **New York 9 days within window**; **Default 14-day safe harbor**; **Default at 14-day boundary expired**; **cure period strict ordering CA < FL < NY < Default invariant** (3 < 7 < 10 < 14); **only California excludes weekends invariant** (4-regime sweep); **only Florida has recurrence bypass invariant** (4-regime sweep); **non-curable no cure right all regimes invariant**; **citation pins all authorities** (§ 1161(2) + § 1161(3) + § 1161(4) + § 83.56(2)(a) + § 83.56(2)(b) + § 83.56(3) + RPAPL § 753(4) + HSTPA + Restatement § 13.1 + § 16.1); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for non-rent vs rent-payment cure + late-fee grace period); **defensive negative days no premature expiration**; **cure window expired truth table curable** (8-cell sweep — 4 regimes × 2 day-counts each at boundary and one-day-before).

`traderview-expense::portable_tenant_screening_report` is the **portable / reusable tenant screening report regulation module** — when must a landlord accept a tenant-prepared pre-pulled screening report in lieu of conducting and charging for their own? Distinct from sibling `application_fees` (general caps on screening fees) and `adverse_action_notice` (FCRA disclosure when application denied based on report). Both Colorado and Washington regimes build on the federal Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq.) consumer reporting agency framework.

**Three regimes** with sharply different acceptance + fee rules:

| Regime | Authority | Acceptance rule | Penalty |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| Colorado | Colo. Rev. Stat. § 38-12-902 / § 38-12-904 (HB23-1099, 2023) | **MANDATORY** acceptance of compliant report | **$2,500** ($50 if cured within 7 days) |
| Washington | RCW 59.18.257 | **OPT-IN** disclosure — landlord publishes acceptance status on property website | **$100** max + attorney fees |
| Default | None | No portability requirement; landlord may demand fresh fee-based screening | n/a |

**Colorado § 38-12-902 / § 38-12-904 — mandatory acceptance.** Four conditions for a compliant portable report: (1) ≤30 days old at submission; (2) prepared directly by consumer reporting agency or third-party website complying with state and federal laws; (3) made available to landlord at NO cost to access/use; (4) includes tenant's STATEMENT OF NO MATERIAL CHANGE in the report information. If conditions met, landlord MUST accept AND MAY NOT charge any rental-application or report-access fee. § 38-12-904(4) violation: $2,500 plus court costs + attorney fees, REDUCED to $50 if landlord cures within 7 calendar days of notice. § 38-12-904 single-application-at-a-time exception: landlord accepting only one application at a time + refunding fee within 20 days of denial is exempt from mandatory acceptance. Pinned by `colorado_compliant_report_accepted_compliant`, `colorado_compliant_report_refused_2500_penalty`, `colorado_refused_cured_within_7_days_50_penalty`, `colorado_accepted_but_fee_charged_violation`, `colorado_single_application_exception_no_portability_required`, `colorado_report_31_days_old_outside_validity`, and `colorado_missing_material_change_statement_not_compliant_report`.

**Washington RCW 59.18.257 — opt-in disclosure.** Landlord must publish on the property's online listing AND in pre-screening writing to the prospective tenant whether the landlord will accept a Comprehensive Reusable Tenant Screening Report (CRTSR). CRTSR statutory components: consumer credit report + criminal history + eviction history + employment verification + address and rental history; ≤30 days old. If landlord OPTS IN (declares acceptance), MUST accept compliant CRTSR and may NOT charge a screening fee. If landlord OPTS OUT (declares non-acceptance), may conduct own screening at actual cost capped at customary local screening-service rate. § 59.18.257(2) violation: up to $100 + court costs + reasonable attorney fees. Pinned by `washington_landlord_accepts_compliant_report_compliant`, `washington_landlord_declared_accepts_then_refused_100_penalty`, `washington_landlord_opt_out_no_portability_required`, `washington_accepts_then_charges_fee_violation`, and `washington_missing_crtsr_component_landlord_may_reject`.

**Default — no portability requirement.** Landlord may demand a fresh fee-based screening under the general `application_fees` regime and FCRA disclosure obligations. The portable-report mandate originated in Colorado (HB23-1099, 2023) and the opt-in disclosure model in Washington; most other states retain default landlord discretion.

**Multi-regime invariants** — `colorado_penalty_higher_than_washington_invariant` (CO $2,500 = 25× WA $100); `colorado_cure_reduces_penalty_50x_invariant` ($50 cured = 1/50 of $2,500 full); `validity_window_30_days_constant_invariant` (both regimes use 30-day report validity; CO 7-day cure window); `only_colorado_has_mandatory_acceptance_invariant` (CO mandatory default; WA depends on disclosure; Default false); `missing_crtsr_components_truth_table` (5-cell sweep — any missing component fails completeness in WA).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references `application_fees` (general screening fee caps) and `adverse_action_notice` (FCRA disclosure on denial) and the federal FCRA framework underlying both state regimes. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/portable-tenant-screening-report`. Twenty-two tests pin: **Colorado compliant report accepted compliant**; **Colorado compliant report refused $2,500 penalty**; **Colorado refused cured within 7 days $50 penalty**; **Colorado accepted but fee charged violation**; **Colorado single-application exception no portability required**; **Colorado report 31 days old outside validity**; **Colorado missing material change statement not compliant**; **Washington landlord accepts compliant report compliant**; **Washington landlord declared accepts then refused $100 penalty**; **Washington landlord opt-out no portability required**; **Washington accepts then charges fee violation**; **Washington missing CRTSR component landlord may reject**; **Default no portability no violation**; **Colorado penalty higher than Washington invariant** (25× ratio); **Colorado cure reduces penalty 50× invariant** ($2,500 → $50); **validity window 30 days constant invariant** (both regimes + Colorado 7-day cure window); **only Colorado has mandatory acceptance invariant** (3-regime sweep with WA opt-in/opt-out branch); **citation pins all regime authorities** (§ 38-12-902 + § 38-12-904 + § 38-12-904(4) + RCW 59.18.257 + § 59.18.257(1) + § 59.18.257(2) + 15 U.S.C. § 1681 + HB23-1099); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for application_fees + adverse_action_notice + FCRA); **defensive negative report age no validity**; **validity boundary 30 days exact within window** (≤ comparison); **missing CRTSR components truth table** (5-cell sweep — each missing component fails).

`traderview-expense::hoa_rental_restriction` is the **HOA rental restriction enforceability module** — when may a homeowners association enforce restrictions on a unit owner's right to rent their property? Trader-critical for landlord-owners holding property inside common-interest communities, where a single HOA amendment can destroy a building's rental cash flow. Distinct from sibling `str_regulation` (municipal short-term-rental laws — public regulation enforced by local government, vs. HOA private covenants), `tenant_topa` (tenant opportunity-to-purchase rights), and `condominium_conversion_protection` (tenant-side protection during condo conversions).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Rule |
|--------|-----------|------|
| Florida | Fla. Stat. § 720.306(1)(h) (eff. July 1, 2021) | **GRANDFATHER RULE** — post-July-2021 amendments bind only owners acquiring after amendment OR consenting; two narrow exceptions bind all |
| Arizona | A.R.S. § 33-1806.01 | **DECLARATION CONTROLS** + statutory third-party agent designation right under § 33-1806.01(B) |
| Default | None | Declaration controls; no statutory grandfather |

**Florida § 720.306(1)(h) — grandfather rule.** Any HOA amendment adopted after July 1, 2021 prohibiting or regulating rentals applies ONLY to (a) owners who acquired title AFTER the amendment took effect; OR (b) owners who AFFIRMATIVELY consented to the amendment. **Silence does NOT count as consent.** TWO NARROW EXCEPTIONS bind ALL owners regardless of grandfather status:

| Exception | Statute | Scope |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Short-term rental | § 720.306(1)(h)1 | Amendments restricting leases ≤ 6 months |
| Frequency | § 720.306(1)(h)2 | Amendments limiting rentals to ≤ 3 per calendar year |

Grandfather protection SURVIVES transfer to (a) heir acquiring on prior owner's death, or (b) entity affiliated with prior owner. **LOST upon transfer to unrelated third party** under § 720.306(1)(h)3. Pinned by `florida_pre_amendment_owner_no_consent_grandfather_engaged`, `florida_pre_amendment_owner_consented_no_grandfather`, `florida_post_amendment_owner_no_grandfather`, `florida_heir_acquires_grandfather_preserved`, `florida_transfer_to_unrelated_party_erodes_grandfather`, `florida_pre_july_2021_amendment_not_subject_to_grandfather` (statute applies only to post-July-2021 amendments), `florida_grandfather_truth_table` (4-cell pre/post × consent sweep), `florida_grandfather_eroded_by_transfer_truth_table` (4-cell heir × transferred sweep), and `florida_exception_overrides_grandfather_invariant` (both exceptions bind all owners regardless of grandfather-favoring facts).

**Florida short-term + frequency exceptions bind everyone.** Even owners with otherwise-perfect grandfather facts (acquired before amendment, did not consent) are bound by these two narrow exception categories. Pinned by `florida_short_term_rental_restriction_binds_all_owners` and `florida_three_or_fewer_rentals_restriction_binds_all_owners`.

**Arizona § 33-1806.01 — declaration controls + agent designation.** Declaration controls rental restrictions in planned communities; HOA may prohibit rentals or impose time-period restrictions if such restrictions appear in the declaration. Statute does NOT set a percentage cap on rentals. Owner has statutory right under § 33-1806.01(B) to designate a third-party agent for HOA matters (except voting in elections and serving on the board) by SIGNED WRITTEN DESIGNATION delivered to the HOA. Pinned by `arizona_declaration_permits_no_enforceable_restriction`, `arizona_declaration_restricts_enforceable`, `arizona_agent_designation_validly_invoked`, and `arizona_no_agent_designation_no_engagement`.

**Default — declaration controls.** No statutory grandfather protection. HOA amendments bind existing owners under general covenants law subject to declaration's amendment procedure. Most states follow this default absent specific legislation. Pinned by `default_declaration_permits_no_enforceable_restriction` and `default_declaration_restricts_enforceable` (confirms no grandfather engaged in Default regime).

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_florida_offers_grandfather_protection_invariant` (3-regime sweep with restriction in declaration; grandfather engages only in FL); `only_arizona_has_statutory_agent_designation_invariant` (3-regime sweep with agent designation signed; engages only in AZ).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result distinguishes HOA private covenants (enforced by association) from municipal STR public regulation (enforced by local government); references `tenant_topa`, `str_regulation`, and `condominium_conversion_protection`. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/hoa-rental-restriction`. Twenty-one tests pin: **Florida pre-amendment owner no consent grandfather engaged**; **Florida pre-amendment owner consented no grandfather**; **Florida post-amendment owner no grandfather**; **Florida heir acquires grandfather preserved**; **Florida transfer to unrelated party erodes grandfather**; **Florida short-term rental restriction binds all owners**; **Florida ≤3 rentals restriction binds all owners**; **Florida pre-July-2021 amendment not subject to grandfather**; **Arizona declaration permits no enforceable restriction**; **Arizona declaration restricts enforceable**; **Arizona agent designation validly invoked**; **Arizona no agent designation no engagement**; **Default declaration permits no enforceable restriction**; **Default declaration restricts enforceable** (no grandfather in Default); **only Florida offers grandfather protection invariant** (3-regime sweep); **only Arizona has statutory agent designation invariant** (3-regime sweep); **Florida grandfather truth table** (4-cell pre/post × consent sweep); **Florida exception overrides grandfather invariant** (both exceptions bind regardless of grandfather-favoring facts); **citation pins all regime authorities** (§ 720.306(1)(h) + § 720.306(1)(h)1 + § 720.306(1)(h)2 + § 720.306(1)(h)3 + A.R.S. § 33-1806.01 + § 33-1806.01(A) + § 33-1806.01(B)); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for HOA private covenants vs. municipal STR public regulation); **Florida grandfather eroded by transfer truth table** (4-cell heir × transferred sweep — transfer to unrelated party always erodes grandfather under § 720.306(1)(h)3).

`traderview-expense::hoa_fee_tenant_enforcement` is the **HOA fee enforcement against tenant in single-family / townhome / condominium rentals** module — when can a homeowners association enforce delinquent dues DIRECTLY against the tenant rather than only the owner-landlord? Critical trader-landlord operational risk: trader-landlord defaults on HOA dues → HOA may seize rent directly from tenant and evict tenant for nonpayment in Florida. Sibling to `hoa_rental_restriction` (HOA's restrictions ON renting), not duplicative.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Direct tenant enforcement | Tenant immunity | Tenant rent credit | HOA eviction authority |
|--------|---------------------------|------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Florida § 720.3085(8) + § 718.116(11) | YES (after demand) | YES | YES | YES |
| Texas § 209.0064 | NO (owner only) | N/A | N/A | NO |
| California Davis-Stirling §§ 5650, 5710, 5715 | NO (foreclose on owner) | N/A | N/A | NO |
| Default | NO | N/A | N/A | NO |

**Florida — Fla. Stat. § 720.3085(8)(a)-(d) (single-family HOA) + § 718.116(11) (condominium)**. Most aggressive direct-tenant enforcement framework in the country. When parcel owner is delinquent on ANY monetary obligation to the association, **§ 720.3085(8)(a)** authorizes the HOA to DEMAND that the tenant pay subsequent rental payments directly to the association. Demand MUST be by hand delivery OR United States mail (verbal demand insufficient). **§ 720.3085(8)(b)** grants tenant IMMUNITY from any landlord claim for rent timely paid to the association after written demand. **§ 720.3085(8)(c)** grants tenant a RENT CREDIT against landlord-owed rent equal to amounts paid to the association — tenant liability capped at amount due to landlord. **§ 720.3085(8)(d)** authorizes the HOA to sue the tenant directly for eviction if tenant fails to pay after written demand.

**Texas — Tex. Prop. Code § 209.0064 (Texas Residential Property Owners Protection Act)**. HOA enforces ONLY against OWNER (landlord), not tenant. If lease passes through HOA dues to tenant, landlord may evict for nonpayment per lease terms — but HOA itself has no direct authority over tenant. § 209.0064 third-party collection requires (i) written notice by certified mail to OWNER listing delinquent amounts + payment plans, (ii) 45-day cure period.

**California — Davis-Stirling Common Interest Development Act (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 5650, 5710, 5715)**. HOA enforces only against OWNER. § 5710 authorizes HOA to foreclose on owner's interest in unit, NOT tenant. No statutory direct-tenant collection mechanism.

**Default — HOA enforces only against owner**. Most states follow CA / TX pattern. Federal **FDCPA (15 U.S.C. § 1692)** applies if HOA hires third-party collector.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/hoa-fee-tenant-enforcement`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **FL full demand with notice engages enforcement** (all 4 flags green); **FL demand without written notice voids enforcement** (§ 720.3085(8) hand-delivery / USPS requirement); **FL no landlord delinquency no enforcement**; **FL no demand no enforcement**; **FL demand with notice but tenant not paid eviction engaged**; **FL condominium parallel rule note present** (§ 718.116(11)); **FL citation pins subsections (a)-(d) and condo section**; **FL tenant credit note describes liability cap**; **TX owner-only enforcement invariant**; **TX delinquent without 45-day notice violation** (§ 209.0064); **TX lease passthrough note describes landlord eviction**; **TX citation pins § 209.0064**; **CA owner-only enforcement invariant**; **CA Davis-Stirling foreclose owner not tenant note** (§ 5710); **CA citation pins §§ 5650, 5710, 5715 + Davis-Stirling Act**; **default no direct tenant authority invariant**; **default FDCPA note for third-party collector** (15 U.S.C. § 1692); **Florida unique direct tenant enforcement invariant** (3-other-regime sweep); **Florida unique tenant immunity invariant** (3-other-regime sweep); **Florida unique rent credit invariant** (3-other-regime sweep); **Florida unique HOA eviction authority invariant** (3-other-regime sweep); **four regimes routed correctly** (FL + TX + CA + Default sweep); **lease passthrough note in three non-Florida regimes**; **FL delinquency without demand no enforcement**; **FL demand without delinquency no enforcement**; **FL four subsection notes all present** ((a) + (b) + (c) + (d)); **TX clean compliance no violations**; **FL clean compliance no violations**.

`traderview-expense::rent_acceleration_enforceability` is the **rent acceleration clause enforceability module** — when may a landlord demand the full unpaid rent balance for the remainder of the lease term as a lump sum upon tenant default? States diverge sharply on (a) whether such a clause is enforceable as liquidated damages, (b) whether the landlord must mitigate or apply a present-value discount, or (c) whether the clause is void entirely as an unenforceable penalty. Trader-critical for landlord-tenant disputes on multi-year commercial leases or residential leases drafted by landlord counsel. Distinct from siblings `duty_to_mitigate_damages` (general mitigation rules), `late_fee_caps` (per-month late charges), `lease_cure_period` (non-rent breach cure), and `holdover_tenant_damages` (post-lease-term recovery).

**Three regimes** with sharply different outcomes:

| Regime | Authority | Lease type | Outcome |
|--------|-----------|------------|---------|
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1671(d) | Residential | **PRESUMED INVALID** — landlord must rebut |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 1671(b) + § 1951.2 | Commercial | Reasonableness test + mitigation duty |
| New York | Holy Properties Ltd., L.P. v. Kenneth Cole, 87 N.Y.2d 130 (1995) | Commercial | **Enforceable WITHOUT mitigation duty**; PV discount required |
| New York | Common law (residential) | Residential | Penalty doctrine + mitigation duty |
| Default | Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356 + Property § 12.1 | Either | Penalty doctrine + mitigation + PV discount |

**California § 1671(d) — strongest residential tenant protection.** Liquidated damages provisions in residential leases are PRESUMED INVALID until the landlord affirmatively proves the amount was a reasonable estimate of damages at contract formation. Expert testimony often required to rebut. § 1671(b) governs commercial reasonableness test. § 1951.2 imposes mitigation duty on commercial landlords. Pinned by `california_residential_presumed_invalid_no_rebuttal` (no rebuttal → enforceable amount = 0), `california_residential_rebutted_capped_at_actual_damages` (rebutted → capped at actual-damages estimate), `california_commercial_reasonable_enforceable_with_mitigation` (mitigation offset applied), and `california_commercial_unreasonable_void_as_penalty` ($120K acceleration vs. $30K damages > 2× threshold → penalty void).

**New York Holy Properties commercial rule — most landlord-favorable in the U.S.** The 1995 Court of Appeals decision holds that a commercial landlord NEED NOT mitigate damages after a tenant's material breach. Acceleration clauses enforceable absent fraud, exploitative overreaching, or unconscionable conduct. BUT — Court of Appeals also requires that accelerated rent be DISCOUNTED TO PRESENT VALUE when the landlord has possession, so that damages are not disproportionate to actual loss. Residential treatment less settled; falls back to penalty doctrine + mitigation duty. Pinned by `new_york_commercial_enforceable_with_pv_discount`, `new_york_commercial_no_pv_discount_violation` (PV discount required), `new_york_commercial_unconscionable_void` (fraud/overreaching/unconscionability voids), `new_york_residential_falls_back_to_penalty_doctrine`, and `new_york_residential_unreasonable_void`.

**Default common-law penalty doctrine + mitigation duty + PV discount.** Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356: liquidated damages enforceable only if (a) reasonable forecast of actual damages AND (b) actual damages difficult to estimate at contract formation. Modern majority adopts Restatement (Second) of Property § 12.1 mitigation duty. Default applies BOTH mitigation reduction AND present-value discount. Pinned by `default_reasonable_with_pv_enforceable`, `default_unreasonable_void_as_penalty`, `default_no_pv_discount_violation_even_when_reasonable`, and `default_mitigation_not_attempted_violation`.

**Multi-regime invariants** — `only_california_residential_has_presumption_invariant` (3-regime sweep — only CA residential triggers PresumedInvalidResidential); `only_new_york_commercial_skips_mitigation_invariant` (Holy Properties unique among the 3 regimes); `pv_discount_required_outside_california_invariant` (CA does not require PV explicitly — addresses via mitigation; NY commercial + Default both require PV); `unconscionable_conduct_voids_all_regimes_for_commercial` (NY-specific exception).

**Mitigation offset math** — `mitigation_offset_reduces_recovery` ($60K - $40K = $20K); `mitigation_offset_exceeds_acceleration_zero_recovery` (clamped at 0; never negative). Standard i64 arithmetic with `.max(0)` clamp to handle the case where mitigation income exceeds the demand.

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references siblings `duty_to_mitigate_damages`, `late_fee_caps`, `lease_cure_period`, `holdover_tenant_damages`, and pins the structural divergence: California most protective for RESIDENTIAL (§ 1671(d) presumption); New York most landlord-favorable for COMMERCIAL (Holy Properties no-mitigation); Default sits between with full penalty doctrine + mitigation + PV discount. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rent-acceleration-enforceability`. Twenty-three tests pin: **California residential presumed invalid no rebuttal** ($0 enforceable); **California residential rebutted capped at actual damages**; **California commercial reasonable enforceable with mitigation** ($6M - $1M offset = $5M); **California commercial unreasonable void as penalty** (acceleration > 2× actual damages); **New York commercial enforceable with PV discount**; **New York commercial no PV discount violation**; **New York commercial unconscionable conduct void**; **New York residential falls back to penalty doctrine**; **New York residential unreasonable void** ($120K vs $10K → void); **default reasonable with PV enforceable**; **default unreasonable void as penalty**; **default no PV discount violation even when reasonable**; **default mitigation not attempted violation**; **only California residential has presumption invariant** (3-regime sweep); **only New York commercial skips mitigation invariant** (Holy Properties); **PV discount required outside California invariant**; **unconscionable conduct voids all regimes for commercial**; **mitigation offset reduces recovery** ($60K - $40K = $20K); **mitigation offset exceeds acceleration zero recovery** (saturating at $0); **citation pins all regime authorities** (§ 1671 + § 1671(b) + § 1671(d) + § 1951.2 + Holy Properties 87 N.Y.2d 130 (1995) + Restatement Contracts § 356 + Property § 12.1); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `duty_to_mitigate_damages` + `late_fee_caps` + `lease_cure_period` + `holdover_tenant_damages` + structural CA/NY/Default divergence); **defensive negative acceleration clamped**; **defensive negative mitigation offset clamped**.

`traderview-expense::tenant_in_foreclosure_protection` is the **tenant-in-foreclosure protections module** — what notice and timing must a successor in interest provide to existing tenants when a residential property is foreclosed? Trader-critical for landlord-investors purchasing foreclosed properties — buying property with existing bona fide tenants triggers a federal floor plus state-specific overlays. Three regimes built on the federal PTFA baseline, with California adding pre-sale posting requirements and New York extending protections to non-named and rent-regulated tenants.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Distinctive feature |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| Federal PTFA (Default) | Pub. L. 111-22 § 702 (restored permanently by Pub. L. 115-174 § 304, eff. June 23, 2018) | National floor — greater of 90 days OR lease remainder |
| California | Cal. Civ. Code § 2924.8 + § 1161b | Pre-sale notice posting + first-class mailing; § 2924.8(d) **$100 infraction** for tearing down notice within 72 hours |
| New York | N.Y. RPAPL § 1305 | § 1305(4) preserves additional rights for rent-control/stabilization/subsidy tenants AND tenants not named in foreclosure action |

**PTFA bona fide tenant — three-prong test** (BOTH first conditions + the conjunctive rent test):

| Prong | Rule |
|-------|------|
| (1) | Tenant is NOT the mortgagor or mortgagor's spouse, parent, or child |
| (2) | Lease is an arm's-length transaction |
| (3) | Rent is NOT substantially less than fair market rent (UNLESS reduced/subsidized by federal, state, or local program) |

All three required. Pinned by `bona_fide_baseline_all_prongs_pass`, `mortgagor_family_disqualifies`, `non_arms_length_disqualifies`, `rent_substantially_below_market_disqualifies`, `subsidy_rescues_below_market_rent` (subsidy rescues the third prong even when rent is substantially below market), and `bona_fide_prongs_truth_table` (8-cell sweep — only the all-three-true cell engages bona fide status).

**PTFA notice timing — greater of 90 days or lease remainder**:

| Scenario | Notice period |
|----------|---------------|
| Bona fide + written lease + lease remaining ≥ 90 days | Lease remainder |
| Bona fide + written lease + lease remaining < 90 days | 90 days minimum |
| Bona fide + no lease (month-to-month) | 90 days minimum |
| Non-bona-fide tenant | No PTFA notice protection (state law still applies) |
| Owner-occupant buyer primary residence | 90 days minimum (lease early-terminated) |

Pinned by `bona_fide_with_lease_gets_lease_end` (180 days remaining → 180 days), `bona_fide_lease_below_90_days_gets_90`, `bona_fide_no_lease_gets_90_days_only`, `non_bona_fide_no_notice_period`, `owner_occupant_exception_engages`, `early_eviction_violation_before_90_day_window` (eviction within 90-day window flagged as PTFA violation), `day_90_passed_no_ptfa_window_violation`, and `ptfa_90_day_floor_all_regimes_invariant` (3-regime sweep confirms 90-day minimum is universal floor).

**California § 2924.8** layers pre-sale notice posting + first-class mailing addressed to "Resident of property subject to foreclosure sale" on top of PTFA. Failure to post + mail violates procedural prerequisites. § 2924.8(d) makes it an INFRACTION to tear down the posted notice within 72 hours of posting — $100 fine (applies to person who tore it down, NOT to landlord). § 1161b parallels PTFA for unlawful detainer post-foreclosure proceedings. Pinned by `california_pre_sale_notice_posted_compliant`, `california_pre_sale_notice_missing_violation`, `california_tear_down_infraction_engages`, `california_tear_down_only_applies_in_california`, and `only_california_has_tear_down_infraction_invariant` (3-regime sweep).

**New York RPAPL § 1305** mirrors PTFA's greater-of-90-or-lease-end rule. § 1305(4) preserves additional rights for (a) federally subsidized tenants, (b) rent-control/rent-stabilization tenants, and (c) tenants NOT NAMED in the foreclosure action. These tenants retain ALL pre-foreclosure protections layered ON TOP of § 1305 + PTFA floor. Pinned by `new_york_rent_control_preserves_additional_rights`, `new_york_not_named_in_foreclosure_preserves_rights`, `new_york_preservation_only_in_ny` (CA + Default ignore NY-specific fields), `new_york_no_preservation_when_neither_condition_met`, and `only_new_york_preserves_additional_rights_invariant` (3-regime sweep).

**Sibling distinction note** — every result references siblings `foreclosure_tenant_rights`, `mid_tenancy_ownership_change`, and `eviction_notices`. Federal PTFA creates a national FLOOR (90-day notice + lease-end honoring for bona fide tenants); state law adds CEILING when more protective. Pinned by `sibling_distinction_note_present` (UX-text regression).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/tenant-in-foreclosure-protection`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **bona fide baseline all prongs pass**; **mortgagor family disqualifies**; **non-arms-length disqualifies**; **rent substantially below market disqualifies**; **subsidy rescues below-market rent**; **bona fide with lease gets lease end** (180 days); **bona fide lease below 90 days gets 90**; **bona fide no lease gets 90 days only**; **non bona fide no notice period**; **owner occupant exception engages**; **early eviction violation before 90-day window**; **day 90 passed no PTFA window violation**; **California pre-sale notice posted compliant**; **California pre-sale notice missing violation**; **California tear-down infraction engages**; **California tear-down only applies in California**; **New York rent control preserves additional rights**; **New York not named in foreclosure preserves rights**; **New York preservation only in NY**; **New York no preservation when neither condition met**; **PTFA 90-day floor all regimes invariant** (3-regime sweep); **bona fide prongs truth table** (8-cell sweep — only all-three-true cell engages); **only California has tear-down infraction invariant** (3-regime sweep); **only New York preserves additional rights invariant** (3-regime sweep); **citation pins all regime authorities** (Pub. L. 111-22 § 702 + Pub. L. 115-174 § 304 + § 2924.8 + § 2924.8(a)/(d) + § 1161b + RPAPL § 1305 + § 1305(2)/(4) + June 23, 2018 effective date); **sibling distinction note present** (UX-text regression for `foreclosure_tenant_rights` + `mid_tenancy_ownership_change` + `eviction_notices` + national-FLOOR concept); **defensive negative lease remaining clamped** (negative → 0 → 90-day fallback); **defensive negative days since notice no violation** (notice not yet served); **CA tear-down fine constant** ($100 = 10,000 cents; 72-hour window; PTFA 90 days).

`traderview-expense::tenant_late_fee_cap` is the **multi-jurisdictional residential tenant late fee cap framework module** — five-jurisdiction comparison reveals dramatically different rule architectures: NY hard cap, CA reasonableness/liquidated-damages test, TX percentage safe-harbor with treble damages, FL manufactured-home reasonableness regime, Default common-law liquidated damages. Trader-landlord critical because late-fee over-collection is one of the most common landlord mistakes — each over-cap charge can trigger statutory damages plus attorney fees. Distinct from siblings `rental_security_deposit_interest`, `landlord_self_help_eviction_prohibition`, `landlord_retaliation_damages`, `rental_junk_fee_transparency`.

**Five-jurisdiction statutory framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Late Fee Cap | Grace Period | Remedy |
|--------------|--------|--------------|--------------|--------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 1671(d) + Orozco v. Casimiro (2004) | LIQUIDATED-DAMAGES REASONABLENESS test (5-6% threshold) | None statutory | Fee VOID in toto; tenant recovers charged amount |
| **New York** | N.Y. Real Prop. Law § 238-a (HSTPA of 2019) | **LESSER of $50 OR 5% of monthly rent** | **5 days (mandatory)** | Refund of excess + no eviction solely for late fees |
| **Florida (manufactured home)** | Fla. Stat. § 83.808 | Reasonable: common $20 OR 20% of monthly rent (whichever GREATER) | None statutory | Court reasonableness |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code § 92.019 (revised 2019) | **12% safe harbor for ≤ 4 units / 10% for > 4 units** | **2 days (mandatory)** | **$100 + TREBLE late fee collected + attorney fees** |
| **Default** | Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 356 | Reasonable relationship to actual/anticipated damages; NOT a penalty | None statutory | Common-law damages |
