**New York City — NYC Admin Code § 28-304 + NYC Building Code Chapter 30 + Table N1 of ASME A17.1 as modified by Chapter K1 of Appendix K** — two annual inspections required: **Category 1 test (PCT)** by approved elevator agency + Department of Buildings (DOB) periodic inspection. **Category 5 test** required every **5 years**; **Category 3 hydraulic test** every **3 years**. § 28-304.6.1 — inspections must be performed by **DOB-approved elevator agency**. **PVT-A Form** (Periodic Test and Inspection Affirmation) must be filed with DOB within **60 days** of inspection. § 28-304.6.5 — civil penalty **$3,000-$10,000 per violation** + DOB stop-use order authority.

**Default — ASME A17.1-2025 (Safety Code for Elevators and Escalators) + ANSI/ASME A17.1/CSA B44-2025** — ASME A17.1-2025 published; periodic inspection per Table N1; state adoption timelines vary. **QEI (Qualified Elevator Inspector)** certification by ASME or state authority. **Category 1 test (annual)** + Category 3 (3-year hydraulic) + Category 5 (5-year full). Building owner responsible for engaging certified inspector + maintaining inspection records on premises.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-elevator-safety-inspection`. Thirty-eight tests pin: **CA clean compliant**; **CA annual 366 days violation** (§ 7320); **CA uncertified inspector violation** (§ 7317 4 years); **CA Form 80 not posted violation**; **CA $200/day penalty exact compliant**; **CA wrong penalty amount violation**; **NYC clean compliant**; **NYC annual Category 1 366 days violation** (§ 28-304 PCT); **NYC hydraulic Category 3 3-year boundary compliant**; **NYC hydraulic Category 3 over 3-year violation**; **NYC non-hydraulic no Category 3 check**; **NYC Category 5 5-year boundary compliant**; **NYC Category 5 over 5-year violation**; **NYC unapproved agency violation** (§ 28-304.6.1); **NYC PVT-A not filed violation** (60 days); **NYC penalty at $3,000 minimum in range**; **NYC penalty at $10,000 maximum in range**; **NYC penalty below minimum violation**; **NYC penalty above maximum violation**; **default clean compliant**; **default QEI certified inspector accepted**; **default no certification violation**; **citation pins CA / NYC / default authorities** (all three); **note pins CA $200/day penalty**; **note pins CA 4 years inspector experience**; **note pins NYC three test categories** (Category 1 PCT + Category 3 hydraulic + Category 5 full); **note pins NYC PVT-A 60-day filing**; **note pins NYC $3,000-$10,000 penalty range**; **note pins default ASME A17.1-2025**; **note pins default three category schedule**; **Category 3 only engages for hydraulic invariant**; **CA uniquely requires Form 80 invariant**; **NYC uniquely requires PVT-A 60-day filing invariant**; **NYC Category 5 5-year invariant**; **inspector certification truth table for default** (4-cell sweep — 3 certified types + None); **multiple NYC violations stack** (3 violations).

`traderview-expense::rental_flood_hazard_disclosure` is the **multi-jurisdictional residential rental flood hazard disclosure framework module** — climate-era statutory disclosure regime added across multiple states between 2018 and 2024. Trader-landlord critical because waterfront / coastal / floodplain-zone investment properties (common in trader real-estate portfolios) trigger mandatory written pre-lease disclosure under state law, and failure routes directly to tenant LEASE TERMINATION right plus statutory damages exposure. Distinct from siblings `rental_basement_water_intrusion_disclosure` (subsurface water), `rental_sinkhole_disclosure` (FL specific), `rental_property_registration` (registration regimes), `tenant_in_foreclosure_protection`.

**Four-jurisdiction statutory framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Effective Date | Trigger | Tenant Remedy |
|--------------|--------|----------------|---------|---------------|
| **California** | Cal. Gov. Code § 8589.45 (AB 646 of 2017) | **July 1, 2018** | ACTUAL KNOWLEDGE + FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area OR Cal OES area of potential flooding | Civil penalties + lease damages |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code § 92.0135 (HB 531 of 2021) | **January 1, 2022** | 100-year floodplain (unless elevation above per federal regulations) OR 5-year prior flooding damage | **TERMINATE LEASE within 30 days of SUBSTANTIAL LOSS** (50%+ of personal property value) |
| **New Jersey** | N.J.S.A. 46:8-50 et seq. (Flood Risk Notification Law 2024) | **March 20, 2024** | Commercial OR 3+ residential units (4+ if owner-occupied) + FEMA 100-year OR 500-year floodplain | **TERMINATE LEASE + statutory damages + attorney fees** |
| **Default** | Common-law fraudulent concealment + implied warranty of habitability | N/A | Actual/constructive knowledge + concealment | Common-law damages |

Pinned by `ca_special_flood_hazard_compliant`, `ca_no_landlord_knowledge_no_obligation`, `ca_pre_july_2018_no_obligation` (boundary precision — June 2018 = no obligation, July 2018 = obligation), `texas_floodplain_100_year_triggers_obligation`, `texas_elevation_above_floodplain_exception_lifts_obligation`, `texas_pre_2022_no_obligation` (boundary), `nj_3_unit_floodplain_triggers_obligation`, `nj_2_unit_no_obligation`, `nj_pre_march_2024_no_obligation` (boundary — Feb 2024 = no, March 2024 = obligation).

**California content requirements — Cal. Gov. Code § 8589.45**:

| Content element | Source |
|------------------|--------|
| Special Flood Hazard Area / area of potential flooding statement | § 8589.45(a)(1) |
| Cal OES MyHazards website URL | § 8589.45(a)(2) |
| Renter's insurance + flood insurance recommendation | § 8589.45(a)(3) |
| Owner not required to provide additional info statement | § 8589.45(a)(4) |
| 8-point minimum type size | § 8589.45(c) |

Pinned by `ca_missing_myhazards_url_violation`, `ca_missing_insurance_recommendation_violation`, `ca_under_8_point_type_violation`, `ca_missing_disclosure_violation`.

**Texas two-prong trigger — Tex. Prop. Code § 92.0135**:

| Prong | Source | Trigger | Exception |
|-------|--------|---------|-----------|
| 1 (geographic) | § 92.0135(a)(1) | Dwelling in 100-year floodplain | Elevation above per federal regulations |
| 2 (historical) | § 92.0135(a)(2) | Landlord knowledge of flooding damage within preceding 5 years | None |

Pinned by `texas_5_year_prior_flooding_triggers_obligation`. The TX 30-day tenant termination right requires: (1) failed disclosure; (2) tenant suffered SUBSTANTIAL LOSS (50%+ of personal property repair/replacement value); (3) termination filed within 30 days of loss. Pinned by `texas_substantial_loss_within_30_days_engages_termination_right` (day 15 = engaged; day 31 = no longer engaged).

**New Jersey unit-threshold matrix — N.J.S.A. 46:8-50**:

| Owner-occupied? | Unit count | Disclosure obligation |
|------------------|------------|------------------------|
| No (investor-owned) | 1-2 | NOT required |
| No (investor-owned) | 3+ | REQUIRED |
| Yes | 1-3 | NOT required |
| Yes | 4+ | REQUIRED |
| Any | Commercial | REQUIRED regardless of unit count |

Pinned by `nj_3_unit_floodplain_triggers_obligation`, `nj_2_unit_no_obligation`, `nj_owner_occupied_4_unit_triggers_obligation`, `nj_owner_occupied_3_unit_no_obligation`, `nj_commercial_property_triggers_regardless_of_units`. NJ uniquely engages the **500-year Moderate Risk Flood Hazard Area** trigger — pinned by `nj_uniquely_engages_500_year_moderate_risk_invariant` (CA + TX only engage 100-year).

**New Jersey format requirements — N.J.S.A. 46:8-50(c)**: separate rider + 12-point minimum typeface + signed/acknowledged by tenant. Pinned by `nj_missing_rider_with_signature_violation`.

**Lease termination right uniquely engages in TX (substantial-loss-conditioned) and NJ (categorical)** — pinned by `nj_failure_to_disclose_engages_lease_termination_right`. CA does not provide statutory lease termination right (general civil + lease damages instead).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-flood-hazard-disclosure`. Thirty-five tests pin: **CA special flood hazard compliant**; **CA no landlord knowledge no obligation**; **CA pre-July 2018 no obligation** (boundary precision); **CA missing disclosure violation**; **CA missing MyHazards URL violation**; **CA missing insurance recommendation violation**; **CA under-8-point type violation**; **Texas floodplain 100-year triggers obligation**; **Texas elevation above floodplain exception lifts obligation**; **Texas 5-year prior flooding triggers obligation**; **Texas substantial loss within 30 days engages termination right** (day 15 engaged; day 31 no longer engaged); **Texas pre-2022 no obligation**; **NJ 3-unit floodplain triggers obligation**; **NJ 2-unit no obligation**; **NJ owner-occupied 4-unit triggers obligation**; **NJ owner-occupied 3-unit no obligation**; **NJ commercial property triggers regardless of units**; **NJ pre-March 2024 no obligation** (boundary); **NJ moderate risk 500-year floodplain triggers obligation**; **NJ missing rider with signature violation**; **NJ failure to disclose engages lease termination right**; **default no obligation**; **citation pins all four jurisdictions**; **note pins CA July 2018 effective date**; **note pins CA MyHazards website**; **note pins CA renter's flood insurance recommendation**; **note pins CA 8-point type requirement**; **note pins Texas January 2022 two-prong framework**; **note pins Texas 30-day substantial loss remedy**; **note pins NJ March 2024 effective date unit threshold**; **note pins NJ 12-point rider signature requirement**; **note pins NJ lease termination remedy**; **note pins FEMA NFIP separate from state disclosure**; **jurisdiction truth table four cells**; **NJ uniquely engages 500-year moderate risk invariant**.

`traderview-expense::rental_energy_benchmarking` is the **rental property energy benchmarking + GHG emissions disclosure compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord owning a large multifamily building annually report energy/water consumption AND comply with carbon emissions caps? Trader-landlord critical for any large multifamily owner in NYC + Boston + other jurisdictions with building-emissions ordinances: failure triggers ongoing per-ton penalties ($268/ton NYC LL97; $300/ton Boston BERDO 2.0) plus annual benchmarking penalties ($2,000/year max NYC LL84). Distinct from siblings `rental_property_registration` (general landlord registration), `rental_gas_appliance_ban` (new construction electrification mandate), and `landlord_annual_rent_statement` (rent disclosure).

**Three regimes** — NYC most aggressive vs Boston BERDO 2.0 vs Default:

| Regime | Benchmarking threshold | Emissions threshold | Per-ton penalty |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| NYC | LL84: >25,000 sq ft OR groups >100,000 sq ft aggregate | LL97 covered buildings | **$268/ton CO2e** |
| Boston BERDO | ≥35,000 sq ft | Net-zero by 2050 | **$300/ton CO2e** |
| Default | Verify local (CA AB 802 = 50,000 sq ft + Seattle BAEDO + DC GBES + Chicago) | Less common | N/A statewide |

**NYC Local Law 84 of 2009 + Local Law 97 of 2019 (Climate Mobilization Act)** — LL84 requires annual energy + water benchmarking via ENERGY STAR Portfolio Manager by May 1st; LL84 penalties $500 missed deadline + $500/quarter additional + $2,000/year max; LL97 requires annual GHG emissions reporting to NYC DOB with $268/metric ton CO2e exceedance penalty. Pinned by `nyc_compliant_passes`, `nyc_under_25000_sqft_not_in_scope` (exactly 25,000 = not in scope), `nyc_at_25001_sqft_in_scope`, `nyc_group_over_100000_sqft_in_scope` (group threshold), `nyc_ll84_missed_deadline_500_dollar_penalty` (50,000 cents = $500), `nyc_ll84_one_quarter_past_1000_dollar_penalty` ($1,000), `nyc_ll84_three_quarters_past_2000_dollar_penalty` ($2,000 cap), `nyc_ll84_capped_at_2000_dollar_annual_max` (100 quarters → still capped at $2,000), `nyc_ll97_emissions_under_cap_no_penalty`, `nyc_ll97_emissions_at_cap_boundary_no_penalty`, `nyc_ll97_emissions_1_ton_over_268_dollar_penalty` (1 ton × $268 = 26,800 cents), `nyc_ll97_emissions_100_tons_over_26800_dollar_penalty` (100 tons × $268 = $26,800), `nyc_combined_ll84_and_ll97_penalties_stack`, `nyc_citation_pins_ll84_ll97`, `nyc_note_pins_268_per_ton_penalty`, and `nyc_note_pins_may_1st_deadline`.

**Boston BERDO 2.0 (Building Emissions Reduction and Disclosure Ordinance, eff. 2021)** — annual energy + water consumption reporting + GHG emissions standards with declining limits through net-zero by 2050; penalties $300/metric ton CO2e above limits (uniquely higher per-ton penalty than NYC). Pinned by `boston_compliant_passes`, `boston_under_35000_sqft_not_in_scope` (34,999 fails threshold), `boston_at_35000_sqft_in_scope` (exactly 35,000 = in scope), `boston_emissions_over_cap_300_dollar_per_ton` (100 tons × $300 = $30,000), `boston_missed_deadline_violates`, `boston_citation_pins_berdo`, `boston_note_pins_300_per_ton_penalty`, and `boston_uniquely_higher_per_ton_penalty_invariant` ($300 Boston > $268 NYC).

**Default — locality-controlled fallback** — no federal benchmarking mandate; most major U.S. cities adopt similar programs with size thresholds 25,000-50,000 sq ft: CA AB 802 (50,000 sq ft statewide), Seattle BAEDO, DC GBES, Chicago Energy Benchmarking, Minneapolis. Emissions caps less common than NYC + Boston. Pinned by `default_no_violations_regardless` (out of scope regardless of inputs), `default_citation_pins_ca_ab_802`, and `three_regimes_routed_correctly`.

**NYC 25,000 sq ft threshold uniquely lower than Boston 35,000 invariant** — 30,000 sq ft building IS in scope NYC; NOT in scope Boston. Pinned by `nyc_unique_25000_sqft_threshold_vs_boston_35000_invariant`.

**Defensive arithmetic** — emissions exceedance via saturating subtraction prevents underflow; emissions cap = 0 + emissions = u32::MAX produces large penalty without overflow. Pinned by `defensive_emissions_under_cap_zero_penalty` and `defensive_emissions_overflow_saturating`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-energy-benchmarking`. Thirty tests pin: **NYC compliant passes**; **NYC under 25,000 sq ft not in scope**; **NYC at 25,001 sq ft in scope**; **NYC group over 100,000 sq ft in scope**; **NYC LL84 missed deadline $500 penalty**; **NYC LL84 one quarter past $1,000 penalty**; **NYC LL84 three quarters past $2,000 penalty**; **NYC LL84 capped at $2,000 annual max**; **NYC LL97 emissions under cap no penalty**; **NYC LL97 emissions at cap boundary no penalty**; **NYC LL97 emissions 1 ton over $268 penalty**; **NYC LL97 emissions 100 tons over $26,800 penalty**; **NYC combined LL84 + LL97 penalties stack**; **Boston compliant passes**; **Boston under 35,000 sq ft not in scope**; **Boston at 35,000 sq ft in scope**; **Boston emissions over cap $300 per ton**; **Boston missed deadline violates**; **default no violations regardless**; **NYC citation pins LL84 + LL97**; **Boston citation pins BERDO**; **default citation pins CA AB 802 + Seattle BAEDO**; **NYC note pins $268 per ton penalty**; **NYC note pins May 1st deadline**; **Boston note pins $300 per ton penalty**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **NYC unique 25,000 sq ft threshold vs Boston 35,000 invariant**; **Boston uniquely higher per-ton penalty invariant** ($300 > $268); **defensive emissions under cap zero penalty**; **defensive emissions overflow saturating** (u32::MAX).

`traderview-expense::rental_broadband_mte_rules` is the **FCC Multiple Tenant Environment (MTE) broadband-access rules module** — when may a trader-landlord lawfully enter into a contract with an internet service provider granting exclusive access + revenue sharing + bulk billing arrangements for residential or commercial multifamily buildings? Trader-landlord critical for any multifamily property considering ISP partnership: an exclusive contract OR an exclusive revenue-sharing agreement OR a graduated revenue-sharing agreement for broadband-only is categorically PROHIBITED under 47 CFR § 64.2500 et seq. and the FCC's 2024 Open Internet Order. Distinct from siblings `tenant_data_privacy`, `tenant_organizing`, `landlord_identification_disclosure`, and `lease_disclosures`.

**Two regimes** — Federal FCC vs Default state law:

| Regime | Authority | Scope |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| Federal FCC | 47 CFR § 64.2500-64.2503 + FCC Open Internet Order FCC 24-52 (July 2024) + FCC 22-12 + FCC 10-49 | Telecom + cable + broadband providers in MTEs |
| Default | Common-law lease analysis + state-specific broadband disclosure (CA SB 1130 + NY HSTPA 2019) | When FCC rules silent |

**Federal FCC arrangement-type truth table** — six contract arrangements, three prohibited for broadband-only:

| Arrangement | Telecom | Cable | Broadband-only |
|-------------|---------|-------|----------------|
| Exclusive access | **PROHIBITED** | **PROHIBITED** | **PROHIBITED** |
| Exclusive revenue-sharing | **PROHIBITED** | **PROHIBITED** | **PROHIBITED** |
| Graduated revenue-sharing | Permitted | Permitted | **PROHIBITED** (Open Internet Order) |
| Flat licensing fee | Permitted | Permitted | Permitted |
| Bulk billing | Permitted | Permitted | Permitted (Carr Jan 2025 withdrew ban) |
| Non-exclusive marketing | Permitted | Permitted | Permitted |

Pinned by `arrangement_truth_table_fcc_mte_broadband` (6-arrangement sweep for broadband-only), `provider_type_distinguishes_graduated_revenue_sharing` (3-provider sweep: telecom + cable permitted; broadband-only prohibited), `fcc_categorical_prohibition_invariant_for_exclusive_access_and_exclusive_revenue` (2-arrangement × 3-provider sweep = all 6 prohibited), `fcc_flat_licensing_fee_lawful_for_all_provider_types` (3-provider sweep), `fcc_bulk_billing_lawful_for_all_provider_types` (3-provider sweep).

**Exclusive access categorical prohibition** — 47 CFR § 64.2500-64.2503 prohibits exclusive access contracts between landlords and providers for telecom + cable + broadband in MTEs. Pinned by `fcc_exclusive_access_prohibited` and `fcc_exclusive_revenue_sharing_prohibited`.

**FCC Open Internet Order FCC 24-52 (July 2024) broadband-only graduated revenue-sharing ban** — graduated (revenue-tied) sharing agreements between landlord and broadband-only ISP are prohibited; flat licensing fees not tied to revenue remain permitted. Pinned by `fcc_broadband_only_graduated_revenue_sharing_prohibited`, `fcc_telecom_graduated_revenue_sharing_lawful` (telecom NOT subject to 2024 ban), `fcc_cable_graduated_revenue_sharing_lawful`, and `fcc_note_pins_open_internet_order_broadband_only`.

**Bulk billing arrangements REMAIN PERMITTED** — FCC 10-49 (2010) confirmed bulk billing is permitted; Chairman Carr withdrew the 2024 proposed bulk-billing ban in January 2025. Bulk billing arrangements where a provider serves every tenant and tenants are billed a prorated share remain lawful. Pinned by `fcc_bulk_billing_lawful` and `fcc_note_pins_bulk_billing_carr_withdrawal` (FCC 10-49 + Carr + January 2025 + REMAIN PERMITTED).

**MTE engagement gate** — FCC rules apply only when the property is a multiple tenant environment (residential or commercial multifamily). Single-tenant properties are not subject to MTE rules. Pinned by `fcc_single_tenant_not_subject_to_mte_rules`, `fcc_mte_required_for_prohibition_invariant` (exclusive access permitted on single-tenant property; prohibited on MTE), and `fcc_uniquely_engages_mte_rules_invariant`.

**Default state law fallback** — common-law lease analysis governs when FCC rules silent; California SB 1130 imposes broadband disclosure obligations on landlords; New York HSTPA 2019 contains broadband access provisions. Pinned by `default_no_fcc_rules_apply` (exclusive access not prohibited at default level since FCC engagement not triggered), and `default_citation_pins_state_disclosure` (CA SB 1130 + NY HSTPA 2019).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-broadband-mte-rules`. Twenty-four tests pin: **FCC flat licensing fee lawful**; **FCC bulk billing lawful**; **FCC non-exclusive marketing lawful**; **FCC exclusive access prohibited** (§ 64.2500); **FCC exclusive revenue sharing prohibited**; **FCC broadband-only graduated revenue sharing prohibited** (FCC 24-52); **FCC telecom graduated revenue sharing lawful**; **FCC cable graduated revenue sharing lawful**; **FCC single tenant not subject to MTE rules**; **FCC citation pins authorities** (§ 64.2500-64.2503 + FCC 24-52 + July 2024 + FCC 22-12 + FCC 10-49); **FCC note pins exclusive access prohibition**; **FCC note pins exclusive revenue sharing prohibition**; **FCC note pins Open Internet Order broadband-only**; **FCC note pins bulk billing Carr withdrawal** (FCC 10-49 + Carr + January 2025 + REMAIN PERMITTED); **default no FCC rules apply**; **default citation pins state disclosure** (CA SB 1130 + NY HSTPA 2019); **two regimes routed correctly**; **arrangement truth table FCC MTE broadband** (6-arrangement sweep); **provider type distinguishes graduated revenue sharing** (3-provider sweep); **FCC uniquely engages MTE rules invariant**; **FCC MTE required for prohibition invariant**; **FCC categorical prohibition invariant for exclusive access and exclusive revenue** (2-arrangement × 3-provider sweep); **FCC flat licensing fee lawful for all provider types**; **FCC bulk billing lawful for all provider types**.

`traderview-expense::rental_hot_water_temperature` is the **rental property minimum hot water temperature compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord supply hot water at a statutorily-specified minimum temperature? Trader-landlord critical for any rental owner: failure to supply hot water at required minimum temperature breaches implied warranty of habitability + triggers tenant remedies (rent withholding + repair-and-deduct + state-specific penalties + HPD/health-department complaints). Distinct from siblings `heat_requirements` (space heat temperature standards), `cooling_requirements`, `lead_in_drinking_water_disclosure`, and `water_heater_earthquake_strap` (CA § 19211 seismic anchoring).

**Three regimes** — California vs NYC vs Default:

| Regime | Authority | Minimum temp | Year-round required |
|--------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| California | Cal. Health & Safety Code §§ 114192, 17920.3 + Cal. Civ. Code § 1941.1 + 22 CCR § 81088 | **120°F** at faucet | No statutory year-round mandate |
| NYC | NYC HMC § 27-2031 (Article 8 Heat and Hot Water) + HPD § 27-2115 | **120°F** | **YES — 365 days per year** |
| Default | International Plumbing Code § 607.1.1 + state habitability | **110°F** at outlet | No federal mandate |

**California 120°F at faucet** — Cal. Health & Safety Code § 114192 requires minimum 120°F at faucet; Cal. Civ. Code § 1941.1 implied warranty of habitability covers hot water supply; 22 CCR § 81088 establishes 105-120°F range for care facilities. Pinned by `ca_125f_compliant` (125°F = compliant), `ca_at_120f_boundary_compliant` (exactly 120°F compliant), `ca_119f_violates` (1°F below threshold violates), `ca_no_hot_water_violates`, `ca_citation_pins_authorities` (§§ 114192, 17920.3 + § 1941.1 + 22 CCR § 81088), `ca_note_pins_120_at_faucet`, `ca_note_pins_warranty_of_habitability`, and `ca_note_pins_22_ccr_81088`.

**NYC 120°F constant 365 days/year** — NYC HMC § 27-2031 (Article 8 Heat and Hot Water) requires constant minimum 120°F supply year-round; failure triggers HPD violation + tenant remedies + civil penalties + § 27-2115 private right of action. Pinned by `nyc_125f_year_round_compliant`, `nyc_at_120f_boundary_compliant` (exactly 120°F compliant), `nyc_119f_violates`, `nyc_seasonal_supply_violates` (year-round requirement), `nyc_no_hot_water_violates`, `nyc_3_violations_stack_at_seasonal_below_no_supply` (2 violations: no supply + not year-round), `nyc_citation_pins_authorities`, `nyc_note_pins_365_days`, `nyc_note_pins_hpd_enforcement`, and `nyc_uniquely_requires_year_round_invariant` (only NYC requires 365-day supply).

**Default IPC § 607.1.1 110°F threshold** — International Plumbing Code § 607.1.1 requires minimum 110°F at outlet; many states default to 120°F via plumbing code adoption; state-specific habitability standards may impose higher minimums. Pinned by `default_110f_compliant`, `default_at_110f_boundary_compliant`, `default_109f_violates` (1°F below IPC threshold violates), `default_119f_below_ca_nyc_threshold_but_compliant` (119°F below CA/NYC 120°F but above IPC 110°F), `default_citation_pins_ipc` (IPC § 607.1.1 + state habitability), `ca_and_nyc_120f_default_110f_threshold_invariant` (CA 120°F + NYC 120°F + Default 110°F).

**Habitability breach engages across all regimes when below minimum or no hot water** — pinned by `habitability_breach_engages_when_below_minimum` (3-regime sweep at 50°F) and `no_hot_water_engages_habitability_breach_all_regimes`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-hot-water-temperature`. `traderview-expense::rental_gas_appliance_ban` is the **rental property gas appliance ban / electrification mandate compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord building NEW CONSTRUCTION OR substantially renovating an existing rental property comply with statutory bans on natural gas / propane hookups and fossil-fuel-burning appliances? Trader-landlord critical for any new-construction or substantial-renovation project in NY (post-January 1, 2026 pending Second Circuit ruling) and CA (2025 Energy Code effective January 1, 2026). DOES NOT require existing landlords to replace existing gas appliances. Distinct from siblings `cooling_requirements` (cooling habitability standards), `heat_requirements` (heat habitability standards), `detector_requirements` (CO + smoke detector requirements), and `landlord_repair_response_timeframe` (repair obligations).

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | New construction trigger | Enforcement status |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| New York | All-Electric Buildings Act, S4006C / A3006C Part RR (2023) | Bans fossil-fuel hookups in MOST new homes | **STAYED pending 2nd Cir. ruling** (fall 2026 expected) |
| California | Title 24 Part 6 (2025 Energy Code) | Heat pumps required for most space + water heating | Active (no stay) |
| Default | Locality-controlled | None statewide; many locality variations | EPCA preemption limits direct bans |

**New York All-Electric Buildings Act (effective January 1, 2026)** — bans fossil-fuel hookups in MOST new homes. Covers natural gas mains + propane tanks + boilers + furnaces + tank/tankless water heaters + gas ranges + gas dryers + gas fireplaces + supply/distribution/delivery piping. State agreed to NOT enforce zero-emissions standard until Second Circuit U.S. Court of Appeals resolves challenge (expected fall 2026). Does NOT apply to existing buildings or existing appliances. Pinned by `ny_new_construction_gas_appliance_violates`, `ny_new_construction_all_electric_compliant`, `ny_existing_building_appliance_replacement_compliant`, `ny_substantial_renovation_does_not_engage_ban` (only NEW CONSTRUCTION engages, not renovation), `ny_maintenance_compliant`, `ny_gas_appliance_violation_message`, `ny_fossil_fuel_piping_violation_message`, `ny_enforcement_stayed_flag`, `ny_citation_pins_act` (S4006C + A3006C + Part RR), `ny_note_pins_january_1_2026`, `ny_note_pins_appliance_list` (boilers + furnaces + water heaters + ranges + dryers + fireplaces), `ny_note_pins_second_circuit_stay`, `ny_new_construction_2_violations_stack` (gas appliance + fossil fuel piping = 2 simultaneous violations), `ny_uniquely_engages_ban_invariant` (only NY engages ban), and `ny_uniquely_enforcement_stayed_invariant`.

**California 2025 Energy Code (effective January 1, 2026; Title 24, Part 6)** — new construction permits require heat pumps for most space and water heating. Does NOT require existing landlords to replace existing gas appliances. San Francisco considering ordinance for major-renovation electrification by 2027 (not yet enacted statewide). Pinned by `ca_new_construction_heat_pump_compliant`, `ca_new_construction_gas_without_heat_pump_violates`, `ca_existing_landlord_no_retroactive_replacement` (replacement on existing building does not engage heat-pump requirement), `ca_citation_pins_title_24`, `ca_uniquely_requires_heat_pump_invariant`, and `ca_note_pins_2027_sf_ordinance`.

**Cal. Restaurant Ass'n v. City of Berkeley, 89 F.4th 1094 (9th Cir. 2023)** — Berkeley's 2019 gas-hookup ban was ENJOINED as federally preempted by Energy Policy and Conservation Act (42 USC § 6297). CA cities post-Berkeley pursue indirect bans via building/health code. Pinned by `ca_note_pins_berkeley_injunction` and `default_note_pins_epca_preemption`.

**Default fallback** — federal law silent on gas appliance bans; locality-controlled with substantial status variation; EPCA § 6297 limits direct-ban approach. Pinned by `default_no_ban_compliant`, `default_citation_pins_epca_preemption`, and `default_note_pins_epca_preemption`.

**4-cell project-type truth tables** — only NewConstruction triggers ban/heat-pump engagement in NY/CA; SubstantialRenovation + AppliancePeplacement + Maintenance do not. Pinned by `project_type_truth_table_ny` and `project_type_truth_table_ca_heat_pump_requirement`. Existing appliance replacement is compliant across ALL regimes. Pinned by `existing_appliance_replacement_compliant_in_all_regimes`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-gas-appliance-ban`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **NY new construction gas appliance violates** (All-Electric Buildings Act); **NY new construction all-electric compliant**; **NY existing building appliance replacement compliant**; **NY substantial renovation does not engage ban**; **NY maintenance compliant**; **NY gas appliance violation message**; **NY fossil fuel piping violation message**; **NY enforcement stayed flag**; **NY citation pins Act** (S4006C + A3006C + Part RR); **NY note pins January 1 2026**; **NY note pins appliance list**; **NY note pins Second Circuit stay**; **CA new construction heat pump compliant**; **CA new construction gas without heat pump violates** (Title 24 Part 6); **CA existing landlord no retroactive replacement**; **CA citation pins Title 24** (2025 Energy Code + 89 F.4th 1094 + § 6297); **CA note pins Berkeley injunction** (EPCA preemption); **CA note pins 2027 SF ordinance**; **default no ban compliant**; **default citation pins EPCA preemption**; **default note pins EPCA preemption**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **NY uniquely engages ban invariant**; **CA uniquely requires heat pump invariant**; **NY uniquely enforcement stayed invariant**; **project type truth table NY** (4-cell sweep); **project type truth table CA heat pump requirement** (4-cell sweep); **NY new construction 2 violations stack**; **existing appliance replacement compliant in all regimes** (3-regime invariant).

`traderview-expense::rental_junk_fee_transparency` is the **rental junk fee + non-rent fee transparency compliance module** — when a residential landlord advertises, leases, or renews a rental, what statutory disclosure obligations attach to fees, charges, or other amounts beyond base rent? Distinct from siblings `application_fees` (application-stage caps), `late_fee_caps` (post-default late charges), `pet_fees` (pet deposits), and `broker_fee_allocation` (broker fee party-allocation). Trader-landlord operational concern in an emerging transparency-regulation wave: MA 940 CMR 38.00 (eff. 2025-09-02), CO HB25-1090 (eff. 2026-01-01), pending federal FTC 16 CFR Part 464.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Total-price-single-number | Utility markup ban | CAM ban | Markup fee cap | Treble damages |
|--------|----------------------------|---------------------|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Massachusetts 940 CMR 38.00 | YES + clear/conspicuous | — | — | — | YES (M.G.L. c. 93A) |
| Colorado § 38-12-1101 (HB25-1090) | YES (single number) | YES | YES | 2% / $10/mo | — |
| California § 1950.5 (AB 12) | — | — | — | — (security cap 1 mo rent) | — |
| Default 16 CFR Part 464 + FTC Act § 5 | (short-term rentals + hotels primarily) | — | — | — | — |

**Massachusetts — 940 CMR 38.00 (effective September 2, 2025)**. Issued March 3, 2025 under M.G.L. c. 93A Consumer Protection Act. Landlords must CLEARLY AND CONSPICUOUSLY disclose the TOTAL PRICE inclusive of ALL fees, charges, or other expenses when the rental is initially advertised. Applies broadly to advertising, leasing, AND renewals. **TREBLE damages + attorney's fees + private right of action** under Chapter 93A. Pinned by `ma_missing_total_price_violates`, `ma_buried_disclosure_violates`, `ma_chapter_93a_treble_damages_note_present`, `ma_effective_date_2025_09_02_note_present`, `ma_advertising_leasing_renewals_scope_note`.

**Colorado — Colo. Rev. Stat. § 38-12-1101 et seq. (HB25-1090, effective January 1, 2026)**. Honest Pricing / Junk Fee Law. Six prohibitions: (1) total price must be SINGLE NUMBER without separation into individual fees; (2) **utility markup ABOVE provider's actual charges prohibited**; (3) **Common Area Maintenance (CAM) charges prohibited** for residential rentals; (4) markup fees may NOT exceed **2% OR $10 per month**, whichever is greater; (5) charges for services NOT actually provided prohibited; (6) costs associated with landlord's responsibilities (property tax, maintenance, insurance) may NOT be passed through as separate tenant fees. Pinned by `co_utility_markup_violates`, `co_cam_charge_violates`, `co_markup_above_2_percent_or_10_violates`, `co_undelivered_service_charge_violates`, `co_landlord_responsibility_passthrough_violates`, `co_separating_fees_in_advertisement_violates`, `co_all_six_violations_simultaneous`.

**California — Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5 (AB 12, effective July 1, 2024)**. Non-rent security capped at ONE month's rent in TPA-covered units. Pending broader junk fee transparency legislation as of 2026-06.

**Default — 16 CFR Part 464 + 15 U.S.C. § 45 (FTC Act § 5)**. Federal FTC Unfair or Deceptive Fees Rule (finalized 2024) primarily covers short-term rentals + hotels; residential long-term rental coverage incomplete. State UDAP statutes may apply for materially misleading advertising.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-junk-fee-transparency`. Twenty-nine tests pin: **MA clean disclosure compliant**; **MA missing total price violates** (940 CMR 38.00); **MA buried disclosure violates** (CLEARLY AND CONSPICUOUSLY); **MA Chapter 93A treble damages note**; **MA effective date 2025-09-02 note**; **MA advertising/leasing/renewals scope note**; **MA citation pins 940 CMR 38.00 + Chapter 93A**; **CO clean disclosure compliant**; **CO utility markup violates**; **CO CAM charge violates**; **CO markup above 2% / $10 violates**; **CO undelivered service charge violates**; **CO landlord responsibility passthrough violates**; **CO separating fees in advertisement violates**; **CO effective date 2026-01-01 note**; **CO all six violations simultaneous**; **CO citation pins § 38-12-1101 + HB25-1090**; **CA AB 12 security deposit cap note** (ONE month's rent in TPA-covered units); **CA no statewide mandate note when missing disclosure**; **CA compliant even without full disclosure**; **default FTC Part 464 note** (short-term rentals); **default FTC Act § 5 note** (15 U.S.C. § 45 UDAP); **default UDAP note when no disclosure**; **default no violations even with aggressive fees**; **CO unique CAM prohibition invariant** (3-other-regime sweep); **CO unique utility markup prohibition invariant** (3-other-regime sweep); **four regimes routed correctly** (MA + CO + CA + Default sweep); **MA compliant clean path no violations**; **CO compliant clean path no violations**.

`traderview-expense::rental_lead_pipe_disclosure` is the **rental property lead service line / lead pipe disclosure compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord operating a property served by a water service line containing lead notify tenants of lead service line presence and replacement plans under EPA Lead and Copper Rule Revisions (LCRR, eff. October 16, 2024) and Lead and Copper Rule Improvements (LCRI, eff. November 1, 2027)? Trader-landlord operational concern: undisclosed lead service line creates breach of warranty of habitability + per-tenant statutory damages + tort liability for lead poisoning + post-Flint heightened regulatory scrutiny. Distinct from siblings `rental_underground_storage_tank_disclosure` (UST), `rental_basement_water_intrusion_disclosure` (water/mold), `rental_sinkhole_disclosure`, federal § 4852d lead-based paint disclosure (paint, not pipes).

**Three regimes** — Federal LCRR/LCRI vs Illinois LSLRNA vs New Jersey Lead Service Line Replacement Act:

**Federal — 40 CFR Part 141 Subpart I (EPA Lead and Copper Rule Revisions + Lead and Copper Rule Improvements) + Safe Drinking Water Act 42 USC § 300f et seq.** — **LCRR compliance date October 16, 2024**: public water systems must complete initial **service line inventory** + notification to persons served of known or potential lead service lines + **Tier 1 public notification within 24 hours** after lead action level exceedance. **LCRI compliance date November 1, 2027**: reduces lead action level to **10 ppb** (from prior 15 ppb); mandates community water systems replace **ALL lead service lines by November 1, 2037** (10-year mandate); information must be accessible to consumers INCLUDING renters and limited English proficiency.

**Illinois — 415 ILCS 5/17.12 (Illinois Lead Service Line Replacement and Notification Act, eff. January 1, 2022)** — community water supplies must maintain lead service line inventory and submit to IL EPA by April 15, 2023; all lead service lines must be replaced on defined population-based schedule (50-year statewide goal); **landlords must provide written notice to tenants of known or potential lead service line within 30 DAYS of receiving water supply notification**; IL EPA Act § 42 civil penalty up to **$50,000 per violation + $10,000 per day continuing**.

**New Jersey — N.J.S.A. 58:12A-40 et seq. (NJ Lead Service Line Replacement Act, P.L. 2021, c.183) + N.J.S.A. 58:10A-10** — all lead service lines must be replaced **within 10 years (by July 2031)**; property owners must disclose lead service line presence to tenants **BEFORE LEASE SIGNING**; NJDEP administers replacement program with cost-sharing between water utility and property owner; civil penalty under § 58:10A-10 up to **$50,000 per violation PER DAY** for failure to replace.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-lead-pipe-disclosure`. Thirty-two tests pin: **federal clean compliant**; **federal no inventory violation** (LCRR service line inventory); **federal no Tier 1 24-hour notice violation**; **federal non-lead no LCRR obligations**; **IL clean compliant**; **IL no tenant notice violation** (415 ILCS 5/17.12); **IL 30-day boundary compliant**; **IL 31-day violation**; **IL penalty at $50K cap compliant**; **IL penalty above $50K cap violation** (IL EPA Act § 42); **NJ clean compliant**; **NJ no pre-lease disclosure violation** (BEFORE LEASE SIGNING); **NJ penalty at $50K per day cap compliant**; **NJ penalty above $50K per day cap violation** (§ 58:10A-10); **NJ galvanized requires replacement disclosure**; **NJ non-lead no disclosure required**; **citation pins federal / IL / NJ authorities** (all three); **note pins LCRR October 2024 compliance date**; **note pins LCRI November 2027 compliance date**; **note pins LCRI 2037 replacement mandate**; **note pins LCRI 10 ppb action level**; **note pins IL 30-day tenant notice**; **note pins IL $50,000 penalty**; **note pins NJ pre-lease disclosure**; **note pins NJ 2031 replacement deadline**; **IL uniquely requires 30-day tenant notice invariant**; **NJ uniquely requires pre-lease disclosure invariant**; **service line status truth table 4 cells**; **multiple IL violations stack** (3 violations); **LCRR and LCRI engaged invariant across regimes**.

`traderview-expense::rental_hoa_disclosure_at_lease` is the **rental property HOA / Condominium Association disclosure at lease signing compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord renting a unit governed by a Homeowners Association (HOA) or Condominium Owners Association (COA) disclose association rules, CC&Rs, fee structure, rental restrictions, and tenant-information sharing to prospective tenants at lease execution and to the association before/after lease execution? Trader-landlord operational concern: undisclosed HOA rules create breach of warranty of quiet enjoyment + tenant rescission claim + per-incident assessment exposure when HOA fines landlord for tenant rule violation. Distinct from siblings `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (screening), `tenant_data_privacy` (general data handling), `rental_property_registration` (state/municipal registration), `short_term_rental_conversion` (STR restrictions).

**Three regimes** — California (Davis-Stirling) vs Florida (§ 720.401 + § 718.503) vs Nevada (NRS § 116.335):

**California — Cal. Civ. Code § 4740 + § 4525 + § 1102 et seq. (Davis-Stirling Common Interest Development Act)** — landlord must provide HOA with **tenant's name and contact information BEFORE lease execution** (§ 4740); landlord may **REDACT** personal and financial information from signed lease before sending to HOA (financial privacy protection); § 4525 — HOA must furnish governing documents to prospective tenant on request; pre-acquisition rental prohibitions enforceable; post-acquisition prohibitions grandfathered for existing owners; § 1102 TDS required at sale.

**Florida — FL Statute § 720.401 + § 718.503 (Condominium Act)** — **HOA Disclosure Summary** required before contract execution; notifies tenant/buyer of (1) HOA membership subject + (2) potential assessments + (3) failure to pay results in lien on property; § 718.503 — condominium association may make **written demand to TENANT** to submit rental payments to association until delinquent balance paid (rent diversion remedy); HOA documents (CC&Rs + rules + fee structure) furnished on request.

**Nevada — Nev. Rev. Stat. § 116 + § 116.335 (Uniform Common-Interest Ownership Act) + § 118A Landlord and Tenant** — association may NOT prohibit unit owner from renting or leasing UNLESS declaration prohibited at **TIME OF PURCHASE**; pre-acquisition restrictions grandfathered; post-acquisition prohibition unenforceable against existing owner; § 118A general landlord-tenant disclosure framework applies in addition to Chapter 116 HOA framework.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-hoa-disclosure-at-lease`. Twenty-eight tests pin: **CA clean compliant**; **CA no tenant info to HOA violation** (§ 4740); **CA sensitive info shared without redaction violation** (financial privacy); **CA pre-acquisition rental prohibition enforceable**; **CA post-acquisition rental prohibition not enforceable**; **CA owner leased despite pre-acquisition prohibition violation** (Davis-Stirling); **FL clean compliant**; **FL no Disclosure Summary violation** (§ 720.401); **FL no CC&Rs/rules furnished violation**; **NV clean compliant**; **NV post-acquisition prohibition unenforceable violation** (§ 116.335); **NV owner leased despite pre-acquisition prohibition violation**; **NV pre-acquisition only enforceable**; **citation pins CA / FL / NV authorities** (all three); **note pins CA tenant info requirement** (BEFORE lease execution); **note pins CA redaction right**; **note pins FL Disclosure Summary three elements** (HOA membership + assessments + lien); **note pins FL Condominium rent diversion** (§ 718.503); **note pins NV § 116.335 pre-acquisition grandfathering** (TIME OF PURCHASE); **note pins NV § 118A landlord-tenant framework**; **CA uniquely requires tenant info to HOA invariant**; **FL uniquely requires Disclosure Summary invariant**; **NV uniquely prohibits post-acquisition restriction invariant**; **pre-acquisition rental prohibition invariant across CA + NV**; **CA redaction right uniquely available invariant**; **multiple CA violations stack** (3 violations).

`traderview-expense::rental_pesticide_application_notification` is the **multi-jurisdictional residential rental pesticide application notification framework module** — trader-landlord critical because pesticide application is routine maintenance (ant colonies, cockroach extermination, termite treatment, mosquito spraying, rodent bait) but failure to satisfy state pre-application notice requirements creates per-violation civil exposure PLUS tenant common-law tort claims for chemical exposure injuries (especially when a tenant has chemical sensitivity, asthma, or pregnancy that the landlord knew or should have known about). Distinct from siblings `rental_carbon_monoxide_detector` (CO sensor framework), `rental_organic_waste_collection_disclosure` (organic waste diversion), `rental_basement_water_intrusion_disclosure` (water damage), `landlord_emergency_entry_notice` (entry without notice).

**Four-jurisdiction statutory framework**:

| Jurisdiction | Source | Required Notice |
|--------------|--------|------------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 1940.8.5 (SB 328 of 2015, effective January 1, 2016) | **24-HOUR** pre-application notice with 5 delivery methods |
| **New York** | NY ECL § 33-1004 + § 33-1005 (Pesticide Neighbor Notification Law + 2020 amendment) | **48-HOUR** notice + visual markers + English/Spanish multilingual requirement |
| **Massachusetts** | Mass. G.L. c. 132B § 6F + § 6I (Children and Families Protection Act of 2000) | **48-HOUR** notice + visual markers + IPM plans |
| **Default** | 40 CFR Part 170 (federal Worker Protection Standard) + EPA FIFRA 7 USC § 136 | No statewide pre-application notice; common-law negligence claims preserved |

Pinned by `ca_24_hour_advance_notice_compliant`, `ny_48_hour_advance_notice_compliant`, `ma_48_hour_advance_notice_compliant`, `default_no_advance_notice_compliant_but_warning_engaged`, `jurisdiction_truth_table_four_cells`, `ca_uniquely_provides_24_hour_standard_invariant` (CA 24 < NY 48 = MA 48), `ny_ma_share_48_hour_standard_invariant`.

**California Civ. Code § 1940.8.5 four-prong notice requirement**:

| Subsection | Element |
|-------------|---------|
| § 1940.8.5(a) | Definitions: pesticide + active ingredient + dwelling unit + authorized agent |
| § 1940.8.5(b) | Required notice content: brand name + active ingredient + scheduled date/time + reasonable application statement + label warnings |
| § 1940.8.5(c) | **24-HOUR** pre-application notice via 5 delivery methods (first-class mail + personal delivery + under-door + electronic + posted) |
| § 1940.8.5(d) | Emergency exception: immediate health/safety threat → post-notice within **1 HOUR after application** |
| § 1940.8.5(e) | Tenant-requested oral agreement exception: must include **PESTICIDE PRODUCT BRAND NAME** |

Pinned by `ca_23_hour_advance_notice_violation` (23 hours = violation), `ca_missing_content_violation`, `ca_emergency_within_one_hour_post_notice_compliant`, `ca_emergency_post_notice_2_hours_violation` (2 hours = violation), `ca_tenant_oral_agreement_with_brand_name_compliant`, `ca_tenant_oral_agreement_without_brand_name_violation`.

**New York ECL 33-1004 + 33-1005 dual-format requirements**:

| Element | Source | Rule |
|---------|--------|------|
| Pre-application notice | NY ECL 33-1004 | **48-HOUR** for commercial lawn applications + residential one-or-two-family dwelling pesticide label |
| Visual notification markers | NY ECL 33-1004 | Signs/flags posted at residential lawn application sites |
| Multilingual notice (2020 amendment) | NY ECL 33-1004 | **English AND Spanish** for commercial lawn applications (plus other language Commissioner deems necessary) |
| Schools | NY ECL | 48-hour notice to registered staff/parents |
| Daycare facilities | NY ECL | 48-hour pre-application notice posted in common area visible to those dropping off/picking up children |

Pinned by `ny_47_hour_advance_notice_violation`, `ny_no_multilingual_notice_violation`, `ny_no_visual_markers_violation`.

**Massachusetts Mass. G.L. c. 132B § 6F + § 6I — Children and Families Protection Act of 2000**: 48-HOUR written notice to occupants of indoor pesticide application + visual notification markers + posted in common area + mandatory IPM (Integrated Pest Management) plans for schools and daycare facilities. Pinned by `ma_under_48_hours_violation`.

**California Bus. & Prof. Code § 8538 parallel structural pest control operator framework** — licensed companies have parallel pre-application notice obligations under SPCB regulations; § 1940.8.5 closes the gap for LANDLORD-applied pesticides.

**EPA FIFRA 7 USC § 136 federal preemption carveout**: federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act preempts state pesticide labeling but PRESERVES state notice and disclosure requirements for landlord-tenant relationships.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-pesticide-application-notification`. Thirty-three tests pin: **CA 24-hour advance notice compliant**; **CA 23-hour advance notice violation** (1-hour boundary precision); **CA missing content violation**; **CA emergency within 1-hour post-notice compliant**; **CA emergency post-notice 2 hours violation**; **CA tenant oral agreement with brand name compliant**; **CA tenant oral agreement without brand name violation**; **NY 48-hour advance notice compliant**; **NY 47-hour advance notice violation**; **NY no multilingual notice violation** (2020 amendment); **NY no visual markers violation**; **MA 48-hour advance notice compliant**; **MA under 48 hours violation**; **default no advance notice compliant but warning engaged**; **jurisdiction truth table four cells**; **NY/MA share 48-hour standard invariant**; **CA uniquely provides 24-hour standard invariant**; **CA emergency only engages with immediate threat**; **CA oral agreement only engages with tenant request**; **citation pins all four jurisdictions** (§ 1940.8.5 + SB 328 + § 8538 + NY ECL § 33-1004 + § 33-1005 + § 33-0903 + § 33-0905 + Mass. c. 132B § 6F + § 6I + 40 CFR Part 170 + EPA FIFRA 7 USC § 136); **note pins CA 24-hour five delivery methods** (first-class mail + personal + under-door + electronic + posted); **note pins CA required content** (brand + active ingredient + warnings); **note pins CA emergency exception 1-hour**; **note pins CA tenant oral agreement brand name**; **note pins NY ECL 48-hour visual markers**; **note pins NY 2020 amendment English+Spanish**; **note pins NY school daycare 48-hour registry**; **note pins MA IPM Children and Families Protection Act**; **note pins default federal floor** (40 CFR Part 170 agricultural + common-law negligence); **note pins CA SPCB § 8538 parallel**; **note pins FIFRA federal preemption carveout**; **defensive zero hours routine non-compliant**; **multiple failures stack**.

`traderview-expense::rental_pet_deposit_separate_security` is the **rental property pet deposit and separate security charge compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord fold pet deposits into the general security deposit cap vs. when may they collect separate pet deposits, pet fees, and pet rent simultaneously? Trader-landlord operational concern: post-2024 California framework folds all pet deposits into a single security deposit cap; misclassifying a "pet deposit" as separate from the security deposit creates per-violation statutory damages and refund liability. Distinct from siblings `pet_fees` (general pet fee framework), `security_deposit_caps` (general security deposit caps), `rental_application_denial_disclosure` (screening).

**Four regimes** — California (single-cap fold-in) vs Washington ($150 pet damage cap + separate non-refundable fee) vs New York (no separate pet category) vs Texas (no cap):

**California — Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5 + AB 12 of 2023 (eff. July 1, 2024) + SB 611 of 2023 (eff. July 1, 2025)** — pet deposits **fold into single security deposit cap**; standard cap **1 month's rent** (furnished or unfurnished, unified post-AB 12); **small-landlord exception 2 months** for natural persons owning ≤ 2 residential rental properties with collectively ≤ 4 units; monthly pet rent permitted as recurring fee IF disclosed upfront in lease; SB 611 prohibits military security deposit surcharge and requires itemized fee disclosure; § 1950.5(g) requires refund within 21 days with itemized statement.

**Washington — RCW 59.18.260 + RCW 59.18.285** — **refundable pet damage deposit capped at $150**; non-refundable pet fee permitted if **CLEARLY LABELED non-refundable AND separate from security deposit**; distinction matters: pet damage deposit (refundable) capped at $150; pet non-refundable fee uncapped but must be labeled non-refundable; monthly pet rent permitted as recurring charge.

**New York — NY GOL § 7-103 (HSTPA of 2019) + NYC Admin Code § 27-2009.1 (NYC Pet Law)** — **ONE security deposit maximum: one month's rent; FULLY REFUNDABLE**; **NO separate pet deposit permitted** (distinct from CA single-cap framework — NY explicitly PROHIBITS pet-labeled deposit category); monthly pet rent permitted only if clearly in lease; **PROHIBITED in rent-stabilized apartments** under NY rent regulation (hidden-rent doctrine); HSTPA 14-day return deadline + itemized statement.

**Texas — Tex. Prop. Code §§ 92.101-92.110** — **no statutory cap** on pet deposit or pet fee; pet deposit + pet fee + pet rent may be charged simultaneously; refundable unless lease specifies non-refundable; § 92.103 — 30-day return after surrender + forwarding address; most permissive regime among comparators.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/rental-pet-deposit-separate-security`.
