Pinned by `ma_strict_law_requires_deleading_with_child_under_6` (federal-compliant + child + no deleading = NOT compliant) + `ma_no_child_under_6_no_state_violation` (no child = MA state rules don't trigger, federal floor alone suffices).

**NJ Lead-Safe Law (P.L. 2021, c.182, effective July 2022)** requires periodic inspections of ALL pre-1978 rentals on a 3-year cycle, regardless of whether a child occupies. Initial inspection deadline was July 22, 2024 (or first tenant turnover if earlier). Pinned by `nj_lead_safe_law_periodic_inspection_required` (no child needed; missing inspection alone is a violation).

**RI / VT inspection-at-occupancy-change** is the third major model. Each new tenant triggers a new inspection requirement; landlord cannot stack inspections by spacing turnovers. Pinned by `ri_inspection_at_change_of_occupancy_required`.

**NY (NYC Local Law 1 of 2004) is the comprehensive case.** Multiple regime flags fire simultaneously (child-trigger + periodic). When both miss, two distinct violations emit. Pinned by `ny_comprehensive_requires_both_periodic_and_child_action` (2 simultaneous violations).

**1978 cutoff is a hard `<` boundary, not `≤`**. Property built in 1978 exactly is post-1978 and gets no federal obligation. Property built 1977 gets the full Title X treatment. Pinned by `property_built_1978_exact_is_post_1978_no_obligation` + `property_built_1977_triggers_federal_floor`.

**Federal-floor-only states do not stack state-side violations.** When state regime = `FederalFloorOnly` (TX / FL / CO / AZ etc., 41 jurisdictions), even missing state-side inputs don't produce violations because the state has no statutory requirement. Pinned by `federal_floor_only_states_no_state_additions` (8-state sweep).

**Post-1978 property skips all state rules too.** Even in MA, a 1980-built rental is exempt from the Massachusetts Lead Law because the law applies only to pre-1978 housing. Pinned by `post_1978_property_state_lead_rules_dont_apply`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lead-disclosure-check`. Twenty-three tests pin: 51-row coverage; **1977/1978/1980 cutoff boundary** (regulatory bright line); each of 4 federal disclosure elements individually triggers a violation; **MA child-under-6 deleading** trigger + no-child case; NJ periodic-inspection regime; RI occupancy-change regime; **NY comprehensive (two simultaneous violations)** when both rules fail; 8-state federal-floor-only sweep; unknown state handled; case-insensitive; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; **child-based-deleading 9-state sweep** (CT/MA/MN/WI/NY/IL/RI/VT/DC); **periodic-inspection 4-state sweep** (NJ/MD/NY/DC); **occupancy-change 4-state sweep** (IL/RI/VT/MD); post-1978 in MA also skips state rules; federal penalty constant matches 40 CFR § 745.118 ($10k/violation); treble-damages flag pinned across 10 states; multiple violations stack (NJ regime: 2 federal + 2 NJ inspections = 4); note describes post-1978 no-obligation case.

`traderview-expense::lead_in_drinking_water_disclosure` is the **mandatory landlord-paid disclosure of LEAD IN DRINKING WATER to tenant module** — when a public water system notifies a landlord that drinking water serving the building exceeds the EPA lead action level (15 µg/L), what disclosure obligation does the landlord owe to tenants? Distinct from siblings `lead_disclosure` (federal Title X lead-based PAINT disclosure), `flood_disclosure`, `radon_disclosure`, `asbestos_disclosure`, and `mold_disclosure`. Trader-landlord operational concern in NJ, MI (Flint legacy), and other states with explicit lead-in-drinking-water statutes.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statutory mandate | Action level | Private right of action |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|---------------------------|
| New Jersey N.J.S.A. 58:12A-12.4 et seq. | 3 business days distribution + prominent posting | Federal 15 µg/L | YES (P.L. 2021 c. 82 + c. 183) |
| Michigan Mich. Comp. Laws § 325.1001 et seq. | Lead Action Level Exceedance Notice distribution | **State 12 µg/L (below federal)** | YES |
| Default | NO statutory landlord distribution mandate | Federal 15 µg/L | NO |

**New Jersey — N.J.S.A. 58:12A-12.4 et seq. (Lead in Drinking Water Notification Act)**. Most explicit statutory framework. Public water system MUST notify landlords when sampling shows lead exceeding action level (15 µg/L). Landlord then MUST (1) distribute the notice to EVERY TENANT within **THREE BUSINESS DAYS** of receipt AND (2) **POST the notice in a PROMINENT LOCATION** accessible to tenants. **P.L. 2021, c. 82** and **P.L. 2021, c. 183** amended the framework to include private right of action + civil penalties. NJ Department of Environmental Protection (NJ DEP) enforces.

**Michigan — Mich. Comp. Laws § 325.1001 et seq. (Safe Drinking Water Act post-Flint amendments)**. Post-Flint framework. Michigan Lead and Copper Rule (effective 2018, MAC R 325.10101 et seq.) sets **STATE action level at 12 µg/L** — BELOW the federal 15 µg/L action level. Landlord must distribute Lead Action Level Exceedance Notice when public water supply reports lead above action level. State enforcement + private right of action.

**Default — federal SDWA + EPA Lead and Copper Rule**. 42 U.S.C. § 300f et seq. + 40 CFR Part 141.85 federal floor only. Public water system MUST deliver Consumer Confidence Report (CCR) annually + issue Lead Public Education Statements when action level exceeded. **NO statutory landlord-tenant distribution mandate at federal level** — landlord's obligation governed by state-specific lead disclosure / habitability framework. State UDAP may reach landlord failure to disclose known lead in drinking water. Common-law negligence per se for pediatric lead poisoning claims.

**Michigan unique lower action level**. Michigan state action level (12 µg/L) is BELOW federal action level (15 µg/L), creating a wider zone of mandatory disclosure than other states. Pinned by `michigan_uniquely_lower_action_level_invariant` and boundary truth tables (`federal_action_level_boundary_truth_table` 5-cell sweep at 13/14/15/16/17 µg/L + `michigan_action_level_boundary_truth_table` 5-cell sweep at 11/12/13/14/15 µg/L).

**NJ unique three-business-day requirement**. Only NJ specifies the strict 3-business-day distribution deadline. MI uses general "must distribute" without specific timing. Default imposes no distribution mandate. Pinned by `nj_three_business_day_unique_invariant` (3-regime sweep).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lead-in-drinking-water-disclosure`. Thirty-two tests pin: **NJ clean compliance passes**; **NJ missing distribution violates** (§ 58:12A-12.4 THREE BUSINESS DAYS); **NJ missing posting violates** (§ 58:12A-12.4 PROMINENT LOCATION); **NJ private water no disclosure obligation** (only public water system triggers); **NJ no lead notice no obligation**; **NJ federal action level 15 µg/L exceeded** (>15 triggers); **NJ at 15 boundary not exceeded** (strict greater-than); **NJ P.L. 2021 amendments note present** (c. 82 + c. 183); **NJ private right of action available**; **NJ citation pins NJSA + amendments + N.J.A.C. 7:10**; **MI clean compliance passes**; **MI action level 12 below federal note**; **MI at 13 state exceeded federal not**; **MI at 12 boundary not exceeded**; **MI at 16 both exceeded**; **MI missing both distribution and posting violates**; **MI citation pins state Lead and Copper Rule**; **default compliant always** (no landlord distribution mandate); **default no landlord distribution mandate note**; **default CCR note when missing CCR** (40 CFR Part 141 Subpart O system-side violation); **default state UDAP and negligence note**; **default no private right of action**; **default citation references federal SDWA + LCR**; **three regimes routed correctly**; **NJ three-business-day unique invariant**; **NJ unique private right of action invariant**; **Michigan uniquely lower action level invariant** (state 12 vs federal 15); **NJ both violations simultaneous**; **federal action level boundary truth table** (5-cell sweep 13/14/15/16/17); **Michigan action level boundary truth table** (5-cell sweep 11/12/13/14/15); **NJ clean no violations**; **MI clean no violations**.

`traderview-expense::lead_renovation_repair_painting` is the **EPA Lead Renovation, Repair, and Painting (RRP) Rule compliance check** — 40 CFR Part 745, Subpart E. Critical for any trader-landlord renovating older multifamily property — TSCA § 16(a) penalties are PER-DAY-PER-VIOLATION and have triggered EPA's largest-ever lead-paint enforcement actions. Distinct from `lead_disclosure` (TSCA § 1018 / 40 CFR Part 745 Subpart F — initial lead-based paint disclosure upon SALE or LEASE), `asbestos_disclosure`, and `bedbug_extermination_cost` — this module addresses ONLY the RENOVATION WORK PRACTICES pathway when work disturbs lead-based paint in pre-1978 housing or a child-occupied facility.

**Two jurisdictional regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | States | Firm registration |
|--------|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| **EPA Federal** | 40 CFR Part 745 Subpart E + TSCA § 16(a) | ~35 states + DC | EPA online application |
| **State-Authorized** | TSCA § 404 (15 USC § 2684) delegation | 15 EPA-delegated states: WI, IA, NC, MS, KS, RI, UT, OR, MA, AL, WA, GA, OK, DE, VT | State lead program |

State-authorized programs MUST be AT LEAST AS PROTECTIVE as the federal floor — the 8 compliance elements are universal regardless of jurisdiction.

**Eight compliance elements** (all required when rule applies):

| # | Element | Authority |
|---|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Target housing (pre-1978) OR child-occupied facility | § 745.83 definitions |
| 2 | Work disturbs paint above de minimis (>6 sq ft interior / >20 sq ft exterior / window replacement / demolition) | § 745.83 minor-repair-and-maintenance exemption |
| 3 | Firm EPA or state-authorized certified | §§ 745.89, 745.90 |
| 4 | Individual renovator trained (EPA-accredited 8-hour course; 5-year validity; 4-hour refresher) | § 745.90 |
| 5 | "Renovate Right" pamphlet provided to owner + occupant + signed acknowledgment or certificate of mailing retained | § 745.84 |
| 6 | Containment used (plastic sheeting + signage) + prohibited work practices avoided (no open-flame burning, no machine sanding/grinding without HEPA, no heat guns above 1100°F) | § 745.85(a)(1), (a)(3) |
| 7 | Cleanup verification completed (visual inspection + dust-cleanup verification cards or clearance testing) | § 745.85(b) |
| 8 | Records retained 3 years post-renovation | § 745.86 |

**Two-prong applicability gate kills RRP at the gate.** If the building is NOT target housing (pre-1978) OR child-occupied facility, the rule doesn't apply — no compliance required regardless of work scope. If work falls within the § 745.83 minor-repair-and-maintenance threshold (≤ 6 sq ft interior / ≤ 20 sq ft exterior / no window replacement / no demolition), the rule also doesn't apply. Pinned by `post_1978_housing_rule_does_not_apply`, `minor_repair_below_de_minimis_rule_does_not_apply`, `neither_target_housing_nor_above_de_minimis_rule_does_not_apply`, and `rule_applies_when_target_housing_and_above_de_minimis`.

**TSCA § 16(a) civil penalty up to $37,500 per day per violation** at statutory base (15 USC § 2615(a)(1)). Inflation-adjusted maximum under 40 CFR § 19.4 is materially higher (mid-$40K range as of 2025; 2026 adjustment canceled by OMB so 2025 figure persists). Criminal penalties under TSCA § 16(b) available for knowing or willful violations — up to $25,000/day plus imprisonment. The penalty note surfaces in every applicable-rule result. Pinned by `note_pins_tsca_section_16_penalty`.

**8-element invariant — any single gap defeats compliance.** Pinned by `all_eight_elements_must_be_satisfied_invariant` (8-iteration sweep flipping each element to false confirms each individually breaks compliance) and `multiple_violations_accumulate_all_listed` (3 simultaneous gaps surface 3+ violations).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/lead-renovation-repair-painting`. Twenty-two tests pin: **full compliance federal passes**; **full compliance state-authorized passes**; **post-1978 housing rule does not apply** (target housing gate); **minor repair below de minimis rule does not apply** (work-scope gate); **missing firm certification violation** (§§ 745.89, 745.90); **missing individual training violation** (§ 745.90 8-hour); **missing pamphlet distribution violation** (§ 745.84 Renovate Right); **missing pamphlet acknowledgment violation**; **pamphlet not provided skips acknowledgment check** (cascade); **missing containment violation** (§ 745.85(a)(1) plastic sheeting); **prohibited work practices violation** (§ 745.85(a)(3) open-flame burning); **missing cleanup verification violation** (§ 745.85(b) clearance testing); **missing records retention violation** (§ 745.86 3 years); **note pins TSCA § 16(a) $37,500/day penalty**; **federal jurisdiction note describes EPA application**; **state-authorized note lists 15 states** (WI, IA, NC, MS, KS, RI, UT, OR, MA, AL, WA, GA, OK, DE, VT); **citation federal pins Part 745 Subpart E + TSCA § 16(a) + 40 CFR § 19.4**; **citation state-authorized pins TSCA § 404 + 15 USC § 2684 + federal floor**; **all 8 elements must be satisfied invariant** (8-iteration sweep); **multiple violations accumulate all listed**; **rule applies when target housing and above de minimis**; **neither target housing nor above de minimis rule does not apply**.

`traderview-expense::detector_requirements` is the **state-by-state smoke + carbon monoxide detector compliance table** — life-safety obligation with massive liability exposure (tenant death from non-functional CO detector = wrongful death suit + criminal exposure in some states). Sibling to `soi_protection`, `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`.

**All 50 states require smoke detectors in residential rental units.** The variation lives in placement rules (every bedroom vs outside sleeping areas vs every level), power source (10-year sealed battery vs replaceable battery), and landlord-install obligations at occupancy.

**Three CO detector regimes:**

| Regime                              | States                                                                  |
|-------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **All units (universal)**           | CO / CT / DC / IL / MA / MD / ME / MN / NH / NJ / NY / OR / RI / VT / WA (15 jurisdictions) |
| **Only with fuel source or garage** | CA / AZ / DE / FL / GA / HI / IA / KS / KY / LA / MI / MT / NC / NE / NM / NV / OH / PA / TN / TX / UT / VA / WI / AK + ~10 others |
| **No statewide CO (smoke only)**    | AL / AR / ID / IN / MO / MS / ND / OK / SC / SD / WV / WY                |

**CA model carve-out is load-bearing.** The CA rule under Health & Safety Code § 13113.7 / SB 745 requires CO detectors only when the property has a fossil-fuel-burning appliance OR an attached garage. Property without either (e.g., all-electric, detached garage) doesn't trigger the CO requirement at all. Pinned by `california_co_only_with_fuel_or_garage` (no fuel + no garage = no CO required, complies) + `california_co_required_with_garage_only` (garage alone triggers) + `california_co_required_with_fuel_only` (fuel alone triggers). The "OR" between the two conditions is the load-bearing logic.

**10-year sealed battery requirement** is the modern wave — CA SB 745 (2014, replacement triggers), NY NYC Local Law 111, MD, OR. Sealed unit prevents the "battery removed at 3am because it was chirping" failure mode that drives most tenant deaths from CO poisoning and fires. Pinned by `ten_year_battery_states_pinned` (4-state sweep) + the per-state violation tests for CA and NY.

**Smoke in every bedroom is rarer than outside-sleeping-area placement.** Only CA, TX, and WI require smoke in every bedroom in the table. All states require smoke outside each sleeping area. Pinned by `texas_smoke_in_every_bedroom_required` (TX-specific) + `smoke_outside_sleeping_area_universal_violation_check` (8-state sweep).

**Universal smoke + landlord-install** — every row has `smoke_in_every_bedroom_installed` or `outside_each_sleeping_area` checked AND `landlord_install_at_occupancy = true`. The landlord-install obligation means the landlord must verify detectors are present and working at occupancy; tenant takes over routine battery replacement after that.

**Violations list distinguishes missing-detector from wrong-placement.** Multiple violations stack — pinned by `multiple_violations_listed_in_result` (3 simultaneous violations all listed individually). Note text reports the count.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/detector-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA fully compliant; **CA OnlyWithFuelOrGarage carve-out** (no fuel + no garage → CO not required); CA CO triggered by garage alone; CA CO triggered by fuel alone; NY AllUnits regime (CO always required regardless of fuel); MA AllUnits same; IL every-level-with-sleeping rule (CO on each level violation); TX every-bedroom smoke rule (TX-specific); **CA 10-year sealed battery violation**; **NY NYC Local Law 111 sealed battery** violation; MO smoke-only no-CO regime (NoStatewide); smoke-outside-sleeping universal sweep; landlord-install-at-occupancy violation; CO model with no-fuel/no-garage produces no violation; multiple violations stack in list; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; **CO-only states pinned** (8-state regime sweep); **CO AllUnits states pinned** (15-state regime sweep); **NoStatewide states pinned** (12-state sweep); 10-year sealed battery states pinned.

`traderview-expense::snow_removal_responsibility` is the **state snow/ice removal landlord responsibility compliance check** — addresses one of the most heavily litigated premises-liability topics in cold-weather states. Three jurisdictions have meaningfully different rules with sharply different liability postures: Massachusetts abolished the natural-vs-unnatural accumulation distinction and imposes reasonable-care duty; Illinois grants statutory IMMUNITY to residential owners who voluntarily clear public sidewalks; New York City mandates a 4-hour removal window by municipal ordinance.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Landlord duty | Timeline | Tenant delegation valid? | Immunity? |
|--------|-----------|----------------|----------|----------------------------|-----------|
| `Massachusetts` | Papadopoulos v. Target Corp. 457 Mass. 368 (2010) + 105 CMR 410.452 | **Reasonable care** + primary means-of-egress duty | None statutory (reasonable) | **Only if** single-family + tenant has independent private entrance | No |
| `Illinois` | 745 ILCS 75/ Snow and Ice Removal Act (1979) | **None statutory** | None | Per lease | **YES** on public sidewalk (residential, voluntary, non-willful) |
| `NewYorkCity` | NYC Admin Code § 16-123 | Mandatory removal | **4 hours** after snow stops (except 9 PM–7 AM) | Only single-family; owner still gets ticket | No |
| `Default` | — | Common-law habitability | None | Per lease | No |

**Three regression-critical distinguishers**:
- **Only IL has voluntary-clearing immunity** — IL Snow Removal Act 745 ILCS 75/ shields landlords who voluntarily clear public sidewalks from liability unless willful/wanton. MA + NYC + Default impose liability for negligent clearing.
- **Only NYC has 4-hour timeline** — § 16-123 sets a concrete statutory clock; MA + IL + Default have no statutory timeline.
- **Only MA requires independent-entrance for valid delegation** — MA Sanitary Code 105 CMR 410.452 bars landlord delegation of means-of-egress duty unless tenant has independent private entrance. NYC permits single-family delegation regardless of entrance (but owner still gets ticket).

**Massachusetts Papadopoulos invariant**: pre-2010, MA distinguished "natural" snow (no landlord duty) from "unnatural" accumulation (duty). The 2010 SJC decision ELIMINATED this distinction — landlords now owe reasonable care to all lawful visitors regardless of natural vs unnatural accumulation.

**Illinois immunity carve-outs**: immunity does NOT extend to (a) private property (driveway, walkway to front door, garage entrance), (b) willful or wanton conduct, or (c) failures to correct dangerous conditions that cause accumulation on the public sidewalk.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/snow-removal-responsibility`. Twenty-one tests pin: **MA means-of-egress landlord duty + uncleared violation** + Papadopoulos citation; **MA natural-accumulation-distinction-abolished** regression (12-hour-uncleared still violates regardless of natural); **MA lease delegation invalid in multi-unit** + delegation_to_tenant_valid = false; **MA lease delegation VALID single-family with independent private entrance**; **IL public-sidewalk immunity applies** + 745 ILCS 75/ citation (regression-critical — 12-hour-uncleared no violation under IL Act); **IL willful/wanton overrides immunity** + WillfulOrWantonOverrideImmunity violation; **IL private property no immunity** + "does NOT extend to private property" citation; **IL private property cleared compliant**; **NYC at 4-hour boundary compliant** (> strict required); **NYC at 5 hours violation** + § 16-123 + "4 hours" citation; **NYC multi-unit invalid delegation** + violation; **NYC single-family delegation valid**; **default no-specific-obligation** + "common-law habitability" citation; **jurisdiction routing MA/IL/NYC (NY-Buffalo → Default) + case-insensitive**; **only-IL-has-voluntary-clearing-immunity** 4-regime invariant; **only-NYC-has-4-hour-timeline** 3-regime invariant; **only-MA-requires-independent-entrance-for-delegation** (regression-critical — MA single-family-without-independent-entrance bars delegation but NYC permits); **citations pin authorities** (Papadopoulos + 745 ILCS 75/ + § 16-123).

`traderview-expense::soft_story_seismic_retrofit` is the **mandatory soft-story seismic retrofit ordinance compliance module** — when must a trader-landlord owning a wood-frame multifamily building with a soft/weak first story (open ground-floor parking, retail, or similar large openings lacking lateral bracing) commission a mandatory structural retrofit? Trader-landlord critical for CA multifamily owners: non-compliance exposes owner to ordinance penalties + uninhabitable-building findings + insurance non-renewal. Distinct from siblings `balcony_inspection` (SB 721 / SB 326 Exterior Elevated Elements visual inspection), `water_heater_earthquake_strap` (CA § 19211 individual appliance seismic anchoring), and `fire_sprinkler_disclosure` (fire-suppression system disclosure).

**Four regimes** — three CA cities with distinct frameworks plus default:

| Regime | Authority | Unit threshold | Compliance timeline | Penalty |
|--------|-----------|----------------|---------------------|---------|
| San Francisco | Building Code Chapter 34B (Ord. 66-13, operative June 17, 2013) | **5+ residential units** + 2+ stories over soft story | All deadlines PASSED September 15, 2021 | ~$840/day + SF § 102A.16 UNSAFE designation |
| Los Angeles | Ordinance 183893 (Nov 22, 2015) | None (any wood-frame soft story) | **3-phase staged** (2yr / 3.5yr / 7yr) | LAMC § 91.6314 Order to Comply + civil + criminal misdemeanor + recordation |
| Berkeley | BMC Chapter 19.39 (eff. 2015) | **3+ residential units** + soft-story first floor | Ongoing certification | BMC enforcement |
| Default | Common-law premises liability + local | N/A | N/A | N/A statutory |

**San Francisco 5+ unit + 2+ stories threshold** — wood-frame structures with 5 or more residential units AND 2 or more stories OVER a soft/weak story. All tier compliance deadlines passed as of September 15, 2021; non-compliance triggers SF Building Code § 102A.16 UNSAFE designation and ~$840/day penalty. Pinned by `sf_compliant_passes`, `sf_4_units_out_of_scope`, `sf_5_units_in_scope_boundary` (exactly 5), `sf_not_wood_frame_out_of_scope`, `sf_no_soft_story_out_of_scope`, `sf_in_scope_not_retrofit_complete_violates`, `sf_uniquely_requires_5_units_invariant` (SF requires 5+; Berkeley requires only 3+), `sf_citation_pins_ordinance` (Chapter 34B + Ord. 66-13 + June 17, 2013), `sf_note_pins_5_unit_threshold`, and `sf_note_pins_840_per_day_penalty` (§ 102A.16).

**Los Angeles 3-phase staged compliance** — Ordinance 183893 (Nov 22, 2015) covers ~13,500 wood-frame soft-story buildings with staged deadlines:
- **2 years**: structural report + retrofit/demolition plans
- **3.5 years**: secure all required permits
- **7 years**: complete retrofit or demolition + close all permits

Priority 2 deadline: **April 2026**. Pinned by `la_compliant_at_year_8_retrofit_complete`, `la_year_1_structural_report_compliant`, `la_year_1_not_started_violates`, `la_year_2_boundary_structural_report_engages`, `la_year_3_permits_required`, `la_year_3_only_structural_report_violates`, `la_year_4_retrofit_complete_required`, `la_year_8_retrofit_complete_required`, `la_year_8_permits_secured_violates`, `la_no_unit_threshold_3_units_in_scope` (LA has no unit minimum), `la_citation_pins_ordinance_183893` (LAMC § 91.6314), `la_note_pins_april_2026_priority_2_deadline`, `la_note_pins_3_phase_timeline`, and `la_uniquely_has_staged_compliance_invariant` (SF + Berkeley = single completion stage; LA = 3-phase).

**Berkeley 3+ unit lower threshold** — BMC Chapter 19.39 (eff. 2015) applies to wood-frame multifamily structures with 3 or more units (lower than SF's 5+ threshold) AND soft-story first floor; ongoing certification requirement. Pinned by `berkeley_3_unit_boundary_in_scope` (exactly 3), `berkeley_2_units_out_of_scope`, `berkeley_not_complete_violates`, `berkeley_citation_pins_bmc`, and `berkeley_uniquely_lower_3_unit_threshold_invariant` (Berkeley catches 3-unit buildings that SF doesn't).

**Default fallback** — no statutory soft-story retrofit requirement; common-law premises liability + state building code (where adopted) + local ordinances may apply. Analogous programs exist in Oakland + San Jose + Pasadena + West Hollywood. Pinned by `default_no_violation`, `default_citation_pins_local_ordinances`, and `default_note_pins_other_ca_cities` (Oakland + San Jose + Pasadena + West Hollywood).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/soft-story-seismic-retrofit`. Thirty-three tests pin: **SF compliant passes**; **SF 4 units out of scope**; **SF 5 units in scope boundary**; **SF not wood frame out of scope**; **SF no soft story out of scope**; **SF in scope not retrofit complete violates** (§ 102A.16); **SF citation pins ordinance** (Chapter 34B + Ord. 66-13 + June 17, 2013); **SF note pins 5-unit threshold**; **SF note pins $840 per day penalty** (§ 102A.16); **LA compliant at year 8 retrofit complete**; **LA year 1 structural report compliant**; **LA year 1 not started violates**; **LA year 3 permits required**; **LA year 3 only structural report violates**; **LA year 8 retrofit complete required**; **LA year 8 permits secured violates**; **LA no unit threshold 3 units in scope**; **LA citation pins Ordinance 183893** (LAMC § 91.6314); **LA note pins April 2026 Priority 2 deadline**; **LA note pins 3-phase timeline** (2yr / 3.5yr / 7yr); **Berkeley 3-unit boundary in scope**; **Berkeley 2 units out of scope**; **Berkeley not complete violates** (Chapter 19.39); **Berkeley citation pins BMC**; **default no violation**; **default citation pins local ordinances**; **default note pins other CA cities**; **four regimes routed correctly**; **SF uniquely requires 5 units invariant**; **LA uniquely has staged compliance invariant**; **Berkeley uniquely lower 3-unit threshold invariant**; **LA year 2 boundary structural report engages**; **LA year 4 retrofit complete required**.

`traderview-expense::soi_protection` is the **state-by-state source-of-income (SOI) discrimination protection table** — sibling to `just_cause_eviction`, `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. The **federal Fair Housing Act does NOT cover source of income** — landlords may refuse Section 8 voucher holders nationwide unless a state or local statute provides protection. ~18-20 states + DC + many cities have enacted SOI protection since CT (1989) became the first.

**Three regimes:**

| Regime                              | States                                                                       |
|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Statewide full protection**       | CA / CO / CT / DC / DE / IL / MA / MD / ME / MN / NJ / NM / OR / RI / VA / VT / WA (17 jurisdictions) |
| **Statewide challenged / partial**  | NY (HSTPA 2019 struck down at appellate level March 2026 — NYC local survives) / UT (veterans only) |
| **No statewide (federal floor only)** | 33 other states; local ordinances in GA (Atlanta), PA (Philadelphia/Pittsburgh), TN (Memphis), TX (Austin), WI (Madison/Dane County) |

**Historical waves:**

| Wave            | States                                  |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Pre-2000 pioneers | DC (1977), CT/MA/VT (1989), RI (1996) |
| 2002-2014       | NJ (2002), ME (2009), OR (2014)         |
| 2018 wave       | WA (2018)                               |
| 2019 wave       | CA SB 329 (2019), NY HSTPA (2019)       |
| 2020 wave       | CO / DE / MD / VA — racial-equity / fair-housing push |
| 2023 wave       | IL / MN / NM — most recent batch        |

**`verify_current_status_needed` flag** is load-bearing. NY's SOI provision was struck down at the appellate level in March 2026 — statewide enforcement is contested but NYC Human Rights Law still applies. UT § 13-21-302 covers veterans only, not full voucher population. Both flag `verify_current_status_needed: true` to tell the UI to surface "check current court status + locality" rather than reporting a definitive answer. Pinned by `new_york_challenged_regime_verify_needed` and `utah_partial_only_veterans` + the `challenged_states_set_verify_needed_flag` sweep.

**Voucher type doesn't change the answer.** Section 8 HCV, HUD-VASH, FUP, and other federal/state subsidy vouchers all get the same state-law treatment — the SOI statute classifies "source of income" without distinguishing voucher programs. The `voucher_type` enum is captured on input for future-proofing if a state someday differentiates. Pinned by `voucher_type_does_not_change_state_law_outcome` (CA returns identical results across all four voucher types).

**Local-only ordinances surface in the citation.** GA, PA, TN, TX, WI all have `NoProtection` regime statewide but mention local ordinances (Atlanta / Philadelphia / Memphis / Austin / Madison) in the citation field. Caller must check municipal law for these states. Pinned by `local_only_states_flagged_in_citation`.

**Unknown state defaults to federal floor (landlord may refuse).** Safest assumption — if we don't have the state in the table, federal FHA doesn't cover SOI so the landlord can refuse. Pinned by `unknown_state_defaults_to_federal_floor`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/soi-protection-check`. Eighteen tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA full protection (landlord may NOT refuse); TX no statewide (landlord may refuse, federal floor only); CT 1989 oldest statewide; OR 2014 first post-2010; **NY challenged regime sets verify flag**; UT partial-only-veterans sets verify flag; **17-state full-protection sweep** (catches regime-flag regression); voucher_type doesn't change outcome (all four types same); unknown state defaults to federal floor; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; **2023 wave** (IL/MN/NM all enacted 2023); **2020 wave** (CO/DE/MD/VA); pre-2000 pioneers (CT/MA/VT/RI/DC); local-only-states flagged in citation (GA/PA/TN/TX/WI); `verify_current_status_needed` flag set for both NY and UT.

`traderview-expense::immigration_status_protection` is the **state Immigrant Tenant Protection landlord compliance check** — two states (California AB 291 2017 + Illinois Immigrant Tenant Protection Act 2019) have enacted statutes prohibiting landlords from weaponizing tenants' immigration status. The federal Fair Housing Act bars national-origin discrimination but does NOT specifically protect against immigration-status-related threats — these state laws fill that gap.

**Three regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Prohibited acts | Application-inquiry prong | Warrant carve-out | Criminal prosecution | $/violation |
|--------|---------|-----------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| `CaliforniaAb291` | Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1940.05, 1940.2, 1940.3, 1942.5 | Disclosure + threat + eviction + retaliation + **application inquiry** | **Yes** (§ 1940.3, CA-only) | **Yes** (§ 1940.2 — judge-signed warrant/subpoena in criminal investigation) | **Yes** (AG/DA) | $2,000 |
| `Illinois` | 765 ILCS 755/ (eff. 2019-08-23) | Disclosure + threat + eviction + retaliation | No | No | No | $2,000 |
| `Default` | — | None statutorily | — | — | — | $0 |

**Three CA-only prongs distinguish CA from IL**: § 1940.3 application-inquiry prohibition; § 1940.2 judicial-warrant carve-out (only CA allows disclosure under judge-signed warrant/subpoena); criminal prosecution by AG/DA. All three are CA-specific; IL has none of them.

**Both states share $2,000 per-violation civil penalty + actual damages + attorney fees**. CA additionally permits criminal charges; IL adds equitable relief.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/immigration-status-protection`. Twenty-three tests pin: **CA disclosure to ICE violation** + § 1940.2 + $2,000 penalty regression; **CA judicial-warrant carve-out excuses disclosure** + "judicial warrant" citation; **CA threat to disclose violation** + AB 291 citation; **CA threat NOT excused by warrant** (regression — warrant only excuses disclosure, not threats); **CA eviction-based-on-status violation** + § 1942.5; **CA retaliation violation**; **CA application-inquiry violation** + § 1940.3 + "CA-only" citation regression; **CA criminal prosecution available**; **IL disclosure violation** + 765 ILCS 755/ citation; **IL threat violation**; **IL eviction violation** + § 5 citation; **IL application-inquiry NOT prohibited** (regression — IL lacks the § 1940.3-equivalent application prong); **IL no judicial-warrant carve-out** (regression — disclosure violates even with warrant); **IL no criminal prosecution**; **IL $2,000 civil penalty**; **default no obligation** + FHA citation; **state routing CA/IL/Default**; **case-insensitive lookup**; **only CA has application-inquiry prong** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **only CA has judicial-warrant carve-out** (single-test 3-regime invariant — same warrant input only excuses CA disclosure); **only CA has criminal prosecution** (single-test 3-regime invariant); **CA and IL both $2,000 civil penalty** (invariant); **citations pin authorities** (§ 1940.2 + § 1940.3 + § 1942.5 + 765 ILCS 755/).

`traderview-expense::reasonable_accommodation_modification` is the **Federal FHA + state reasonable-accommodation / reasonable-modification compliance check for tenants with disabilities** — implements the two-prong duty under 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3) and the state-level overlays that expand or harden it. Distinct from `service_animal` (which is one specific accommodation flavor) and from `senior_disabled_protection` (which is age-based / income-based eviction protection, not the FHA disability framework).

**Two statutory prongs (federal floor, applies across all four regimes)**:

| Prong | Statute | Cost borne by | Denial threshold |
|-------|---------|---------------|------------------|
| **Modification** — physical change to unit/common area | 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(A) | **Tenant** | Restoration agreement permissible **only** if modification would materially impact the next occupant (per 2008 HUD Joint Statement on Reasonable Modifications) |
| **Accommodation** — change to rule, policy, practice, or service | 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(B) | **Landlord** | Undue financial/administrative burden **OR** fundamental alteration of the program |

**Four-regime overlay**:

| Regime | Statute | Unique requirement |
|--------|---------|--------------------|
| `Federal` | 42 U.S.C. § 3604(f)(3)(A)–(B) | Baseline; HUD Joint Statements (2004 accommodation + 2008 modification) |
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 54.1(b)(3)(A)–(B) + § 54(b) | **Escrow capped at reasonable estimate of restoration cost**; no additional security may be required; § 54(b) sensory-disability coverage **broader than ADA** (blind, visually-impaired, deaf, hearing-impaired) |
| `NYC` | N.Y.C. Admin. Code § 8-107(15)(c) + § 8-102 | **MANDATORY cooperative-dialogue requirement** — landlord MUST engage in documented written/oral dialogue; failure to engage is itself a discriminatory practice independent of substantive outcome |
| `Washington` | RCW 49.60.222(2)(b) | Mirrors FHA cost allocation (modification = tenant pays; accommodation = landlord pays) |

**The NYC cooperative-dialogue mandate is the unique strict requirement.** N.Y.C. § 8-107(15)(c) is the only regime among the four that imposes an affirmative duty on the landlord to engage in a documented dialogue. The dialogue must occur within a reasonable time after the request is received (audit threshold pinned at `NYC_DIALOGUE_REASONABLE_TIME_DAYS = 30`). Failure to engage is the discriminatory act itself — even if the underlying request would have been denied on undue-burden grounds. Pinned by `nyc_failure_to_engage_dialogue_is_violation`, `nyc_dialogue_beyond_reasonable_time_is_violation`, `nyc_dialogue_at_reasonable_time_boundary_compliant`, `nyc_dialogue_failure_independent_of_substantive_grant`, and the multi-regime invariant `only_nyc_imposes_cooperative_dialogue_mandate_4_regime_invariant`.

**The California escrow cap is the unique state expansion.** Cal. Civ. Code § 54.1(b)(3)(B) prohibits the landlord from imposing additional security on the disabled tenant and caps any escrow agreement at the reasonable-estimate-of-restoration-cost benchmark. Pinned by `california_modification_escrow_within_cap_compliant`, `california_modification_escrow_at_cap_boundary_compliant`, `california_modification_escrow_exceeds_cap_violation`, and the multi-regime invariant `only_california_caps_escrow_at_reasonable_estimate_4_regime_invariant`.

**Restoration agreement is permissible only where the modification materially impacts the next occupant.** Under HUD's 2008 Joint Statement on Reasonable Modifications, restoration is reasonable for modifications that would not be useful or could be hazardous to future tenants (e.g., narrowed doorways, lowered countertops), but NOT for modifications a typical occupant would prefer (e.g., grab bars in the bathroom, ramps at the entrance). Pinned by `federal_modification_restoration_permissible_only_when_materially_impacts_next` and `federal_modification_restoration_not_permissible_when_no_material_impact`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/reasonable-accommodation-modification`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **federal modification grantable + tenant pays**; **federal modification restoration permissible iff materially impacts next**; **federal accommodation grantable + landlord pays**; **federal accommodation undue burden defeats duty**; **federal accommodation fundamental alteration defeats duty**; **no qualifying disability no duty**; **no nexus no duty**; **CA modification escrow within cap**; **CA modification escrow at cap boundary compliant** (≤); **CA modification escrow exceeds cap violation**; **CA § 54(b) sensory disability note** (UX-text regression); **NYC cooperative dialogue required flag set**; **NYC failure to engage = violation**; **NYC dialogue beyond reasonable time = violation**; **NYC dialogue at 30-day boundary compliant**; **NYC failure independent of substantive grant**; **WA modification grantable + tenant pays**; **WA accommodation grantable + landlord pays**; **only-NYC-imposes-cooperative-dialogue 4-regime invariant**; **only-CA-caps-escrow-at-reasonable-estimate 4-regime invariant**; **modification always at tenant expense across all 4 regimes invariant**; **accommodation always at landlord expense across all 4 regimes invariant**; **nexus universal across all 4 regimes invariant**; **undue burden defeats accommodation across all 4 regimes invariant**; **citation pins subsection per regime + request type** (§ 3604(f)(3)(A) for fed mod, § 3604(f)(3)(B) for fed acc, § 54.1(b)(3) for CA mod, § 8-107(15)(c) + § 8-102 for NYC, RCW 49.60.222(2)(b) for WA); **notes always include accommodation-vs-modification distinction** (UX-text regression across all 8 regime/request combinations).

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_ownership_change` is the **state landlord mid-tenancy ownership-change notice + security-deposit-transfer compliance check** — operational concern for any landlord SELLING or transferring ownership of a tenant-occupied property mid-tenancy. Distinct from `landlord_identification_disclosure` (initial identity disclosure at tenancy start) and `foreclosure_tenant_rights` (foreclosure-driven ownership change with PTFA protections). This module addresses the VOLUNTARY mid-tenancy sale path: successor must notify tenant + transfer security deposit to successor or refund to tenant.

**Six regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Statutory deadline | Joint+several liability | Unique feature |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1962(c) + § 1950.5(g) | **15 days** | **YES** | § 1962(c) BARS nonpayment eviction during noncompliance |
| `Massachusetts` | Mass. G.L. c. 186 § 15B(2)(b) | **45 days** | **YES** | Deposit transfer plus accrued interest |
| `Florida` | Fla. Stat. § 83.49(5) | Upon sale (immediate) | **YES** | Simultaneous tenant notice required |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.060(2) + RCW 59.18.270 | Reasonable time | **YES** | Updated landlord identification required |
| `NewYork` | N.Y. GOL § 7-105 | **5 days** (strictest) | **YES** | Transferor personal liability |
| `Default` | None | None | No | Common-law successor-liability principles |

**New York § 7-105 has the strictest deadline in the matrix.** Five days from transfer for the prior landlord to transfer the security deposit to the new owner + provide written notice to tenant. Failure creates personal liability of the transferor. Pinned by `new_york_within_5_days_compliant`, `new_york_at_5_day_boundary_compliant`, `new_york_past_5_day_deadline_violation`, and the 5-cell boundary invariant `five_day_boundary_truth_table_new_york_invariant`.

**California § 1962(c) is the only regime that BARS nonpayment eviction during successor noncompliance.** If the successor owner has not provided § 1962 identity disclosure within 15 days, the successor (or transferor) CANNOT serve an eviction notice for nonpayment of rent that accrued during the period of noncompliance. This is a uniquely strong tenant remedy. Pinned by `california_nonpayment_eviction_during_noncompliance_barred` and the 6-regime invariant `nonpayment_eviction_barred_only_in_california_invariant` (CA vs 5 other regimes confirmed do not bar).

**Deposit disposition compliance has two alternative paths**: (1) transfer to successor with tenant notice, OR (2) refund to tenant. Either path satisfies the statute; failing both is a violation. Pinned by `california_refund_to_tenant_alternative_path_compliant`, `california_neither_transfer_nor_refund_violation`, `california_transfer_without_tenant_notice_violation`, and the 4-cell truth-table invariant `deposit_disposition_compliant_iff_transfer_or_refund_invariant`.

**Joint and several liability applies in 5 of 6 regimes.** California, Massachusetts, Florida, Washington, and New York all impose joint and several liability of successor and prior landlord for deposit repayment if successor fails to comply. Only the Default fallback regime lacks the statutory joint-and-several attachment. Pinned by `joint_and_several_liability_only_in_5_regulated_regimes_invariant`.

**Massachusetts has the longest transfer window at 45 days, but requires accrued interest.** The MA c. 186 § 15B(2)(b) framework gives transferor more time but adds the interest-transfer obligation. Pinned by `massachusetts_within_45_days_compliant`, `massachusetts_at_45_day_boundary_compliant`, `massachusetts_past_45_day_deadline_violation`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-ownership-change`. Twenty-five tests pin: **CA within 15 days with disclosure compliant + past 15-day deadline no disclosure violation + at 15-day boundary compliant + nonpayment eviction during noncompliance BARRED + refund to tenant alternative path compliant + neither transfer nor refund violation + transfer without tenant notice violation**; **MA within 45 days + at boundary + past deadline violation**; **FL upon sale with transfer compliant + neither transfer nor refund violation**; **WA reasonable time compliant**; **NY within 5 days + at boundary + past 5-day deadline violation**; **Default no specific deadline but deposit disposition required + neither transfer nor refund violation**; **deposit disposition compliant iff transfer or refund 4-cell truth-table invariant**; **statutory deadline per regime invariant** (CA 15 + MA 45 + FL 0 + WA 0 + NY 5 + Default 0); **joint+several liability only in 5 regulated regimes invariant**; **nonpayment eviction barred only in California 6-regime invariant**; **citation pins authority per regime** (§ 1962(c) + § 1950.5(g) for CA; c. 186 § 15B for MA; § 83.49(5) for FL; RCW 59.18.060 for WA; § 7-105 for NY); **sibling-module note across all 6 regimes** (UX-text regression for landlord_identification_disclosure + foreclosure_tenant_rights + VOLUNTARY scope); **5-day boundary truth-table NY invariant** (5 cells: 0/4/5/6/30 days).

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_security_deposit_increase` is the **mandatory landlord-paid prohibition on mid-tenancy security deposit increase module** — when may a landlord demand additional security deposit during the tenancy after initial collection? Distinct from siblings `security_deposit_caps` (initial cap), `damage_deduction_itemization`, `deposit_interest`, `security_deposit_bank_disclosure`, and `deposit_return_windows`. Trader-landlord operational concern when annual rent increase or pet addition triggers desire for higher deposit.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Cap | Bad-faith damages |
|--------|-----------|-----|---------------------|
| California Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5(c) + AB 12 | 1 mo unfurnished / 2 mo furnished (eff. July 1, 2024) | Standard remedies |
| New Jersey N.J.S.A. §§ 46:8-21.2 + 46:8-19 | 1.5 mo | DOUBLE DAMAGES + attorney fees |
| New York N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 7-108(1-a)(a) HSTPA 2019 | 1 mo statewide | HSTPA remedies |
| Default | Lease + state caps | State-specific UDAP |

**California — Cal. Civ. Code § 1950.5(c) + AB 12**. Mid-tenancy security deposit increase generally NOT permitted unless (a) lease modification basis (new pet, additional occupant) AND (b) total deposit remains within § 1950.5(c) statutory cap (one month's rent for unfurnished; two months for furnished, per AB 12 effective July 1, 2024) AND (c) tenant written consent. Pinned by `ca_no_basis_violates`, `ca_cap_exceeded_violates`, `ca_no_consent_violates`, `ca_multiple_violations_simultaneous`.

**New Jersey — N.J.S.A. §§ 46:8-21.2 + 46:8-19**. Mid-tenancy increase prohibited absent lease modification basis OR proportional annual rent increase basis. Total deposit cap is 1.5 months' rent (§ 46:8-19). **Bad-faith mid-tenancy increases recoverable with DOUBLE DAMAGES + attorney fees + court costs** (uniquely punitive among the four regimes). Pinned by `nj_no_basis_violates`, `nj_cap_exceeded_violates`, `nj_double_damages_note_present`, `nj_unique_double_damages_invariant`.

**New York — N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 7-108(1-a)(a) (HSTPA 2019)**. Security deposit capped at ONE MONTH's rent statewide. Mid-tenancy increase permitted only when proportional to rent increase AND total remains within one-month cap.

**Default — lease controls + common-law contract modification + good-faith doctrine**. Most states allow mid-tenancy increase if lease permits OR by mutual written modification. State UDAP statutes may reach abusive mid-tenancy increases.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-security-deposit-increase`. Twenty-three tests pin: **CA clean increase authorized**; **CA no basis violates** (lease modification basis required); **CA cap exceeded violates** (AB 12 cap); **CA no consent violates** (tenant written consent required); **CA citation pins AB 12**; **NJ clean increase authorized**; **NJ no basis violates** (§ 46:8-21.2 + proportional rent or lease modification); **NJ cap exceeded violates** (§ 46:8-19 1.5 months); **NJ double damages note present** (uniquely punitive); **NY clean increase authorized**; **NY cap exceeded violates** (§ 7-108(1-a)(a) one-month HSTPA); **NY HSTPA 2019 note present**; **NY no basis violates**; **default compliant always**; **default authorized with consent or basis**; **default no basis no consent not authorized but compliant**; **no increase attempted always compliant** (4-regime sweep); **four regimes routed correctly**; **CA multiple violations simultaneous** (3 violations); **NJ unique double damages invariant**; **CA + NJ + NY clean no violations**.

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_term_modification` is the **mid-tenancy modification of NON-RENT lease terms** landlord compliance check — when may a landlord unilaterally change pet policy, parking rules, smoking policy, late-fee structure, utility allocation, or other non-rent terms during an existing tenancy? Distinct from `rent_increase_notice_period` (rent-amount changes for CA/WA/OR), `lease_termination_notice` (ending tenancy or non-renewal across NY/CA/OR/WA/NJ + default), and `lease_waiver_enforceability` (enforceability of lease waivers of statutory tenant rights). This module addresses ONLY mid-tenancy modification of non-rent terms during an ongoing tenancy.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Periodic-tenancy unilateral modification | Fixed-term | Mail extension |
|--------|-----------|-------------------------------------------|------------|----------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code § 827(a); CCP §§ 1162, 1013(a) | YES — 30 days written notice for monthly; 7-day floor for shorter periodic (agreement may reduce to ≥ 7 days) | bilateral consent required | +5 days for mail within CA |
| **New York** | RPL § 226-c (HSTPA 2019) + common law | NO — no statute authorizes unilateral non-rent term modification; § 226-c covers rent + non-renewal only | bilateral consent required | n/a |
| **Texas** | Tex. Prop. Code §§ 91.001, 92.006 | NO absent lease clause; lease-granted right enforceable with written notice + prescribed advance period | bilateral consent required absent lease clause | n/a |
| **Default** | Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 149 + common law | NO — bilateral consent required; § 149 statute of frauds applies to material modifications of 1-year-plus leases | bilateral consent required | n/a |

**California § 827(a) is the only US statute permitting unilateral mid-tenancy modification of non-rent terms in periodic tenancies.** Outside CA, even week-to-week and month-to-month landlords must obtain tenant signed consent for non-rent term changes mid-tenancy — or terminate the tenancy per state termination-notice statute and re-let on new terms. Pinned by `ca_monthly_thirty_day_personal_service_valid`, `ny_monthly_unilateral_modification_void`, `tx_unilateral_without_lease_clause_void`, `default_unilateral_modification_void`.

**California's CCP § 1013(a) 5-day mail extension is silent but load-bearing.** A landlord who mails a § 827(a) modification notice 30 days before the effective date has under-noticed by 5 days — the modification is unenforceable. Service by personal delivery under CCP § 1162 carries no extension. Pinned by `ca_monthly_mail_service_requires_extra_five_days`, `ca_monthly_mail_service_thirty_five_days_valid`, `ca_thirty_day_with_mail_one_day_short_invalid`.

**The 7-day floor under § 827(a) cannot be contracted below.** A rental agreement clause specifying 3-day modification notice is void as against the statutory floor; the floor (7 days) is the minimum enforceable notice. Pinned by `ca_agreement_reduces_below_seven_void_falls_to_floor` (3 days specified → 7 days required). Agreement may reduce monthly periodic 30-day notice to as low as 7 days — pinned by `ca_monthly_agreement_reduces_to_ten_days_valid` (10 days valid).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-term-modification`. Twenty-six tests pin: **CA monthly 30-day personal service valid baseline**; **CA monthly short notice violation** (25 < 30); **CA monthly mail service requires extra 5 days** (30 short of 35 mail-extended); **CA monthly mail service 35 days valid**; **CA monthly agreement reduces to 10 days valid**; **CA agreement reduces below 7 days void → falls to 7-day floor**; **CA week-to-week 7 days valid baseline**; **CA fixed-term requires bilateral consent** (§ 827(a) does not reach fixed-term); **CA oral notice invalid for periodic** (§ 827(a) requires writing); **CA fixed-term tenant consent satisfies modification** (bilateral path bypasses § 827(a) analysis); **NY monthly unilateral modification void** (no statute authorizes); **NY fixed-term unilateral modification void**; **NY tenant consent satisfies bilateral requirement**; **NY notes termination alternative** (§ 226-c 30/60/90-day termination path); **TX unilateral without lease clause void**; **TX lease clause with written notice valid** (lease-granted modification right); **TX lease clause oral notice invalid**; **TX lease clause zero days notice invalid**; **default unilateral modification void**; **default consent satisfies modification**; **default notes statute of frauds** (Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 149); **citation CA pins § 827(a) and §§ 1162, 1013(a)** (service statutes); **citation NY pins § 226-c HSTPA 2019**; **citation TX pins §§ 91.001, 92.006**; **CA 7-day floor boundary with mail extension** (W2W + 5 = 12 days; 11 days insufficient); **CA 30-day with mail 1-day-short invalid** (34 < 35 effective).

`traderview-expense::mid_tenancy_temporary_relocation` is the **mid-tenancy temporary relocation rights** module — when landlord requires tenant to TEMPORARILY vacate for substantial repairs / renovation / abatement work with tenant retaining the right to return. Distinct from `tenant_relocation_assistance` (no-fault permanent eviction dollar amounts), `lease_termination_catastrophic_damage` (fire/flood termination), and `demolition_tenant_notice` (permanent demolition).

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Authority | Threshold | Relocation requirement |
|--------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **California** | Cal. Civ. Code §§ 1946.2(d)(2), 1946.2(d)(3), 1942.5(b); SF Rent Ord. § 37.9(a)(11); Long Beach SRTD | Substantial remodel requires vacancy ≥ 30 days | One month rent OR alternative housing + tenant right to return |
| **New Jersey** | N.J.S.A. §§ 2A:18-61.1(g), 2A:18-61.11 | Anti-Eviction Act renovation removal | Alternative housing OR relocation expenses |
| **Washington** | RCW 59.18.085; Seattle SMC 22.210; Bellingham Tenant Protections Ord. | Displacement for repair / maintenance / abatement | Relocation assistance + written notice |
| **Default** | common-law habitability + municipal overlays (SF / Long Beach / Seattle / Bellingham) | lease + common law | varies by municipality |

**Universal cosmetic-improvement carve-out.** Painting, decorating, and minor repairs do NOT qualify as substantial remodel under Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2(d)(2) and trigger no temporary relocation requirement in any regime. Pinned by `cosmetic_improvement_no_relocation_required_universal` (4-regime sweep) and `cosmetic_improvement_path_bypasses_all_other_compliance_checks`.

**CA § 1946.2(d)(2) 30-day vacancy threshold uniquely defines substantial remodel.** Cal. Civ. Code requires the work to require tenant vacancy for AT LEAST 30 DAYS to qualify as substantial remodel just-cause. Work not meeting the 30-day threshold cannot serve as substantial remodel grounds. Pinned by `ca_substantial_remodel_without_30_day_vacancy_violation`, `ca_substantial_remodel_30_day_threshold_invariant` (boundary check), `ca_30_day_threshold_unique_to_california` (4-regime sweep confirms only CA imposes the 30-day threshold).

**CA dual-path compliance: relocation payment OR alternative housing.** § 1946.2(d)(3) — landlord may EITHER pay one-month rent relocation assistance OR provide alternative housing. Either path satisfies. Pinned by `ca_missing_relocation_or_housing_violation` (both missing → violation) and `ca_alternative_housing_satisfies_compliance` (housing alone satisfies).

**CA § 1942.5(b) tenant right to return preserved.** Temporary relocation must preserve tenant's right to return at completion of work. Permanent displacement without proper § 1946.2 just-cause termination is unlawful. Pinned by `ca_no_right_to_return_violation`.

**NJ Anti-Eviction Act § 2A:18-61.1(g) renovation removal + § 2A:18-61.11 alternative housing or relocation expenses.** Same dual-path compliance as CA — alternative housing OR relocation expenses satisfy. Pinned by `nj_full_compliance_passes`, `nj_missing_written_notice_violation`, `nj_missing_both_housing_and_relocation_violation`, `nj_relocation_payment_satisfies_compliance`, `nj_unique_anti_eviction_act_invariant` (4-regime sweep confirms only NJ engages § 2A:18-61.1(g)).

**Local municipal overlay** notes engaged when `local_jurisdiction_overlay_applies` true. SF Rent Ordinance § 37.9(a)(11), Long Beach SRTD Ordinance, Seattle SMC 22.210, and Bellingham Tenant Protections Ordinance all impose additional requirements beyond state law. Pinned by `ca_local_jurisdiction_overlay_note_engaged`, `wa_local_jurisdiction_overlay_note_engaged`, `default_municipal_overlay_note_always_present`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/mid-tenancy-temporary-relocation`. Twenty-seven tests pin: **cosmetic improvement no relocation required universal** (4-regime sweep); **CA substantial remodel full compliance passes**; **CA substantial remodel without 30-day vacancy violation** (§ 1946.2(d)(2)); **CA missing written notice violation**; **CA missing relocation or housing violation** (§ 1946.2(d)(3)); **CA alternative housing satisfies compliance** (dual-path); **CA no right to return violation** (§ 1942.5(b)); **CA local jurisdiction overlay note engaged** (SF / Long Beach); **NJ full compliance passes**; **NJ missing written notice violation** (§ 2A:18-61.1(g)); **NJ missing both housing and relocation violation** (§ 2A:18-61.11); **NJ relocation payment satisfies compliance**; **WA full compliance passes**; **WA missing written notice violation** (RCW 59.18.085); **WA missing relocation assistance violation**; **WA local jurisdiction overlay note engaged** (Seattle / Bellingham); **default compliant with lease + common law**; **default municipal overlay note always present**; **citation CA pins subsections + municipal overlay**; **citation NJ pins § 2A:18-61.1(g) + § 2A:18-61.11**; **citation WA pins RCW 59.18.085 + local overlays**; **CA substantial remodel 30-day threshold invariant** (with/without check); **cosmetic improvement path bypasses all other compliance checks** (universal carve-out); **NJ no return right note engaged**; **CA multiple violations accumulate**; **NJ unique Anti-Eviction Act invariant** (4-regime sweep); **CA 30-day threshold unique to California** (4-regime sweep).

`traderview-expense::landlord_identification_disclosure` is the **state landlord-identification / emergency-contact-information disclosure compliance check** — addresses the upfront requirement that a landlord disclose who they are, where they can be served, and how the tenant reaches an emergency contact. Sibling to `tenant_data_privacy` (data handling) — this module addresses the inverse direction (LANDLORD's identification disclosed TO tenant).

**Four regimes**:

| Regime | Statute | Deadline | Phone required | Emergency contact | Municipal registration | Unlawful detainer consequence |
|--------|---------|----------|----------------|---------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1962 | 15 days | **Yes** (CA-only) | No | No | **JURISDICTIONAL PREREQUISITE** to UD; defective disclosure bars eviction |
| `NewJersey` | N.J.S.A. 46:8-27 through 46:8-37 (Landlord Identity Law) | 30 days | No | **Yes** (NJ-only) | **Yes** (NJ-only) — file with municipal clerk | No |
| `Washington` | RCW 59.18.060 | None | No | No | No | No |
| `Default` | — | None | — | — | — | No |

**California § 1962 is the strictest**: defective disclosure is JURISDICTIONALLY fatal to an unlawful detainer action. A landlord who attempts to evict for nonpayment without § 1962-compliant disclosure cannot maintain the action. Sets up name + telephone + street-address-for-personal-service + entity-for-rent-payments — all four required. 15-day deadline applies to oral agreement creation OR written agreement execution OR successor-owner succession.

**New Jersey's Landlord Identity Law has two-step process**: (a) file Certificate of Registration with municipal clerk within 30 days; (b) supply registration info to each tenant. NJ-unique emergency-contact requirement: registration must identify a representative of owner or managing agent who may be contacted in case of emergency. This is regression-critical — CA + WA do not require an emergency contact.

**Washington's RCW 59.18.060 is the simplest**: name + address of (a) person authorized to manage premises and (b) owner or authorized agent. No phone, no emergency contact, no deadline.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/landlord-identification-disclosure`. Twenty-five tests pin: **CA full compliance**; **CA missing written disclosure bars unlawful detainer** + JURISDICTIONAL citation; **CA missing phone violation** (regression — CA-only phone requirement); **CA missing street address violation**; **CA past 15-day deadline violation** + § 1962 citation; **CA at 15-day boundary compliant** (≤ strict); **CA unlawful-detainer flag only when attempting**; **NJ full compliance**; **NJ not-registered violation** + § 46:8-28 citation; **NJ missing emergency contact violation** + "emergency contact" citation (regression — NJ-only); **NJ past 30-day deadline violation**; **NJ at 30-day boundary compliant**; **WA full compliance**; **WA missing disclosure violation** + RCW 59.18.060 citation; **WA missing name violation**; **WA missing address violation**; **WA no phone or emergency-contact requirement** (regression — WA simpler than CA/NJ); **default no obligation**; **state routing CA/NJ/WA/Default + case-insensitive**; **only-CA-has-unlawful-detainer-jurisdictional-consequence** 3-regime invariant (regression-critical — CA defect bars UD; NJ + WA do not have parallel consequence); **only-NJ-has-emergency-contact-requirement** 3-regime invariant; **only-NJ-requires-municipal-clerk-registration** 2-regime invariant; **only-CA-requires-phone-disclosure** 3-regime invariant; **citations pin authorities** (§ 1962 + 46:8 + RCW 59.18.060).

`traderview-expense::just_cause_eviction` is the **state-by-state just-cause eviction + relocation assistance table** — sibling to `dv_termination`, `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Major statutory shift 2019-2021 — CA AB 1482 (Tenant Protection Act), OR SB 608, WA HB 1236 — changed whether a landlord can terminate at lease end at all, and required relocation assistance equal to one month's rent for no-fault grounds.

**Four regimes**:

| Regime                          | Coverage threshold | Relocation                          | States                                  |
|---------------------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| **Statewide after N months**    | CA 12mo / OR 12mo  | 1 month current rent                | CA Civ. Code § 1946.2 (AB 1482) / OR ORS § 90.427 (SB 608) |
| **Statewide day-one**           | Immediate          | WA: 1 mo rent / DC: 0 / NJ: 0       | WA RCW § 59.18.650 (HB 1236) / DC § 42-3505.01 / NJ Anti-Eviction Act |
| **Partial by building**         | Rent-regulated / 4+ unit only | Statewide does NOT reach all rentals — caller checks rent-regulation status | NY HSTPA / IL local ordinances / ME 14 M.R.S. § 6002 |
| **No statewide regime**         | No coverage        | No relocation                       | 41 other states                         |

**OR small-landlord exemption is load-bearing.** SB 608 carves out landlords with ≤ 4 units from the relocation obligation even though the just-cause requirement still applies — tenant still gets protection, landlord still needs grounds, but no $$$ on no-fault terminations. Pinned by `oregon_small_landlord_4_units_exempt_from_relocation` (4 units exempt, $0 relocation) and `oregon_5_unit_landlord_owes_relocation` (5 units owes full month's rent). CA has no equivalent small-landlord carve-out.

**At-fault vs no-fault distinction.** All grounds satisfy just-cause once it applies, but only no-fault grounds (owner move-in, substantial remodel, withdrawal from market) trigger relocation assistance. At-fault grounds (non-payment, lease violation, nuisance, criminal activity) require no payment regardless of state. Pinned by `california_at_fault_grounds_no_relocation` and `oregon_at_fault_grounds_no_relocation_regardless_of_unit_count` (the OR small-landlord exempt flag stays false on at-fault path because the exemption only matters for no-fault).

**Relocation is current-rent-anchored.** The figure is the displaced tenant's monthly rent as of the notice date — not market rent. AB 1482 makes this explicit; OR SB 608 mirrors. Deliberate pro-tenant choice: the displaced tenant is compensated for what THEY were paying.

**Boundary at 12-month threshold matters for both CA and OR**: 11 months → no-cause termination still available; 12 months exactly → just-cause kicks in. Pinned by `california_under_12_months_no_just_cause_required` and `california_at_12_months_just_cause_required` + the OR mirror in `oregon_at_12_months_boundary`.

**PartialByBuilding flag (`partial_by_building_check_rent_regulation`) tells the caller to look further.** NY HSTPA covers rent-stabilized buildings, IL has Chicago RLTO + Evanston + Mt. Prospect local ordinances, ME applies just-cause to 4+ unit buildings only. The compute returns `just_cause_required: false` for these (because the statewide regime doesn't reach the building by default) but sets the flag so the downstream caller queries the appropriate per-building or per-municipality table.

**NJ Anti-Eviction Act 1974 is the strongest pro-tenant in the US** but has no statutory relocation (regime carries `relocation_months_rent: 0`). The regime still flags `just_cause_required: true` and `no_cause_termination_available: false`. Pinned by `new_jersey_anti_eviction_act_day_one_no_relocation`.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/just-cause-check`. Twenty-five tests pin: 51-row coverage; **CA 12-month boundary** (under/at); at-fault grounds satisfy without relocation; no-fault owner move-in = 1 mo rent; **OR small-landlord 4-units exempt + 5-units owes** (load-bearing carve-out); OR 12mo boundary mirrors CA; WA day-one + 1mo relocation; NJ Anti-Eviction Act day-one no-relocation; DC day-one no-relocation; **NY/ME/IL partial-by-building flag set** (caller-must-check-further regression target); no-statewide states pass through; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations on every row; substantial-remodel and withdrawal-from-market both trigger CA relocation; **OR at-fault grounds never trigger relocation** even at 100 units; zero rent → zero relocation no overflow; **i64::MAX/2 rent no overflow** (saturating_mul regression target).

`traderview-expense::just_cause_termination_notice_content` is the **just-cause termination notice CONTENT requirements table** — what specific content and format must a landlord's WRITTEN termination notice contain to satisfy just-cause statutory requirements? Distinct from sibling `just_cause_eviction` (substantive just-cause grounds + relocation assistance) and `lease_termination_notice` (general termination notice periods). Trader-landlord operational concern in just-cause jurisdictions — even when the substantive ground is valid, a defective notice is VOIDABLE and forces restart. Notice content + format requirements are independent procedural traps from the substantive just-cause analysis.

**California — Cal. Civ. Code § 1946.2(c)**. Written notice MUST state cause from § 1946.2(b)(1) at-fault grounds (14 enumerated categories: nonpayment, breach of material term, nuisance, criminal activity, refusal of entry, etc.) OR § 1946.2(b)(2) no-fault grounds (owner move-in, withdrawal from market, demo, gov order, substantial remodel). For CURABLE at-fault violations (lease breach, unauthorized pets, unlawful subletting, property damage), 3-day cure opportunity required before notice to quit. For no-fault grounds, § 1946.2(d)(3) one-month-rent relocation assistance required. **§ 1946.2(c) — failure to comply renders the written termination notice VOID** (most aggressive consequence of any state).

**Washington — RCW 59.18.650(2)**. Written notice MUST state specific cause AND specific facts and circumstances constituting the cause (stricter than CA written-reason requirement). § 59.18.650(2)(a)-(p) enumerates 16 just-cause categories.

**Oregon — ORS 90.427 (SB 608)**. Written notice MUST state reason. Tenancies < 1 year permit no-cause termination with 30-day notice; tenancies ≥ 1 year require just-cause. Strict-compliance regime — defective notice is VOIDABLE.

**New Jersey — N.J.S.A. 2A:18-61.2**. Anti-Eviction Act notice MUST state specific cause from § 2A:18-61.1(a)-(r) 18 enumerated statutory grounds (nonpayment, disorderly conduct, substantial breach, illegal use, drug-related criminal activity, owner occupancy, conversion, demo, substantial conversion, etc.). Notice content must match the statutory ground EXACTLY — generic "breach" or "misconduct" does not satisfy.

**Default — limited content requirements**. Most non-just-cause states require minimal content (date, parties, termination date); lease + state-specific eviction statute control content + format.

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/just-cause-termination-notice-content`. Thirty-one tests pin: **CA at-fault non-curable full compliance**; **CA missing specific cause voidable** (§ 1946.2(c)); **CA oral notice voidable** (WRITTEN required); **CA at-fault curable without cure opportunity voidable** (3-day cure); **CA at-fault curable with cure opportunity compliant**; **CA no-fault without relocation assistance voidable** (§ 1946.2(d)(3)); **CA no-fault with relocation assistance compliant**; **CA improper service voidable** (Code Civ. Proc. § 1162); **CA VOID consequence note always present**; **WA full compliance**; **WA missing specific facts voidable** (FACTS AND CIRCUMSTANCES); **WA unique facts/circumstances requirement invariant** (4-other-regime sweep — only WA requires); **WA 16 enumerated categories note** (§ 59.18.650(2)(a)-(p)); **OR full compliance**; **OR missing specific cause voidable** (ORS 90.427); **OR under-one-year no-cause path note** (SB 608); **NJ full compliance**; **NJ missing specific cause voidable** (§ 2A:18-61.2 + § 2A:18-61.1(a)-(r)); **NJ exact statutory ground match required note**; **default oral notice violation**; **default written notice compliant**; **citation California pins (b)(1)/(b)(2)/(c)/(d)(3) + Code Civ. Proc. § 1162**; **citation Washington pins § 59.18.650(2) + (a)-(p) + RCW 59.12.040**; **citation Oregon pins § 90.427 + SB 608 + ORS 90.155**; **citation New Jersey pins § 2A:18-61.1(a)-(r) + 2A:18-61.2**; **CA void consequence uniquely explicit invariant**; **CA at-fault grounds note lists 14 categories**; **CA no-fault grounds note lists substantial remodel category**; **NJ Anti-Eviction Act lists 18 grounds note**; **voidable flag engaged on any violation** (regression target); **five regimes routed correctly** (CA + WA + OR + NJ + Default sweep).

`traderview-expense::dv_termination` is the **state-specific domestic violence early lease termination table** — sibling to `lockout_penalties`, `application_fees`, `entry_notice`, `retaliation_windows`, `eviction_notices`, `late_fee_caps`, `deposit_interest`, `deposit_return_windows`, `lease_disclosures`, `habitability_remedies`, `rent_control`, `military_termination`, `security_deposit_caps`, and `contractor_1099`. Federal **VAWA** (34 U.S.C. § 12491) provides a floor for federally-assisted housing (HUD, Section 8, LIHTC, public housing). State laws extend the protection to the private market and provide affirmative early-termination rights with notice + documentation requirements.

Four notice-period bands + three special cases:

| Band                    | States                                                          |
|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------|
| **3 days**              | IL Safe Homes Act (765 ILCS 750/15) — strictest pro-tenant      |
| **7 days**              | ME / NM                                                          |
| **14 days**             | CA § 1946.7 / DC § 42-3505.07 / HI § 521-80 / OR § 90.453 / TN / VT |
| **28-30 days**          | AZ / CT / DE / IA / IN / LA / MD / MI / MT / NC / ND / NE / NH / NJ / NV / NY § 227-c / PA / RI / SD / TX § 92.0161 / UT / VA / WI |
| **End of current month**| WA § 59.18.575 (calendar-anchored)                              |
| **Immediate (0 days)**  | MA § 24 / MN § 504B.206 (immediate with documentation)          |
| **No fixed period**     | CO § 38-12-402 (statute exists, "reasonable" notice required)   |
| **No state statute**    | 15 states (VAWA floor only)                                     |

**Documentation requirements** are nearly uniform across states with statutes: at least ONE of (a) protective order, (b) police report, or (c) qualified-third-party statement (medical provider, mental health professional, victim service provider, clergy). Compute returns `documentation_sufficient: true` if any of the three flags is true.

**Documentation freshness window** is state-specific and varies 30 days (CT, IA, NE) → 60 days (DC, IL, NJ, NV, WI) → 90 days (HI, MA, NC, OR, WA) → 180 days (CA, DE, RI) → no requirement (most 30-day-notice states). Notice date - incident date must be within the window. Pinned by `california_180_day_freshness_window` (179 days inside / 181 days outside).

**Three immediate-termination triggers** can override the notice requirement entirely:
- **Co-tenant violence**: TX § 92.0161(b), AZ, HI, IL, MA, MN, OR allow immediate termination when the abuser is a co-tenant on the lease
- **Landlord/agent violence**: WA § 59.18.575, AZ, DC, HI, IL, MA, MN, OR allow immediate termination when the abuser is the landlord
- Compute returns `immediate_termination_available: true` whenever the state's per-trigger flag is set AND the corresponding input flag is true

States WITHOUT a carve-out don't override: setting `violence_by_co_tenant = true` in CA has no effect because CA § 1946.7 still requires the 14-day notice. Pinned by `co_tenant_violence_does_not_trigger_immediate_in_states_without_carveout` (7-day actual still shortfalls by 7 against the 14-day requirement).

**Washington's EndOfCurrentMonth regime** is calendar-anchored, not day-counted — required_notice_days returns None. Notice on Jan 15 → must terminate ≥ Jan 31 (end of month). The `end_of_month()` helper handles year rollover (Dec → next Jan) and short-month February correctly. Pinned by `washington_short_month_february_handled` (Feb 5 notice → Feb 28 end) and `washington_december_notice_wraps_to_dec_31` (Dec 15 notice → Dec 31).

Mounted at `POST /api/rental/dv-termination-check`. Twenty-four tests pin: 51-row coverage; CA 14-day exact boundary + 13-day shortfall; TX 30-day boundary; **IL 3-day strictest band**; WA end-of-month with year-rollover and short-Feb handling (calendar-anchored math regression targets); WA Jan-31 boundary; TX co-tenant violence waives notice; WA landlord violence waives notice; **CA without co-tenant carve-out doesn't waive** (7-day shortfall pinned); documentation sufficient with only protective order; documentation insufficient when all three false; CA 180-day freshness window inside/outside; no-freshness states accept any-age documentation; 15 no-statute states flagged correctly with VAWA-only note; unknown state errors; case-insensitive lookup; sorted `all_states()`; non-empty citations; notice-before-incident negative freshness window; end-of-month boundary same-day; shortfall zero when compliant; shortfall reported only for Days regime not EndOfCurrentMonth (note text carries the explanation instead).

`traderview-expense::lock_change_between_tenancies` is the **state landlord lock-change / rekey / security-device compliance check for new-tenant turnover** — operational concern for every landlord between tenancies. Tenant safety topic distinct from `lockout_penalties` (which addresses unlawful self-help lockouts during tenancy) and `dv_termination` (which addresses domestic-violence emergency lock change during tenancy). This module addresses the BETWEEN-TENANCIES lock change / security device installation duty.

**Six regimes** diverge along three axes:

| Regime | Authority | Mandatory rekey | Deadline | At landlord expense | Specific security device |
|--------|-----------|------------------|----------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| `Texas` | Tex. Prop. Code § 92.156(a) | **YES** | **7 days** from move-in | **YES** (§ 92.156 requires landlord pays; tenant pays only § 92.156(b) additional rekey) | No |
| `California` | Cal. Civ. Code § 1941.3(a)(1)–(2) | No | n/a | n/a | **Deadbolt** (13/16 inch bolt extension into doorjamb) |
| `Illinois` | 765 ILCS 5/12 + Chicago RLTO | **YES** | **0 days** (same-day) | **YES** | No |
| `Virginia` | Va. Code § 55.1-1221 | No | n/a | n/a | **Locks AND peepholes** |
| `NewYork` | No statewide mandate | No | n/a | n/a | NYC Housing Maintenance Code § 27-2043 may impose peephole standards |
| `Default` | None | No | n/a | n/a | None |

**Illinois is stricter than Texas on timing.** Same-day rekey (0 days) vs Texas 7-day window. Pinned by `illinois_one_day_after_move_in_violation` (1 day post-move-in violates IL but not TX) and the comparative invariant `illinois_stricter_than_texas_timing_invariant`.
